## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Q1. Differentiate between `pip` and `conda` as Python package managers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `pip` | `conda` |
|---|---|---|
| Source repository | **PyPI** (Python Package Index) | **Anaconda Cloud** (channel-based) |
| Languages handled | Python packages only | Python, R, C/C++, CUDA, system libs |
| Dependency solver | Basic, sometimes conflicts | SAT-based, very robust |
| Comes from | Bundled with Python | Bundled with Anaconda / Miniconda |
| Best for | Pure-Python Data Science libs | Complex packages needing C/Fortran (NumPy, SciPy with MKL) |

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**Q2. What is a virtual environment in Python, and why is it used in Data Science projects?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **virtual environment** is an isolated, self-contained directory tree that contains its own Python interpreter, `pip`, and `site-packages` folder, independent of the system Python. It is created using `python -m venv myenv` (or `conda create --name myenv`).

**Why used in Data Science:**
1. **Dependency isolation** — Project A may need `pandas==1.5` while Project B needs `pandas==2.0`; venvs prevent conflict.
2. **Reproducibility** — `pip freeze > requirements.txt` exports the exact versions so any teammate can rebuild the same environment.
3. **Cleanliness** — Avoids polluting the global `site-packages` with experimental or conflicting libraries.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 ESE]`

#### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to install the Anaconda distribution on a Windows machine and set it up for Data Science work. List the verification commands you would run. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat commands, describe how to install the eight core Data Science packages (`numpy`, `pandas`, `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, `scikit-learn`, `scipy`, `jupyter`, `statsmodels`) using `pip` in a single command, and write a short Python program to verify that each of them has been successfully installed by printing their version numbers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Anaconda Installation Steps (7 marks):**

1. Visit `https://www.anaconda.com/download` and download the **Python 3.11 (64-bit) Graphical Installer** for Windows.
2. Double-click the `.exe` installer. Click **Next** → **I Agree**.
3. Choose **"Just Me"** installation (recommended for personal laptops).
4. Set the installation path, e.g., `C:\Users\<you>\anaconda3`. Do NOT install into `C:\Program Files` (permission issues).
5. **Advanced Options screen — check BOTH boxes:**
   - ☑ **Add Anaconda3 to my PATH environment variable** (despite the red warning — it is safe on personal machines).
   - ☑ **Register Anaconda3 as my default Python 3.11**.
6. Click **Install** → wait 5–10 minutes → click **Finish**.
7. Open the **Anaconda Prompt** (Start Menu → "Anaconda Prompt") and execute the verification commands:

   ```bash
   conda --version          # → conda 23.x.x
   python --version         # → Python 3.11.x
   pip --version            # → pip 23.x.x from ...\anaconda3\lib\site-packages\pip
   jupyter --version        # → jupyter core 5.x.x
   ```

8. Update `conda`: `conda update conda -y`
9. Launch Jupyter: `jupyter notebook` — the default browser opens at `http://localhost:8888/tree`.

**(b) — One-shot install + verification script (7 marks):**

```bash
pip install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn scipy jupyter statsmodels
```

This installs the latest stable version of each package from PyPI.

**Verification Python program:**

```python
import importlib

packages = ["numpy", "pandas", "matplotlib", "seaborn",
            "sklearn", "scipy", "jupyter", "statsmodels"]

print(f"{'Package':15s}{'Version':15s}{'Status'}")
print("-" * 40)
for pkg in packages:
    try:
        mod = importlib.import_module(pkg)
        ver = getattr(mod, "__version__", "unknown")
        print(f"{pkg:15s}{ver:15s}OK")
    except ImportError as e:
        print(f"{pkg:15s}{'-':15s}FAILED -> {e}")
```

**Sample output:**
```text
Package        Version        Status
----------------------------------------
numpy          1.24.3         OK
pandas         2.0.1          OK
matplotlib     3.7.1          OK
seaborn        0.12.2         OK
sklearn        1.2.2          OK
scipy          1.10.1         OK
jupyter        5.3.0          OK
statsmodels    0.14.0         OK
```

---

#### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is a Python wheel (`.whl`) file? How does it differ from a source distribution (`.tar.gz`)? Why are wheels preferred for installing Data Science packages such as NumPy? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a virtual environment. Write the exact `bash` / terminal commands to: (i) create a virtual environment named `ktu_ds`, (ii) activate it, (iii) install `numpy` and `pandas` inside it, (iv) export its packages to `requirements.txt`, and (v) deactivate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Wheels vs Source Distributions (7 marks):**

A **wheel** (file extension `.whl`) is a **pre-built binary distribution** format introduced by PEP 427. A wheel filename encodes the Python version, ABI, and platform, e.g., `numpy-1.24.3-cp311-cp311-win_amd64.whl` means NumPy 1.24.3 built for **CPython 3.11** on **64-bit Windows**.

A **source distribution** (`.tar.gz` / `sdist`) contains the **raw source code** and a `setup.py` file. Installing it requires `pip` to invoke a C/Fortran compiler on the user's machine, which fails if MSVC / gfortran / OpenBLAS are missing.

**Why wheels are preferred for Data Science packages:**

- **NumPy, SciPy, pandas** contain C and Fortran extensions for performance. Pre-compiled wheels ship the binaries ready-to-use, so installation completes in **seconds** instead of **minutes**.
- Avoids the dreaded `error: Microsoft Visual C++ 14.0 or greater is required`.
- Wheels are **deterministic** — the same file is installed everywhere, improving reproducibility.

**(b) — Virtual Environment Procedure (7 marks):**

A virtual environment (`venv`) is an **isolated Python workspace** with its own site-packages folder, created via the standard `venv` module. Steps:

```bash
# (i) Create the virtual environment named ktu_ds
python -m venv ktu_ds

# (ii) Activate it (Windows)
ktu_ds\Scripts\activate
# (ii) Activate it (macOS / Linux)
source ktu_ds/bin/activate
# Prompt now shows: (ktu_ds) C:\...>

# (iii) Install packages INSIDE the venv
(ktu_ds) pip install numpy pandas

# (iv) Export installed package versions to a file
(ktu_ds) pip freeze > requirements.txt

# (v) Deactivate when finished
(ktu_ds) deactivate
```

After step (iv), `requirements.txt` will contain lines like:

```text
numpy==1.24.3
pandas==2.0.1
```

A teammate can now replicate the environment in one command: `pip install -r requirements.txt`.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "Add to PATH" checkbox** during Python installation → then `pip` and `python` commands "are not recognized" in CMD. Examiners will deduct marks for not mentioning this in the procedure.
> 2. **Import name vs package name confusion** — the *package* is `scikit-learn` but the *import* is `import sklearn`. Many students write `import scikit-learn` and lose marks.
> 3. **Confusing `pip install` with `conda install`** — the KTU answer key expects you to know that `pip` fetches from **PyPI** while `conda` fetches from **Anaconda Cloud**; writing only one is incomplete.
> 4. **Not mentioning virtual environments** for the 14-mark question — even if not asked, mentioning `venv` shows professional depth and earns bonus credit.
> 5. **Writing `pip install numpy == 1.24.3`** (with spaces around `==`) — this fails. The correct syntax is `pip install numpy==1.24.3` (no spaces).
> 6. **Verifying with `print(numpy.version.version)`** instead of `numpy.__version__` — the latter is the **canonical** attribute every examiner expects.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition:** Installing Python packages = fetching reusable libraries from **PyPI** or **Anaconda Cloud** into the local `site-packages` directory.
- **Two managers:** `pip` (PyPI, Python-only) vs `conda` (Anaconda, cross-language, better at C/Fortran deps).
- **Distribution formats:** `.whl` (pre-built wheel — fast) vs `.tar.gz` (source — slow, may need a compiler).
- **Recommended path for KTU:** Install **Anaconda** → it bundles Python + pip + conda + Jupyter + 250+ packages.
- **Lean path:** Install Python from `python.org` → then `pip install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn scipy jupyter statsmodels`.
- **One-shot install command:** `pip install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn scikit-learn scipy jupyter statsmodels`
- **Verification command:** `python -c "import numpy, pandas; print(numpy.__version__, pandas.__version__)"`
- **Virtual env creation:** `python -m venv myenv` → activate → install → `pip freeze > requirements.txt` → deactivate.
- **Common errors:** `ModuleNotFoundError` (package not installed), `'pip' is not recognized` (PATH issue), `Microsoft Visual C++ 14.0 required` (use wheels, not source).
- **Disk usage:** Full Anaconda ≈ **5 GB**; minimal pip-only Data Science stack ≈ **2–4 GB**.
- **Import names to remember:** `scikit-learn` → `import sklearn`; `Pillow` → `import PIL`; `PyYAML` → `import yaml`.
- **Examination mantra:** *PATH checked → pip installed → packages verified → Jupyter launched → code written.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introducing Jupyter. Data Science Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introducing Jupyter. Data Science Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Jupyter

**Jupyter** is an open-source, web-based **interactive computing environment** that enables the creation and sharing of documents containing live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text. The name *Jupyter* is a reference to the three core programming languages it was originally designed to support: **Ju**lia, **Py**thon, and **R**.

A **Jupyter Notebook** (file extension `.ipynb`) is the central document of this environment. It combines executable code with rich-text elements (Markdown, HTML, LaTeX), images, and video in a single, shareable file.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Jupyter Notebook is a client-server application released in 2015 as a spin-off from the IPython project. It operates on a Read–Evaluate–Print Loop (REPL) model, providing a literate programming environment in which code, output, and documentation coexist in executable cells.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Jupyter Notebook as a **scientist's lab notebook**, but a "smart" digital one. In a traditional paper lab notebook, a chemistry student might write the procedure, paste a printout of the result, and write a conclusion — all separated across pages. In a Jupyter Notebook, you can:
1. Write the *procedure* (code) in a cell.
2. Hit **Shift+Enter** and *immediately* see the *result* (output) right below it.
3. Write the *conclusion* in Markdown right after the result.
The notebook is therefore **executable, reproducible, and self-documenting**.

### 1.2 Introduction to the Data Science Process

**Data Science** is the interdisciplinary field that uses scientific methods, processes, algorithms, and systems to extract knowledge and insights from structured and unstructured data. The **Data Science Process** is the standardized, repeatable workflow used to transform raw data into actionable insights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Mason & Wiggins / OSEMN Framework):** *The Data Science Process is a five-stage lifecycle consisting of **O**btain, **S**crub, **E**xplore, **M**odel, and **i**Nterpret. It is the canonical procedure by which raw, heterogeneous data is progressively refined into a decision-ready model or report.*

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a **chef preparing a gourmet meal**:
- **Obtain** = Go to the market and buy the *ingredients* (raw data).
- **Scrub** = Wash, peel, and chop them (clean the data).
- **Explore** = Taste, smell, and combine small amounts to *understand* flavors (visualize & analyze).
- **Model** = Cook the dish using a *recipe* (apply ML/statistical algorithm).
- **iNterpret** = Plate the dish, present it to the customer, and explain the taste (communicate insights).

You cannot bake the cake (Model) before buying the flour (Obtain) or washing the vegetables (Scrub). The order is **non-negotiable**.

### 1.3 Why Jupyter for Data Science?

| Feature | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **In-browser execution** | No local IDE setup; works on any OS. |
| **Cell-based architecture** | Run one block at a time; ideal for iterative data experiments. |
| **Inline visualizations** | `matplotlib`, `seaborn`, `plotly` plots appear directly below the code. |
| **Markdown + LaTeX** | Mathematical documentation alongside code. |
| **Kernel-backed** | Supports Python, R, Julia, Scala (via Apache Spark). |
| **nbconvert** | Export notebooks to **HTML**, **PDF**, **LaTeX**, or **slideshows**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PBITT404 expects the student to (a) install Jupyter, (b) navigate its dashboard, (c) understand cell types (`Code`, `Markdown`, `Raw`), and (d) describe the five stages of the Data Science Process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of Jupyter

Jupyter follows a classic **client-server architecture** consisting of three logical tiers:

1. **Frontend (Client):** The browser interface served at `http://localhost:8888` by default. It renders the notebook UI using JavaScript widgets.
2. **Server (Jupyter Server / `jupyter notebook`):** A Python process that listens for HTTP/WebSocket requests, manages file I/O on `.ipynb` JSON files, and brokers communication to the kernel.
3. **Kernel:** The actual computational engine. For Python, this is **IPython**. The kernel receives code cells, executes them, and streams output (text, images, errors) back to the notebook.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The browser and the kernel speak different "languages" — the server translates HTTP requests into kernel messages using the **Jupyter Message Protocol** (a ZeroMQ-based asynchronous messaging spec).

### 2.2 Cell Types in Jupyter

Every Jupyter Notebook is a linear sequence of **cells**. There are exactly **three cell types**:

| Cell Type | Purpose | Behavior on Shift+Enter |
|---|---|---|
| **Code** | Executable Python (or other language) | Runs the code, displays output (cell number increments like `In[1]`) |
| **Markdown** | Formatted narrative text, lists, tables, LaTeX | Renders to HTML |
| **Raw** | Unformatted text, intended for `nbconvert` | Pass-through, no rendering |

A cell can be in one of two **modes**:
- **Command Mode** (blue border, `Esc` to enter): For notebook-level actions (add, delete, move cells).
- **Edit Mode** (green border, `Enter` to enter): For typing into a cell.

**Essential Keyboard Shortcuts (frequently asked in KTU viva):**
- `Shift + Enter` — Run cell, select below.
- `Ctrl + Enter` — Run cell, stay on same cell.
- `Alt + Enter` — Run cell, insert new cell below.
- `A` / `B` — Insert cell **A**bove / **B**elow (Command mode).
- `M` / `Y` — Convert to **M**arkdown / **Y** is the default (Code).
- `DD` — Delete a cell (double-tap D).
- `H` — Show keyboard shortcuts.

### 2.3 The Data Science Process — The OSEMN Lifecycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The OSEMN Framework** (popularized by **Hilary Mason** and **William Chen**) is the *de facto* KTU-recommended lifecycle. Each letter is a verb describing a phase of work.

#### Stage 1 — **O**btain (Data Acquisition)
- **Goal:** Collect raw data from a source.
- **Sources:** CSV/JSON files, relational databases (SQL), NoSQL stores, REST APIs (Twitter, GitHub), web scraping (`BeautifulSoup`, `Scrapy`), IoT sensors, log files.
- **Deliverable:** A raw dataset in memory (e.g., a Pandas `DataFrame`).

#### Stage 2 — **S**crub (Data Cleaning / Pre-processing)
- **Goal:** Fix structural and content errors so the data is **consistent, complete, and correct**.
- **Typical operations:** Handle missing values (`.fillna()`, `.dropna()`), remove duplicates, correct data types (`astype()`), parse dates, normalize text (lowercase, strip whitespace), detect outliers.
- **Engineering maxim:** *Garbage In, Garbage Out* (GIGO). A model is only as good as the cleaned data.

#### Stage 3 — **E**xplore (Exploratory Data Analysis — EDA)
- **Goal:** Understand the data through statistics and visualization before modeling.
- **Techniques:** Summary statistics (`.describe()`), distribution plots (histograms, KDE), correlation heatmaps, scatter plots, box plots, pivot tables.
- **Philosophy:** Form **hypotheses** about the data; do not test them with ML yet.

#### Stage 4 — **M**odel (Predictive / Inferential Modeling)
- **Goal:** Build a mathematical or algorithmic model that answers the question.
- **Tasks:** Classification (Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, SVM), Regression (Linear, Random Forest), Clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN), Dimensionality Reduction (PCA).
- **Process:** Train/test split → Fit → Cross-validate → Tune hyperparameters.

#### Stage 5 — **i**Nterpret (Communication & Deployment)
- **Goal:** Translate model results into a **business or scientific decision**.
- **Deliverables:** Reports, dashboards (Tableau, Power BI, Streamlit), REST APIs (Flask, FastAPI), published notebooks.
- **Critical skill:** *Data storytelling* — explaining *why* the result matters to non-technical stakeholders.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Stage | Verb | Input | Key Python Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **O**btain | Acquire | External source | `pandas.read_csv`, `requests` | Raw `DataFrame` |
| **S**crub | Clean | Raw `DataFrame` | `pandas`, `numpy` | Tidy `DataFrame` |
| **E**xplore | Visualize | Tidy `DataFrame` | `matplotlib`, `seaborn` | Plots, summary stats |
| **M**odel | Predict | Tidy `DataFrame` | `scikit-learn` | Fitted model |
| **iN**terpret | Communicate | Fitted model | `nbconvert`, `matplotlib` | Report / dashboard |

| Jupyter Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Kernel** | Computational engine executing code |
| **Notebook** | `.ipynb` JSON document |
| **Cell** | Unit of code/markdown/raw text |
| **Dashboard** | File browser UI at port 8888 |
| **nbconvert** | CLI tool to export notebook formats |
| **REPL** | Read–Eval–Print Loop |
| **Magic commands** | `%` (line) and `%%` (cell) — e.g., `%timeit` |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industry**, this workflow powers:
- **Fraud detection** in banking (Obtain transaction logs → Scrub → Explore → Model with XGBoost → Interpret flagged cases).
- **Predictive maintenance** in manufacturing (Obtain sensor telemetry → Scrub sensor noise → Explore failure patterns → Model RUL → Interpret to schedule service).
- **Recommendation engines** in e-commerce (Obtain clickstream → Scrub → Explore co-occurrence → Model with collaborative filtering → Interpret for UI placement).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION_3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> The Data Science Process is conceptual, not algebraic. Instead of a closed-form derivation, this section provides a **deterministic, stage-by-stage procedural breakdown** — the analytical equivalent used in KTU's "Explain with example" questions.

### 3.1 Worked Walkthrough: Predicting Student Pass/Fail

**Scenario:** A KTU-affiliated college has records of 1000 students (study hours, attendance %, previous score) and their pass/fail outcome. We want a model to **predict** pass/fail for new students.

#### Stage 1 — Obtain

Acquire the historical data into a Pandas `DataFrame`.

$$
D_{\text{raw}} = \{ (x_i, y_i) \mid i = 1, 2, \dots, N \}
$$

where $x_i = (\text{hours}_i, \text{attendance}_i, \text{prev\_score}_i)$ is the feature vector and $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the label ($1$ = pass, $0$ = fail), and $N = 1000$.

**Source:** A CSV file from the college ERP system.

#### Stage 2 — Scrub

A typical cleaning checklist:

1. **Missing values:** Count nulls per column. Replace with median (numerical) or mode (categorical), or drop rows if $\text{nulls} > 5\%$.
2. **Duplicates:** Drop duplicate `student_id` rows.
3. **Type corrections:** Ensure `attendance` is `float`, `pass_fail` is `int`.
4. **Outliers:** Use the IQR rule:

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1
$$

$$
\text{Outlier if } x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} \;\;\text{or}\;\; x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}
$$

where $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ are the **first** and **third quartiles**. Cap outliers at the boundaries (winsorization) or remove them.

5. **Normalization:** Scale features to $[0, 1]$:

$$
x_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}
$$

**Output:** A clean `DataFrame` with **zero nulls**, no duplicates, and consistent dtypes.

#### Stage 3 — Explore

Apply descriptive statistics:

$$
\mu_x = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad\quad \sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \mu_x)^2
$$

Visualize:
- **Histogram** of `study_hours` — is it normally distributed?
- **Boxplot** of `attendance` split by `pass_fail` — do passers attend more?
- **Correlation heatmap** — does `prev_score` strongly correlate with `pass_fail`?

$$
\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\text{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X \, \sigma_Y} = \frac{\mathbb{E}[(X - \mu_X)(Y - \mu_Y)]}{\sigma_X \, \sigma_Y}
$$

A high positive $\rho$ between `prev_score` and `pass_fail` (say $\rho \approx 0.78$) tells us this feature is highly predictive.

#### Stage 4 — Model

Split data: **80% train** (800 samples) / **20% test** (200 samples).

$$
D_{\text{train}} \cup D_{\text{test}} = D_{\text{clean}}, \quad D_{\text{train}} \cap D_{\text{test}} = \emptyset
$$

Fit a **Logistic Regression**:

$$
P(y=1 \mid x) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3
$$

where $w_j$ are the learned weights and $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **sigmoid activation**.

The model minimizes **binary cross-entropy loss**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(w) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \Big]
$$

Evaluate on the test set with accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score.

#### Stage 5 — iNterpret

- **Coefficient interpretation:** A learned weight $w_1 = 0.045$ for `study_hours` means: *for every additional hour of study per day, the log-odds of passing increase by 0.045*, holding other features constant.
- **Deployment:** Wrap the model in a **Flask REST API**. A faculty member enters a new student's stats; the API returns a probability.
- **Reporting:** A Jupyter Notebook with embedded `matplotlib` plots is exported to **HTML/PDF** via `nbconvert` for the HOD.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Jupyter vs Traditional IDE

| Criterion | Jupyter Notebook | Traditional IDE (PyCharm, VS Code) |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution model** | Cell-by-cell (REPL-like) | Whole-script (compile-then-run) |
| **Output locality** | Inline, immediately below cell | Console / terminal pane |
| **Documentation** | First-class (Markdown cells) | Comments only |
| **Best suited for** | EDA, teaching, prototyping | Production software, large codebases |
| **Version control** | Difficult (JSON diffs) | Easy (plain `.py` text) |
| **Collaboration** | JupyterLab / Google Colab | Git + code review |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION_4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jupyter System Architecture (Client–Server–Kernel)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Browser] --> B[Jupyter Server Port 8888]
    B --> C[Jupyter Message Protocol ZeroMQ]
    C --> D[IPython Kernel]
    D --> E[Execute Code Cell]
    E --> F[Stream Output stdout stderr]
    F --> B
    B --> A
    G[Notebook File .ipynb JSON] --> B
    B --> G
    H[nbconvert Utility] --> G
    H --> I[Export HTML PDF LaTeX Slides]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The user types in the browser, the server brokers the message to the IPython kernel, the kernel runs the code, and the result is streamed back. The `.ipynb` file is the persisted state, and `nbconvert` is a one-way export utility.

### 4.2 The OSEMN Data Science Process — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[O Obtain\nRaw Data Sources] --> S[S Scrub\nClean Pre process]
    S --> E[E Explore\nEDA Statistics Plots]
    E --> M[M Model\nML Algorithm Fit]
    M --> N[iN Interpret\nCommunicate Deploy]
    N --> O
    E -.insights feed back.-> S
    M -.evaluation feedback.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The five stages form a **non-linear lifecycle** — the dashed arrows show that insights from Explore can force a re-scrub, and a poorly performing Model can send you back to Explore. This is the **iterative nature** of data science that KTU examiners love to test.

### 4.3 Jupyter Notebook Internal Cell Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Command
    Command --> Edit: Press Enter
    Edit --> Command: Press Esc
    Command --> Code: Press Y
    Command --> Markdown: Press M
    Command --> Raw: Press R
    Edit --> Running: Press Shift plus Enter
    Running --> Output: Execute
    Output --> Command: Select Next Cell
    Output --> Edit: Stay on Cell Ctrl plus Enter
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Where Jupyter Fits in a Data Science Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEFT[Data Sources Layer]
        A1[CSV JSON]
        A2[SQL Database]
        A3[REST API]
        A4[Web Scraping]
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE[Jupyter Notebook Layer]
        B1[Cell 1 Imports]
        B2[Cell 2 Obtain pandas read csv]
        B3[Cell 3 Scrub fillna dropna]
        B4[Cell 4 Explore matplotlib seaborn]
        B5[Cell 5 Model scikit learn]
        B6[Cell 6 Interpret Markdown Report]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[Output Layer]
        C1[nbconvert HTML PDF]
        C2[Flask API]
        C3[Streamlit Dashboard]
        C4[nbviewer GitHub]
    end
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> C1
    B6 --> C2
    B6 --> C3
    B6 --> C4
    B1 --> B2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION_5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define Jupyter Notebook. List any **two** features of Jupyter Notebook.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Jupyter Notebook is an **open-source, web-based interactive computing environment** that allows users to create and share documents containing live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text. The file extension is `.ipynb`.
> **Two features:**
> 1. **Cell-based execution:** Code is executed in independent cells, enabling iterative development and inline output visualization.
> 2. **Multi-language kernels:** Supports Python, R, Julia, and Scala via pluggable kernels.
> 3. *Any one more for safety:* Literate programming — Markdown and LaTeX cells coexist with code.

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2.** List the **five stages** of the Data Science Process (OSEMN). Briefly explain the **"Scrub"** stage.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The five stages are: **O**btain, **S**crub, **E**xplore, **M**odel, **iN**terpret.
> **Scrub stage:** This is the data cleaning and pre-processing stage. Raw data is rarely usable directly. Typical operations include handling **missing values** (imputation or removal), removing **duplicates**, correcting **data types**, detecting and treating **outliers**, and normalizing/standardizing numerical features. The goal is to produce a **consistent, complete, and correct** dataset for downstream analysis. Without scrubbing, the *Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)* principle guarantees poor model performance.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module 1 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> (a) Explain the **architecture of Jupyter Notebook** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between the **client**, **server**, and **kernel** components. (7 marks)
> (b) Describe the **five cell modes** of a Jupyter Notebook and the **essential keyboard shortcuts** used to operate it. (7 marks)
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Architecture (7 marks):**
> Jupyter follows a **three-tier client-server-kernel architecture**.
> 1. **Client (Frontend):** A web browser (Chrome, Firefox) renders the notebook UI served at `http://localhost:8888`. The user types code into cells; the client sends HTTP/WebSocket requests to the server.
> 2. **Server (`jupyter notebook` process):** A Python process that hosts the notebook files, manages file I/O on `.ipynb` JSON documents, and brokers messages between the browser and the kernel using the **Jupyter Message Protocol** (a ZeroMQ-based asynchronous spec).
> 3. **Kernel:** The actual computational engine (default is **IPython** for Python). The kernel receives code cells, executes them in a persistent Python process, and streams the output (stdout, stderr, rich display data like plots) back to the server.
> **Why this design?** It decouples the *interface* from the *computation*, allowing the same notebook to be run on a powerful remote server while being edited from a thin client. *(Draw the block diagram from SECTION 4.1 for full marks.)*
>
> **(b) Cell Modes and Shortcuts (7 marks):**
> Every notebook cell exists in one of **three cell types**:
> | Type | Purpose |
> |---|---|
> | **Code** | Executable Python (or other kernel language). |
> | **Markdown** | Formatted narrative with `**bold**`, `*italic*`, lists, LaTeX `$...$`, tables. |
> | **Raw** | Unformatted text, intended for `nbconvert` templates. |
>
> The cell is also in one of **two modes**:
> - **Command Mode** (blue border, `Esc`): notebook-level actions.
> - **Edit Mode** (green border, `Enter`): typing into the cell.
>
> **Essential shortcuts:**
> - `Shift+Enter` — Run cell, move to next.
> - `Ctrl+Enter` — Run cell, stay in place.
> - `Alt+Enter` — Run cell, insert new cell below.
> - `A` / `B` — Insert cell above / below.
> - `M` / `Y` — Convert to Markdown / Code.
> - `DD` — Delete cell.
> - `H` — Show all shortcuts.

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> (a) Explain the **OSEMN Data Science Process** in detail with a real-world example. (7 marks)
> (b) Compare **Jupyter Notebook** with a **traditional Python IDE** (like PyCharm). In which scenario is each best suited? (7 marks)
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) OSEMN Process (7 marks):**
> The OSEMN framework (popularized by Hilary Mason) defines a five-stage lifecycle:
> 1. **O — Obtain:** Acquire data. Example: pulling sales data from a retailer's MySQL database and a weather API into a Pandas `DataFrame`.
> 2. **S — Scrub:** Clean the data. Example: filling missing prices with the column median, removing duplicate transaction IDs, parsing the `date` column to `datetime64`.
> 3. **E — Explore:** Apply EDA. Example: plotting a histogram of daily sales, computing the Pearson correlation $\rho$ between rainfall and umbrella sales (Section 3.1 formula), generating a heatmap.
> 4. **M — Model:** Fit a predictive algorithm. Example: training a Random Forest Regressor on (weather, day-of-week, holiday) to predict next week's sales.
> 5. **iN — Interpret:** Communicate results. Example: exporting the notebook via `nbconvert` to a PDF report for the store manager, and deploying the model as a Flask API that the inventory system queries nightly.
> **Key point:** the process is **iterative** — if the model accuracy is low, the data scientist may return to the Scrub or Explore stage.
>
> **(b) Jupyter vs Traditional IDE (7 marks):**
> | Feature | Jupyter | PyCharm / VS Code |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Execution** | Cell-by-cell, REPL-style | Whole-script, compile-then-run |
> | **Output** | Inline, immediately below code | Console / terminal pane |
> | **Documentation** | First-class Markdown cells | Comments / docstrings only |
> | **Visualization** | Inline `matplotlib` / `plotly` | Separate pop-up windows |
> | **Version Control** | Hard (`.ipynb` is JSON with outputs) | Easy (plain `.py` text) |
> | **Best for** | EDA, teaching, prototyping, ML experimentation | Production code, large codebases, refactoring |
>
> **Verdict:** A data scientist uses Jupyter for **exploration and storytelling**; a software engineer uses PyCharm for **building and maintaining** production software. KTU expects students to be fluent in *both* environments.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "Kernel" with "Server."** The *server* is the HTTP process; the *kernel* is the *execution engine*. Students routinely lose 2 marks by treating them as the same thing.
> 2. **The Data Science Process is iterative, not linear.** If a KTU question asks "explain the process," explicitly mention that the arrows go *both ways* (feedback loops from Model → Explore and Explore → Scrub).
> 3. **"Scrub" is NOT "Explore."** Scrub is *cleaning*; Explore is *visualizing and statistically summarizing*. Examiners award zero credit for confusing them.
> 4. **File extension matters:** A notebook is **`.ipynb`**, not `.py`, not `.json` (although internally it *is* JSON).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Jupyter Notebook** = web-based, cell-based, interactive computing environment with file extension **`.ipynb`**.
- **Architecture** = **Client (browser) → Server (Jupyter app) → Kernel (IPython)** communicating via the **Jupyter Message Protocol** (ZeroMQ).
- **Cell types** = **Code, Markdown, Raw**. **Modes** = **Command (Esc)** and **Edit (Enter)**.
- **Must-know shortcuts:** `Shift+Enter` (run+next), `A/B` (insert), `M/Y` (convert), `DD` (delete), `H` (help).
- **OSEMN** = **O**btain, **S**crub, **E**xplore, **M**odel, **iN**terpret.
- **Obtain** sources = CSV, SQL, APIs, web scraping, IoT, logs.
- **Scrub** handles = missing values, duplicates, type errors, outliers, normalization.
- **Explore** uses = summary statistics ($\mu$, $\sigma$, $\rho$) and visualizations (histogram, boxplot, heatmap).
- **Model** tasks = classification, regression, clustering; evaluated on a held-out test set.
- **iNterpret** = report writing, dashboards, REST API deployment, `nbconvert` exports.
- The process is **iterative**, with feedback loops from later stages back to earlier ones.
- **Kernel ≠ Server.** The kernel executes code; the server brokers HTTP.
- **Literate programming** is the philosophy: code, output, and narrative coexist.
- Default Jupyter URL: `http://localhost:8888`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Loading and Preprocessing with Pandas: Fast and Easy Data Loading, Data Preprocessing, Data Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Loading and Preprocessing with Pandas: Fast and Easy Data Loading, Data Preprocessing, Data Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pandas** is an open-source, BSD-licensed Python library providing high-performance, easy-to-use data structures and data analysis tools. It is the de-facto standard tool for **structured (tabular) data manipulation** in the Python data science ecosystem and is built on top of the **NumPy** library.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus on *Data Loading and Preprocessing*, the topic is decomposed into three tightly-coupled sub-domains:

| Sub-domain | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Data Loading** | The process of ingesting structured/semi-structured data from external storage (CSV, Excel, SQL, JSON, Web APIs) into in-memory **Pandas objects** (`Series` and `DataFrame`). |
| **Data Preprocessing** | The systematic cleaning, transforming, and re-shaping of raw data into a form that is **consistent, complete, and analysis-ready**. |
| **Data Selection** | The mechanism of retrieving specific rows, columns, or a combination of both (subsets) from a `DataFrame` using **label-based (`.loc`)**, **integer-position-based (`.iloc`)**, and **boolean/conditional indexing**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – Apply):**
> Students must be able to *load, inspect, clean, and subset real-world datasets* using Pandas as a prerequisite for the descriptive and inferential statistics covered in Module 2.

## 1.2 The Two Core Pandas Data Structures

```text
Pandas Data Structures
        │
        ├─── Series  (1-Dimensional, labeled homogeneous array)
        │        └──> Think: A single column of a spreadsheet
        │
        └─── DataFrame (2-Dimensional, labeled, heterogeneous tabular structure)
                 └──> Think: An entire spreadsheet / SQL table
```

A **`Series`** is a one-dimensional labeled array capable of holding any data type (integers, strings, floats, Python objects, etc.). The axis labels are collectively referred to as the **index**.

A **`DataFrame`** is a two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns). It can be thought of as a **dictionary of `Series` objects** where each column is a `Series` sharing the same row index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip:** `DataFrame` is essentially a **dict of Series** sharing a common row index. Every column is a `Series`.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Library Analogy** — Imagine Pandas as a **massive digital library**:
> - **Data Loading** = *Receiving new shipment of books and placing them on shelves* (bringing external data into the system).
> - **Data Preprocessing** = *Cleaning the books* — removing torn pages (missing values), fixing wrong labels (duplicates/typos), and re-categorizing by genre (transformation).
> - **Data Selection** = *The librarian's retrieval system* — fetching exactly the books (rows) and chapters (columns) requested by a patron.

## 1.4 Why Pandas? — The Engineering Motivation

1. **Vectorized Operations** — Eliminates explicit `for` loops; operations apply to entire columns at once.
2. **Heterogeneous Data Handling** — A single `DataFrame` can hold int, float, string, datetime, and category columns.
3. **Missing-Data (`NaN`) First-Class Support** — Built-in handling via `isna()`, `fillna()`, `dropna()`.
4. **Powerful I/O Toolkit** — Native readers/writers for **CSV, Excel, SQL, JSON, Parquet, HDF5, HTML, Clipboard**.
5. **Time-Series Capabilities** — Date-range generation, frequency conversion, moving-window statistics.

## 1.5 Pandas DataFrame Anatomy

```text
              Column Index (axis=1)
              ┌──────┬──────┬──────┐
   Row        │ Name │ Age  │ City │
   Index      ├──────┼──────┼──────┤
  (axis=0)    │ Anu  │  21  │ TVM  │
              │ Raj  │  22  │ EKM  │
              │ Meera│  20  │ KTM  │
              └──────┴──────┴──────┘
        dtypes: object, int64, object
        shape  : (3, 3)
```

- **Axis 0 (rows)** — Vertical direction; the `index`.
- **Axis 1 (columns)** — Horizontal direction; the `columns`.
- **`shape`** — A tuple `(n_rows, n_columns)`.
- **`dtypes`** — The data type of each column.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Data-Engineering Pipeline

### Stage 1 — Data Loading (Ingestion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pandas uses **lazy, format-specific reader functions** of the form `pd.read_<format>()`. These functions parse the file, infer dtypes, and construct a `DataFrame` in RAM.

**Key Reader Functions:**

| Function | Source Format | KTU-Relevant Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| `pd.read_csv()` | Comma-Separated Values | Most common in academic/industry datasets |
| `pd.read_excel()` | `.xls`, `.xlsx` | Government / survey data |
| `pd.read_json()` | JSON | Web API responses |
| `pd.read_sql()` | SQL databases | RDBMS-backed pipelines |
| `pd.read_html()` | HTML `<table>` | Web-scraped tables |
| `pd.read_clipboard()` | System clipboard | Quick prototyping |

### Stage 2 — Data Preprocessing (Cleaning & Transformation)

Preprocessing is decomposed into **five sub-operations**:

1. **Inspection** — `df.head()`, `df.info()`, `df.describe()`, `df.shape`, `df.dtypes`.
2. **Missing-Value Handling** — Detect (`isna()`, `notna()`), then *impute* (`fillna()`) or *drop* (`dropna()`).
3. **Duplicate Handling** — Detect (`duplicated()`) and remove (`drop_duplicates()`).
4. **Type Casting** — `astype(dtype)` to convert columns to proper dtypes.
5. **Feature Transformation** — Apply normalization, encoding (one-hot / label), binning, or string operations (`.str.*`).

### Stage 3 — Data Selection (Retrieval)

Selection is performed using three complementary mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Indexer | Selection Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Label-based** | `.loc[row_labels, col_labels]` | Row/column **names** |
| **Position-based** | `.iloc[row_positions, col_positions]` | Integer **positions** (0-indexed) |
| **Boolean / Conditional** | `df[mask]` or `df.loc[mask, :]` | A **truth-valued Series** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse `.loc` (labels, inclusive of both endpoints) with `.iloc` (positions, exclusive of the end in slicing). Losing 1–2 marks is common in board exams for this.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Parameters of `read_csv()`

| Parameter | Default | Purpose | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| `filepath_or_buffer` | — | File path / URL | Required |
| `sep` / `delimiter` | `,` | Column separator | Use `'\t'` for TSV |
| `header` | `infer` (0) | Row number for column names | `header=None` if no header |
| `names` | `None` | Custom column labels | Used with `header=None` |
| `index_col` | `None` | Column(s) to use as row index | `index_col='id'` |
| `usecols` | `None` | Subset of columns to load | Saves memory |
| `dtype` | `None` | Force column dtypes | Prevents type inference errors |
| `na_values` | `None` | Additional strings to treat as `NaN` | e.g. `['NA','?']` |
| `nrows` | `None` | Number of rows to read | Quick peek at huge files |
| `parse_dates` | `False` | Columns to parse as datetime | `parse_dates=['date']` |
| `encoding` | `utf-8` | File encoding | `'latin-1'` for legacy files |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Use-Cases** aligned with KTU outcomes:
> - **Civil Engineering** — Loading sensor-collected structural-health-monitoring CSV files, handling missing strain-gauge readings.
> - **Mechanical Engineering** — Preprocessing CNC machine logs, removing duplicate timestamps.
> - **Computer Science / AI-ML** — The **mandatory first step** before feeding data to `scikit-learn` estimators, TensorFlow, or PyTorch models.
> - **Biomedical Engineering** — Reading patient records, normalizing lab-test values.

## 2.4 The Three Indexing Paradigms — A Comparison

| Feature | `.loc[]` | `.iloc[]` | Boolean Indexing |
|---|---|---|---|
| Selection by | **Labels** (names) | **Integer positions** | **Condition** |
| Slicing | Inclusive of both ends | Exclusive of end (Python-style) | Not applicable |
| Boolean mask supported? | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ (Native form) |
| Preferred when | Index has meaningful labels | Index is `0,1,2,...` default | Filtering by value |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Walkthroughs & Code-Along

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is **exhaustive by mandate**. Every step is shown. No truncation, no "rest is similar" shortcuts.

## 3.1 Stage-1 Walkthrough — Loading a CSV File

### Step 1: Import the Library

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
```

- `pandas` is conventionally aliased as `pd`.
- `numpy` (aliased `np`) is imported because Pandas is built upon it and shares its data semantics.

### Step 2: Construct / Load the Dataset

We will work with a small inline CSV string for reproducibility (board-exam friendly):

```python
from io import StringIO

csv_data = """id,name,age,city,salary,join_date
1,Anu,21,TVM,35000,2022-01-15
2,Raj,,EKM,42000,2021-07-20
3,Meera,20,KTM,,
4,Raj,22,EKM,42000,2021-07-20
5,Anu,21,TVM,35000,2022-01-15
6,,25,TVM,55000,2020-03-10
"""

df = pd.read_csv(
    StringIO(csv_data),
    parse_dates=['join_date'],
    na_values=['', 'NA', '?']
)
```

**Line-by-line conversion logic:**

1. `StringIO(csv_data)` wraps the multi-line string as an in-memory file object so `read_csv` can consume it without a real disk file.
2. `parse_dates=['join_date']` instructs Pandas to convert that column from `object` (string) to `datetime64[ns]`.
3. `na_values=['', 'NA', '?']` tells Pandas to treat those tokens as `NaN` in addition to its default `NaN` detection.

### Step 3: Inspect the Resulting `DataFrame`

```python
print(df)
print(df.shape)        # (6, 6)
print(df.dtypes)
```

Expected `dtypes`:

```text
id                    int64
name                 object
age                 float64      (note: float due to NaN)
city                 object
salary              float64
join_date    datetime64[ns]
```

**Conversion logic:** Since column `age` contains a missing value, Pandas automatically promotes the entire column to `float64` (because NumPy integers cannot represent `NaN`). This is a critical **type-promotion** behavior.

### Step 4: Use `info()` and `describe()` for Inspection

```python
df.info()
df.describe(include='all')
```

- `info()` prints: index range, column dtypes, **non-null counts** (revealing missing values), and memory usage.
- `describe(include='all')` produces summary statistics for **both numeric and categorical** columns: count, mean, std, min, max, unique, top, freq.

## 3.2 Stage-2 Walkthrough — Preprocessing

### Sub-Operation A: Detecting Missing Values

```python
print(df.isna().sum())
```

Output:

```text
id           0
name         1
age          1
city         0
salary       2
join_date    0
dtype: int64
```

**Conversion logic:** `isna()` returns a Boolean `DataFrame` of the same shape; calling `.sum()` along `axis=0` (default) yields the **count of `True` per column**.

### Sub-Operation B: Handling Missing Values

There are three principal strategies; KTU expects familiarity with all three.

**Strategy 1 — Drop rows with any NaN:**

```python
df_dropped = df.dropna()
```

This is the **most aggressive** approach. With our data, it would drop 3 rows.

**Strategy 2 — Drop only when ALL values are NaN:**

```python
df_dropped2 = df.dropna(how='all')
```

**Strategy 3 — Imputation (filling):**

```python
df['age'].fillna(df['age'].median(), inplace=True)         # Numerical → median
df['salary'].fillna(df['salary'].mean(), inplace=True)     # Numerical → mean
df['name'].fillna('Unknown', inplace=True)                 # Categorical → placeholder
```

**Conversion logic for `inplace=True`:** The operation modifies the `DataFrame` in memory rather than returning a new one. (KTU note: modern Pandas style deprecates `inplace`; the recommended pattern is `df = df.fillna({...})`.)

**Mathematical definition of mean imputation:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

$$
x_{i}^{\text{imputed}} = \begin{cases} x_i & \text{if } x_i \text{ is not NaN} \\ \bar{x} & \text{if } x_i \text{ is NaN} \end{cases}
$$

**Mathematical definition of median imputation:**

$$
\tilde{x} = \begin{cases} x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)} & \text{if } n \text{ is odd} \\ \frac{x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)} + x_{\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)}}{2} & \text{if } n \text{ is even} \end{cases}
$$

$$
x_{i}^{\text{imputed}} = \begin{cases} x_i & \text{if } x_i \text{ is not NaN} \\ \tilde{x} & \text{if } x_i \text{ is NaN} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why median over mean?** The median is **robust to outliers** because it depends only on rank order, not magnitude. For skewed salary distributions, median imputation is preferred.

### Sub-Operation C: Removing Duplicates

```python
duplicates_mask = df.duplicated()
print(duplicates_mask)

df_dedup = df.drop_duplicates()
```

**Conversion logic:** `duplicated()` returns a Boolean `Series` where `True` indicates the row is a duplicate of a *previous* row (by default, all columns are considered). The first occurrence is kept; subsequent ones are flagged.

### Sub-Operation D: Type Casting

```python
df['id'] = df['id'].astype('int32')           # Memory optimization
df['city'] = df['city'].astype('category')    # Categorical dtype
```

**Why `category`?** Storing a low-cardinality string column as `category` reduces memory by storing codes internally while exposing original labels.

### Sub-Operation E: String Transformations

```python
df['name_clean'] = df['name'].str.strip().str.title()
df['city_upper'] = df['city'].str.upper()
```

The `.str` accessor provides **vectorized string methods** that work on the entire column at once.

## 3.3 Stage-3 Walkthrough — Data Selection

### Method 1: Column Selection

```python
# Single column → Series
ages = df['age']

# Multiple columns → DataFrame
subset = df[['name', 'age']]
```

**Conversion logic:** A single-column selection returns a **`Series`**; a list-of-columns selection returns a **`DataFrame`**.

### Method 2: `.loc[]` — Label-Based Selection

```python
# Single row by label
row_0 = df.loc[0]

# Range of rows (labels inclusive)
rows_0_to_2 = df.loc[0:2]

# Specific rows + specific columns
rows_cols = df.loc[0:2, ['name', 'age']]

# Single scalar cell
cell = df.loc[0, 'name']   # 'Anu'
```

**Conversion logic for `df.loc[0:2, ['name', 'age']]`:**

- The first argument `0:2` is a **label-based slice**. Even if the index were not the default integer index, `.loc` would still treat the *labels* `0`, `1`, `2` as endpoints, **inclusive** of both.
- The second argument `['name', 'age']` is a list of **column labels** to retain.

### Method 3: `.iloc[]` — Position-Based Selection

```python
# First three rows, first two columns
subset_iloc = df.iloc[0:3, 0:2]

# Specific positions
cell_iloc = df.iloc[0, 1]    # 'Anu'
```

**Conversion logic for `df.iloc[0:3, 0:2]`:**

- `0:3` selects integer positions `0, 1, 2` — the **stop is exclusive** (standard Python slicing semantics).
- `0:2` selects integer positions `0, 1`.

### Method 4: Boolean / Conditional Indexing

```python
# Filter rows where age > 20
adults = df[df['age'] > 20]

# Multiple conditions (note the parentheses!)
tv_adults = df[(df['city'] == 'TVM') & (df['age'] > 20)]

# Using .loc with a mask + column projection
high_salary = df.loc[df['salary'] > 40000, ['name', 'salary']]
```

**Conversion logic for `df[(df['city'] == 'TVM') & (df['age'] > 20)]`:**

1. `df['city'] == 'TVM'` produces a Boolean `Series` of length 6.
2. `df['age'] > 20` produces another Boolean `Series`.
3. The bitwise `&` performs **element-wise logical AND** (must wrap each condition in parentheses due to Python operator precedence).
4. The combined Boolean `Series` is passed to `df[...]`, which keeps only rows where the mask is `True`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Using `and` / `or` instead of `&` / `|`. The `and`/`or` operators operate on the **entire Series as a single Python object** and raise `ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous`. Always use `&`, `|`, and `~` for element-wise boolean logic on Series.

### Method 5: The `.query()` Method (Modern Style)

```python
result = df.query("age > 20 and city == 'TVM'")
```

**Conversion logic:** `.query()` accepts a string expression and evaluates it against the column names. It is **syntactically cleaner** for complex filters and is the recommended KTU 2024-style approach.

### Method 6: The `.isin()` Method (Membership Test)

```python
cities_of_interest = df[df['city'].isin(['TVM', 'EKM'])]
```

**Conversion logic:** `.isin(list)` returns a Boolean `Series` — `True` for rows whose value is in the supplied list. It is the idiomatic equivalent of multiple `==` conditions joined by `|`.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Methods

| Selection Goal | Recommended Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single column | `df['col']` | Returns a `Series` directly |
| Multiple columns | `df[['c1','c2']]` | Returns a `DataFrame` |
| Row by integer position | `df.iloc[i]` | Explicit and unambiguous |
| Row by index label | `df.loc[label]` | Robust to index changes |
| Filter by condition | `df[mask]` or `df.query()` | Idiomatic and fast |
| Mixed row + col with condition | `df.loc[mask, cols]` | Single most powerful pattern |
| Membership filter | `df[mask.isin(...)]` | Avoids long `\|` chains |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Pandas Preprocessing Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Data Source] --> B[Choose pd.read Format Function]
    B --> C[read CSV / Excel / JSON / SQL]
    C --> D[Raw DataFrame in Memory]

    D --> E[Inspection Stage]
    E --> E1[head / tail]
    E --> E2[info / dtypes]
    E --> E3[describe / shape]

    D --> F[Cleaning Stage]
    F --> F1[isna / notna Detection]
    F --> F2[fillna / dropna Imputation]
    F --> F3[duplicated / drop_duplicates]
    F --> F4[astype Type Casting]

    D --> G[Selection Stage]
    G --> G1["df['col'] Column"]
    G --> G2[".loc[] Label Based"]
    G --> G3[".iloc[] Position Based"]
    G --> G4["Boolean mask & query"]

    F --> H[Clean DataFrame]
    G --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[Analysis Ready Dataset]
    I --> J[Visualization or Modeling]
```

## 4.2 Data Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Select Subset] --> Q1{By Column or Row?}

    Q1 -->|Column| C1{Single or Multiple?}
    C1 -->|Single| C2["df['col']"]
    C1 -->|Multiple| C3["df[['c1','c2']]"]

    Q1 -->|Row| R1{Selection Basis?}
    R1 -->|Label| R2[".loc[label]"]
    R1 -->|Integer Position| R3[".iloc[pos]"]
    R1 -->|Condition| R4[Boolean Mask]
    R1 -->|String Expression| R5[".query()"]

    R2 --> R6{Need Columns Too?}
    R4 --> R6
    R6 -->|Yes| R7[".loc[mask, cols]"]
    R6 -->|No| R8["df[mask]"]
```

## 4.3 Missing-Value Treatment Strategy Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Missing Value Detected via isna] --> P1{Proportion of Missing?}

    P1 -->|Less than 5 percent| P2{Missing Pattern?}
    P1 -->|5 to 40 percent| P3{Data Type?}
    P1 -->|Greater than 40 percent| P4[Consider Dropping Column]

    P2 -->|Random| P5["fillna with mean / median / mode"]
    P2 -->|Systematic| P6[Investigate and Model Imputation]

    P3 -->|Numerical| P7["Median Imputation Robust to Outliers"]
    P3 -->|Categorical| P8["Mode Imputation or New Category"]
```

## 4.4 Memory Layout of a Pandas DataFrame

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Block_DF [DataFrame]
        direction TB
        Idx["Index (axis=0)"]
        CIdx["Columns (axis=1)"]
        subgraph BlockData [Underlying NumPy Block Manager]
            B1["Block 0: int64 data"]
            B2["Block 1: float64 data"]
            B3["Block 2: object data"]
            B4["Block 3: datetime64 data"]
        end
    end
    Idx --> BlockData
    CIdx --> BlockData
```

**Reading the diagram:** A `DataFrame` stores data in **column-homogeneous blocks** managed by an internal `BlockManager`. Each block contains one dtype. This is why operations on a single column are extremely fast (no Python-level iteration needed).
