# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Set A]`

### **Q1.** Define feature engineering. List any **four** techniques used for building new features in a dataset.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge, statistical methods, and data transformations to **create, modify, or encode features** from raw data so as to improve the performance of machine learning models.

**Four Techniques (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **Feature Creation:** Deriving new features from existing ones (e.g., `revenue = price × quantity`).
2. **Feature Transformation:** Rescaling via Min-Max, Z-Score, Log, or Box-Cox.
3. **Feature Encoding:** Converting categorical variables to numerical (One-Hot, Label, Target Encoding).
4. **Binning / Discretization:** Grouping continuous values into discrete intervals (equal-width, equal-frequency).

*(Alternate valid techniques: Interaction features, Polynomial features, Date-time decomposition, Text vectorization.)*

---

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

### **Q2.** Distinguish between **Min-Max Normalization** and **Z-Score Standardization**. State one scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | $x' = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output Range | $[0, 1]$ | Mean = 0, Std = 1 (unbounded) |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | **High** (range is dominated by extremes) | Moderate (uses mean & std) |
| Preserves Distribution Shape | Yes | Yes |

**Preferred Scenarios (1 Mark):**
- **Min-Max:** Neural network image inputs (pixel values must lie in $[0,1]$); KNN with Euclidean distance.
- **Z-Score:** Logistic regression, SVM, PCA — algorithms that assume zero-centered, unit-variance inputs.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> `[KTU End Semester Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]`

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **feature scaling techniques** used in data preprocessing. For each technique, provide the formula, the output range, and a suitable use case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following dataset of monthly incomes (in ₹ thousands) of 5 employees:
$$X = \{15,\ 22,\ 30,\ 45,\ 200\}$$
Apply **(i)** Min-Max Normalization and **(ii)** Z-Score Standardization. Compare the outputs and explain which technique is more affected by the outlier value `200`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**1. Min-Max Normalization (1.5 Marks):**

$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

- **Output Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Use Case:** Neural networks (image pixel scaling), KNN, algorithms requiring bounded inputs.
- **Limitation:** Highly sensitive to outliers.

**2. Z-Score Standardization (1.5 Marks):**

$$x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

- **Output Range:** No fixed range, but mean = 0 and standard deviation = 1.
- **Use Case:** Logistic Regression, SVM, Linear Regression, PCA.
- **Advantage:** Less affected by bounded ranges; widely used.

**3. Robust Scaling (1 Mark):**

$$x' = \frac{x - \text{median}(x)}{\text{IQR}(x)}$$

- **Use Case:** Datasets containing significant outliers (e.g., income, real estate prices).
- **Why preferred:** Uses median and IQR, which are robust statistical measures.

**4. Log Transformation (1 Mark):**

$$x' = \log(x + 1)$$

- **Use Case:** Right-skewed data such as income, population, web traffic counts.
- **Effect:** Reduces skewness, compresses large values.

**5. Power Transformation — Box-Cox / Yeo-Johnson (1 Mark):**

- **Use Case:** Making data approximately Gaussian.
- **Parameter $\lambda$** is found by Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
- **Yeo-Johnson** is an extension that handles zero or negative values.

**6. Unit Vector Scaling / L2 Normalization (1 Mark):**

$$x' = \frac{x}{\|x\|_2} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{\sum_i x_i^2}}$$

- **Use Case:** Text classification (TF-IDF vectors), cosine similarity-based models.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $X = \{15, 22, 30, 45, 200\}$, $N = 5$.

### Part (i) Min-Max Normalization (3 Marks)

$$x_{\min} = 15, \quad x_{\max} = 200, \quad \text{Range} = 200 - 15 = 185$$

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1' &= \frac{15 - 15}{185} = 0.0000 \\
x_2' &= \frac{22 - 15}{185} = \frac{7}{185} \approx 0.0378 \\
x_3' &= \frac{30 - 15}{185} = \frac{15}{185} \approx 0.0811 \\
x_4' &= \frac{45 - 15}{185} = \frac{30}{185} \approx 0.1622 \\
x_5' &= \frac{200 - 15}{185} = \frac{185}{185} = 1.0000 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (ii) Z-Score Standardization (3 Marks)

**Step 1: Mean**

$$\mu = \frac{15 + 22 + 30 + 45 + 200}{5} = \frac{312}{5} = 62.4$$

**Step 2: Variance**

$$\begin{aligned}
(15-62.4)^2 &= 2246.76 \\
(22-62.4)^2 &= 1632.16 \\
(30-62.4)^2 &= 1049.76 \\
(45-62.4)^2 &= 302.76 \\
(200-62.4)^2 &= 18940.16 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 2246.76 + 1632.16 + 1049.76 + 302.76 + 18940.16 = 24171.60$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{24171.60}{5} = 4834.32, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{4834.32} \approx 69.53$$

**Step 3: Standardize**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1' &= \frac{15 - 62.4}{69.53} = \frac{-47.4}{69.53} \approx -0.682 \\
x_2' &= \frac{22 - 62.4}{69.53} = \frac{-40.4}{69.53} \approx -0.581 \\
x_3' &= \frac{30 - 62.4}{69.53} = \frac{-32.4}{69.53} \approx -0.466 \\
x_4' &= \frac{45 - 62.4}{69.53} = \frac{-17.4}{69.53} \approx -0.250 \\
x_5' &= \frac{200 - 62.4}{69.53} = \frac{137.6}{69.53} \approx 1.979 \\
\end{aligned}$$

### Comparison (1 Mark)

| | Min-Max | Z-Score |
|---|---|---|
| Outlier 200 mapped to | $1.000$ | $1.979$ |
| Spread of non-outlier values | $0.000$ to $0.162$ (compressed) | $-0.682$ to $-0.250$ (more spread) |

**Conclusion:** Min-Max Normalization is **more affected** by the outlier `200`. The non-outlier values $\{15, 22, 30, 45\}$ are squashed into the narrow interval $[0, 0.162]$, losing discriminative power. Z-Score, while also affected (the outlier shifts the mean), still preserves more relative spread among non-outliers. For this dataset, **Robust Scaling** would be the best choice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a)** Explain **One-Hot Encoding** and **Label Encoding** with suitable examples. Discuss the **dummy variable trap** and how to avoid it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following dataset with categorical feature `Education` and binary target `Hired` (1 = Hired, 0 = Not Hired):

| ID | Education | Hired |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Graduate | 1 |
| 2 | PostGrad | 0 |
| 3 | PhD | 1 |
| 4 | Graduate | 0 |
| 5 | PostGrad | 1 |
| 6 | PhD | 1 |

Apply **Target Encoding** with smoothing factor $m = 2$ and global mean $\mu_g = 0.667$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**1. Label Encoding (2 Marks):**

Label encoding assigns a unique integer to each category. Example for `Size = {Small, Medium, Large}`:

$$\text{Small} \rightarrow 0,\ \text{Medium} \rightarrow 1,\ \text{Large} \rightarrow 2$$

- **Pros:** Memory efficient (one column).
- **Cons:** Implies artificial ordering; misleading for nominal data in distance-based models.

**2. One-Hot Encoding (2 Marks):**

Creates $k$ binary columns for $k$ categories. Example for `Color = {Red, Green, Blue}`:

| Color | is\_Red | is\_Green | is\_Blue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Red | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| Green | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| Blue | 0 | 0 | 1 |

- **Pros:** No false ordering assumption; works with all model types.
- **Cons:** High cardinality causes dimensionality explosion.

**3. Dummy Variable Trap (2 Marks):**

When all $k$ one-hot columns are used along with an intercept, the columns become **perfectly collinear**:

$$\text{is\_Red} + \text{is\_Green} + \text{is\_Blue} = 1$$

This makes the design matrix $X$ **singular** ($X^TX$ is non-invertible), and OLS regression coefficients cannot be uniquely estimated.

**Avoidance:** Drop one dummy column. Use only $k-1$ dummies. The dropped category becomes the **reference category**, and its effect is captured by the intercept.

**4. Choosing Between Them (1 Mark):**

- Use **Label Encoding** for ordinal data (Low < Medium < High) or tree-based models.
- Use **One-Hot Encoding** for nominal data in linear models, SVM, or neural networks.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Given:**
- Global mean: $\mu_g = 0.667$
- Smoothing factor: $m = 2$
- Smoothing formula:

$$\text{Enc}_{\text{smoothed}}(c) = \frac{n_c \cdot \mu_c + m \cdot \mu_g}{n_c + m}$$

**Step 1: Compute $n_c$ and $\mu_c$ for each category**

**Graduate:** IDs 1, 4 → Hired values: $\{1, 0\}$

$$n_{\text{Grad}} = 2, \quad \mu_{\text{Grad}} = \frac{1+0}{2} = 0.500$$

**PostGrad:** IDs 2, 5 → Hired values: $\{0, 1\}$

$$n_{\text{PostGrad}} = 2, \quad \mu_{\text{PostGrad}} = \frac{0+1}{2} = 0.500$$

**PhD:** IDs 3, 6 → Hired values: $\{1, 1\}$

$$n_{\text{PhD}} = 2, \quad \mu_{\text{PhD}} = \frac{1+1}{2} = 1.000$$

**Step 2: Apply Smoothed Target Encoding**

**For Graduate:**

$$\text{Enc}(\text{Grad}) = \frac{(2)(0.500) + (2)(0.667)}{2 + 2} = \frac{1.000 + 1.334}{4} = \frac{2.334}{4} = 0.5835$$

**For PostGrad:**

$$\text{Enc}(\text{PostGrad}) = \frac{(2)(0.500) + (2)(0.667)}{2 + 2} = \frac{1.000 + 1.334}{4} = \frac{2.334}{4} = 0.5835$$

**For PhD:**

$$\text{Enc}(\text{PhD}) = \frac{(2)(1.000) + (2)(0.667)}{2 + 2} = \frac{2.000 + 1.334}{4} = \frac{3.334}{4} = 0.8335$$

**Step 3: Final Encoded Dataset**

| ID | Education | Hired | Target\_Encoded |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Graduate | 1 | 0.5835 |
| 2 | PostGrad | 0 | 0.5835 |
| 3 | PhD | 1 | 0.8335 |
| 4 | Graduate | 0 | 0.5835 |
| 5 | PostGrad | 1 | 0.5835 |
| 6 | PhD | 1 | 0.8335 |

**Interpretation (1 Mark):**

PhD candidates receive the highest encoded value (0.8335), indicating the highest historical hiring rate. Graduate and PostGrad candidates are tied at 0.5835 (smoothed toward the global mean). The smoothing prevents extreme overfitting to small-sample categories.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Deductions):**
>
> 1. **Dummy Variable Trap:** Students often write all $k$ dummies and lose 2 marks. Always state "$k-1$ dummies are sufficient."
> 2. **Outlier Ignorance:** Applying Min-Max without acknowledging outliers loses marks. Always pair with **RobustScaler** or outlier capping.
> 3. **Mixing Train & Test for Encoding Statistics:** Computing $\mu$, $\sigma$, or target means on the **full dataset** (including test) causes **data leakage**. Always `fit` on train, `transform` on test.
> 4. **Confusing Label vs Ordinal Encoding:** Label Encoding is for *alphabetical* arbitrary order; **Ordinal Encoding** preserves the true semantic order.
> 5. **Skipping the $+1$ in `log(x+1)`:** Causes $\log(0) = -\infty$, a common error in log transformations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Feature Engineering** = Creating + Transforming + Encoding new variables to boost model performance.
- [x] **Feature Creation** sources: domain knowledge, date-time decomposition, mathematical ratios, differences, products.
- [x] **Min-Max Scaling:** $x' = (x - x_{\min})/(x_{\max}-x_{\min})$ → range $[0,1]$; **sensitive to outliers**.
- [x] **Z-Score Standardization:** $x' = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ → mean $0$, std $1$; preferred for linear/PCA models.
- [x] **Robust Scaling:** uses median and IQR; **best for outlier-heavy** data.
- [x] **Log Transform:** $x' = \log(x+1)$ → reduces right skew; requires non-negative data.
- [x] **Box-Cox:** requires $x>0$, finds $\lambda$ via MLE to **Gaussianize** data.
- [x] **Yeo-Johnson:** extension of Box-Cox that handles zero and negative values.
- [x] **Label Encoding:** integer mapping; **only for ordinal data or tree-based models**.
- [x] **One-Hot Encoding:** $k$ binary columns; **dummy trap** → use $k-1$ dummies with intercept.
- [x] **Target Encoding:** replaces category with target mean; **always use K-Fold + smoothing** to prevent leakage.
- [x] **Frequency Encoding:** replace category with its count or relative frequency.
- [x] **Binning:** equal-width, equal-frequency (quantile), or K-Means based.
- [x] **Interaction Features:** $x_1 \times x_2$ or $x_1 / x_2$ capture synergistic effects.
- [x] **Polynomial Features:** of degree $d$ on $n$ original features yield $\binom{n+d}{d}$ new features — **explosive growth**.
- [x] **Date-Time Features:** year, month, day, dayofweek, hour, is_weekend, quarter, week_of_year.
- [x] **Algorithm-Scaling Rule:** Tree-based = no scaling needed; Distance/Gradient-based = scaling mandatory.
- [x] **Golden Rule:** Always `fit_transform()` on **train**, then `transform()` on **test** to prevent data leakage.
- [x] **Validation Strategy:** Re-evaluate engineered features via **cross-validation** and **feature importance** (e.g., SHAP, permutation importance) before finalizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionality Reduction: The Detection and Treatment of Outliers, Validation Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensionality Reduction: The Detection and Treatment of Outliers, Validation Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dimensionality Reduction — Formal Definition

**Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of transforming high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional representation while preserving as much of the original data's meaningful structure, variance, and relationships as possible. In the KTU 2024 Data Science syllabus, it is a critical preprocessing step that mitigates the **"Curse of Dimensionality"** — a phenomenon where data becomes exponentially sparse as dimensions increase, making statistical analysis and machine learning models less reliable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Dimensionality reduction refers to the mapping of the original high-dimensional feature space into a lower-dimensional feature space. The two principal paradigms are **Feature Selection** (selecting a subset of original features) and **Feature Extraction** (projecting data onto a new feature subspace).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suitcase" Intuition

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a two-week trip, but the airline restricts you to **5 kg**. You cannot simply throw away random items. Instead, you must:
1. Identify what is *essential* (toothbrush, passport, clothes) — this is **Feature Selection**.
2. Identify what is *redundant* (e.g., 5 different pairs of shoes when 2 will do) and replace them with versatile alternatives — this is **Feature Extraction**.

Dimensionality reduction operates in the exact same manner on data: it discards redundant, noisy, or low-information features and extracts the most informative representations, allowing the model to learn faster, generalize better, and avoid overfitting.

## 1.3 Outliers — Formal Definition

An **Outlier** (also called an *anomaly* or *discordant observation*) is a data point that deviates so significantly from other observations in the dataset that it arouses suspicion that it was generated by a different mechanism (per Hawkins' classical 1980 definition). In the KTU 2024 framework, outliers are characterized using statistical distance measures, density estimates, or model residuals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Statistical Definition:**
> An observation $x_i$ is considered an outlier if:
> $$P(x_i \mid \text{model}) < \epsilon$$
> where $\epsilon$ is a small probability threshold and the model represents the underlying data distribution.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Marks" Intuition

Consider the marks of 100 students in a class: most score between 40 and 90. Suddenly, one student scores **3** and another scores **99.5**. These two are *outliers* — they lie far from the bulk of the distribution. Whether they represent:
- **Data entry errors** (typo: 93 typed as 3)
- **Genuine exceptional cases** (genius student, or absent student)
- **Measurement errors** (sensor malfunction)

...is what *Detection* answers, and what to do with them is what *Treatment* addresses.

## 1.5 Validation Metrics — Formal Definition

**Validation Metrics** are quantitative, mathematical functions used to evaluate the performance, accuracy, and generalizability of data science models. Under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, validation metrics are essential for assessing whether Course Outcomes (COs) related to model interpretation are achieved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction in Validation:**
> - **Evaluation Metrics** → Measure *how well* a model performs (RMSE, Accuracy, F1-Score).
> - **Validation Strategies** → Measure *how robust* a model is on unseen data (K-Fold Cross-Validation, Hold-Out, Bootstrapping).

### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Grading" Intuition

A teacher giving an exam needs two types of assessments:
1. **Grading Rubrics** (Metrics): How many marks did each student get? Did the exam actually test what it was supposed to? (RMSE, R², Silhouette Score).
2. **Quality of the Exam** (Validation): Is the exam fair? Would the students perform similarly on a re-test? Does it generalize? (Cross-Validation, Stratified Sampling).

Just as a teacher cannot trust a single exam to certify a student, a data scientist cannot trust a single train-test split to certify a model.

## 1.7 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Data Science

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Significance:**
> - **In IoT Systems (KTU Kerala Smart City projects):** Sensor data often contains thousands of features; dimensionality reduction enables real-time processing on edge devices.
> - **In Healthcare Analytics:** Outlier detection identifies tumor anomalies in MRI scans.
> - **In Financial Fraud Detection:** Outliers in transaction patterns reveal fraudulent activity.
> - **In Recommendation Systems:** Validation metrics like RMSE directly quantify recommendation quality.

The three sub-topics — *Dimensionality Reduction*, *Outlier Handling*, and *Validation Metrics* — form a **trinity** in the data science pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the ability to:
1. Reduce dimensions mathematically (PCA derivations).
2. Detect and treat outliers using statistical reasoning.
3. Apply the correct validation metric based on problem type.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dimensionality Reduction — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Two Paradigms of Dimensionality Reduction

**(A) Feature Selection** — Operates on the original feature set $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_p\}$ and chooses a subset $X' \subset X$ such that the intrinsic information is preserved.

Three sub-categories:
- **Filter Methods:** Use statistical scores (Chi-square, ANOVA F-value, Mutual Information) — independent of any model.
- **Wrapper Methods:** Use a predictive model to score feature subsets (Recursive Feature Elimination, Forward/Backward Selection).
- **Embedded Methods:** Feature selection is built into the model training (Lasso L1 regularization, Decision Tree importance).

**(B) Feature Extraction** — Transforms the original space $\mathbb{R}^p$ into a new space $\mathbb{R}^k$ where $k \ll p$, by creating new variables that are *combinations* of original variables.

Major methods:
- **Principal Component Analysis (PCA):** Linear, orthogonal, variance-maximizing.
- **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA):** Supervised, class-separation maximizing.
- **t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE):** Non-linear, neighborhood-preserving (mainly for visualization).
- **Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP):** Non-linear, topology-preserving.

### 2.1.2 The Curse of Dimensionality

As the number of dimensions $d$ increases, the volume of the space grows exponentially, causing data to become sparse. Consequences:
- Distances between points become **inconsequential** (all pairwise distances converge).
- The number of samples required to maintain density grows exponentially: $N \propto c^d$.
- Models overfit due to **Hughes Phenomenon** (more features → worse generalization).

## 2.2 Outliers — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Types of Outliers

**(A) Global (Point) Outliers:** A single data point far from the entire dataset.
- *Example:* Temperature reading of 200°C when others are 20–30°C.

**(B) Contextual (Conditional) Outliers:** A point anomalous only in a specific context (e.g., season, location, time).
- *Example:* 15°C in *Kerala* in December is normal, but 15°C in *Rajasthan* in June is contextual.

**(C) Collective Outliers:** A group of points that is anomalous *as a collection*, even if individual points are not.
- *Example:* A sudden dip in ECG readings indicating a heart attack — each individual reading is plausible, but the *pattern* is abnormal.

### 2.2.2 Outlier Detection — Methodological Taxonomy

**(A) Statistical Methods (Parametric):**
- **Z-Score (Standard Score):** Assumes data is normally distributed. Threshold: $|Z| > 3$ is the **standard 3-sigma rule**.
- **Modified Z-Score (Iglewicz–Hoaglin):** Uses Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) — robust to outliers themselves.
- **Grubbs' Test:** Statistical hypothesis test for a single outlier in a univariate dataset.
- **Tukey's Fences (IQR Method):** Boxplot-based; works for skewed data.

**(B) Distance-Based Methods (Non-Parametric):**
- **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) Distance:** A point is an outlier if the distance to its $k$-th nearest neighbor exceeds a threshold.
- **Mahalanobis Distance:** Accounts for feature correlations; a point is an outlier if its Mahalanobis distance is large.

**(C) Density-Based Methods:**
- **Local Outlier Factor (LOF):** Compares local density of a point to that of its neighbors.
- **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise):** Points in low-density regions are labeled as noise/outliers.

**(D) Model-Based Methods:**
- **Isolation Forest:** Anomalies are "easy to isolate" — fewer random splits are needed.
- **One-Class SVM:** Learns a decision boundary around normal data.

### 2.2.3 Treatment of Outliers

| Strategy | When to Use | Effect on Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Deletion (Listwise Removal)** | Few outliers, MCAR (Missing Completely At Random) | Reduces sample size; may introduce bias |
| **Capping (Winsorization)** | Many outliers, need to preserve sample size | Limits extreme values to a percentile (e.g., 5th–95th) |
| **Imputation** | Outliers are believed to be errors | Replaces with mean/median/mode/KNN-imputed value |
| **Transformation** | Skewed data with outliers | Log, square root, Box-Cox reduce outlier influence |
| **Binning** | Continuous variable with outliers | Converts to categorical bins, neutralizing extremes |
| **Separate Modeling** | Outliers are a meaningful subgroup | Trains two models: one on outliers, one on normal data |

## 2.3 Validation Metrics — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Regression Metrics

For predictions $\hat{y}_i$ and actuals $y_i$ over $n$ samples:

- **Mean Absolute Error (MAE):** Average of absolute residuals. Robust to outliers.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** Penalizes large errors quadratically.
- **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):** Same units as target variable; sensitive to outliers.
- **R² (Coefficient of Determination):** Proportion of variance explained. $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ for sensible models; can be negative for very poor models.
- **Adjusted R²:** Penalizes adding useless features.

### 2.3.2 Classification Metrics

Based on the **Confusion Matrix**: True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), False Negatives (FN).

- **Accuracy:** $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$
- **Precision:** $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ (Quality of positive predictions)
- **Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate):** $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ (Coverage of actual positives)
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **ROC-AUC:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve; threshold-independent.

### 2.3.3 Clustering Metrics (Unsupervised Validation)

- **Silhouette Score:** Measures how similar a point is to its own cluster vs. other clusters. Range: $[-1, 1]$.
- **Davies–Bouldin Index (DBI):** Lower is better. Measures average similarity between clusters.
- **Calinski–Harabasz Index (Variance Ratio Criterion):** Higher is better. Ratio of between-cluster to within-cluster dispersion.

### 2.3.4 Cross-Validation Strategies

- **Hold-Out Validation:** Simple split (e.g., 80/20).
- **K-Fold Cross-Validation:** Data split into K folds; each fold is used once as test set.
- **Stratified K-Fold:** Preserves class distribution in each fold (essential for imbalanced data).
- **Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV):** K = n; computationally expensive, low bias, high variance.
- **Time Series Split:** Respects temporal order; no future-to-past leakage.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM-CRITICAL FORMULAS — Memorize and Practice for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Z-Score | $Z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Threshold: $\vert Z_i \vert > 3$ |
| 2 | Modified Z-Score | $M_i = \dfrac{0.6745(x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}$ | Threshold: $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$ |
| 3 | Median Absolute Deviation | $\text{MAD} = \text{median}(\vert x_i - \tilde{x} \vert)$ | Robust to outliers |
| 4 | IQR Method (Tukey) | Bounds: $[\tilde{x} - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR},\, \tilde{x} + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}]$ | IQR $= Q_3 - Q_1$ |
| 5 | Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M(x) = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^T S^{-1} (x-\mu)}$ | $S$ = covariance matrix |
| 6 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Units: same as $y$ |
| 7 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Units: squared |
| 8 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$ |
| 9 | R² | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i-\bar{y})^2}$ | Best: $R^2 = 1$ |
| 10 | Adjusted R² | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - (1-R^2)\dfrac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | $p$ = number of predictors |
| 11 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | High = few false alarms |
| 12 | Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | High = catches most positives |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| 14 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \dfrac{b(i)-a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\,b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance; $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance |
| 15 | Davies–Bouldin Index | $\text{DBI} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\max_{j \neq i}\left(\dfrac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}}\right)$ | Lower is better |
| 16 | PCA Variance Ratio | $\dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p}\lambda_j}$ | Eigenvalue $\lambda_i$ importance |
| 17 | PCA Reconstruction | $\mathbf{X}_{\text{recon}} = \mathbf{X}_{\text{centered}} \mathbf{W}_k \mathbf{W}_k^T$ | $\mathbf{W}_k$ = top-$k$ eigenvectors |
| 18 | Explained Variance (PCA) | $\text{EV}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{p}\lambda_i}$ | Choose $k$ for threshold (e.g., 0.95) |
| 19 | LOF | $\text{LOF}_k(A) = \dfrac{1}{\vert N_k(A) \vert}\sum_{B \in N_k(A)} \dfrac{\text{lrd}(B)}{\text{lrd}(A)}$ | $\text{lrd}$ = local reachability density |
| 20 | K-Fold CV Score | $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} M_i$ | $M_i$ = metric on fold $i$ |

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Engineering Students Must Master These Formulas:**
> - In **predictive maintenance** of industrial machinery, MAE and RMSE quantify prediction error tolerance.
> - In **medical diagnosis AI**, Recall is paramount — missing a cancer diagnosis (FN) is far more costly than a false alarm (FP).
> - In **clustering of customer segments**, Silhouette score guides the choice of optimal $k$ in K-Means.
> - In **PCA-based image compression**, the cumulative explained variance determines the trade-off between compression and quality.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Z-Score and IQR Outlier Detection

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern - Frequently Asked Type]**

### Problem Setup
Consider the dataset: $\{12, 15, 14, 10, 13, 100, 16, 11, 14, 15, 9, 12\}$. Identify outliers using:
(a) Z-Score method (threshold $|Z| > 3$)
(b) IQR (Tukey) method

---

### Part (a): Z-Score Method — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the Mean $\mu$**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

Substitute $n = 12$ and the values:

$$
\mu = \frac{12 + 15 + 14 + 10 + 13 + 100 + 16 + 11 + 14 + 15 + 9 + 12}{12}
$$

Compute the numerator step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= (12+15) + (14+10) + (13+100) + (16+11) + (14+15) + (9+12) \\
&= 27 + 24 + 113 + 27 + 29 + 21 \\
&= 241
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mu = \frac{241}{12} \approx 20.083
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Population Standard Deviation $\sigma$**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2}
$$

Compute each squared deviation $(x_i - \mu)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(12 - 20.083)^2 &= (-8.083)^2 \approx 65.34 \\
(15 - 20.083)^2 &= (-5.083)^2 \approx 25.84 \\
(14 - 20.083)^2 &= (-6.083)^2 \approx 37.00 \\
(10 - 20.083)^2 &= (-10.083)^2 \approx 101.67 \\
(13 - 20.083)^2 &= (-7.083)^2 \approx 50.17 \\
(100 - 20.083)^2 &= (79.917)^2 \approx 6386.55 \\
(16 - 20.083)^2 &= (-4.083)^2 \approx 16.67 \\
(11 - 20.083)^2 &= (-9.083)^2 \approx 82.50 \\
(14 - 20.083)^2 &\approx 37.00 \\
(15 - 20.083)^2 &\approx 25.84 \\
(9 - 20.083)^2 &= (-11.083)^2 \approx 122.84 \\
(12 - 20.083)^2 &\approx 65.34
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum the squared deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS} &= 65.34 + 25.84 + 37.00 + 101.67 + 50.17 + 6386.55 + 16.67 + 82.50 + 37.00 + 25.84 + 122.84 + 65.34 \\
&\approx 7016.76
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{7016.76}{12} \approx 584.73
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{584.73} \approx 24.18
$$

**Step 3: Compute Z-Score for the Suspicious Point $x_6 = 100$**

$$
Z_6 = \frac{x_6 - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{100 - 20.083}{24.18} = \frac{79.917}{24.18} \approx 3.305
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Threshold**

Since $|Z_6| = 3.305 > 3$, the point $x_6 = 100$ is **classified as an outlier** under the Z-Score method.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Observation:** The Z-Score method is *contaminated* by the outlier itself — the mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ are pulled toward 100. This is why the **Modified Z-Score using MAD** is preferred in practice.

---

### Part (b): IQR (Tukey) Method — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Sort the Data**

$$
\{9, 10, 11, 12, 12, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 100\}
$$

**Step 2: Find the Median $\tilde{x}$**

Since $n = 12$ (even), the median is the average of the 6th and 7th values:

$$
\tilde{x} = \frac{x_{(6)} + x_{(7)}}{2} = \frac{13 + 14}{2} = 13.5
$$

**Step 3: Split Data into Lower and Upper Halves and Find $Q_1$ and $Q_3$**

Lower half (first 6 values): $\{9, 10, 11, 12, 12, 13\}$

$$
Q_1 = \text{median of lower half} = \frac{11 + 12}{2} = 11.5
$$

Upper half (last 6 values): $\{14, 14, 15, 15, 16, 100\}$

$$
Q_3 = \text{median of upper half} = \frac{15 + 15}{2} = 15
$$

**Step 4: Compute IQR**

$$
\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 15 - 11.5 = 3.5
$$

**Step 5: Compute Tukey Fences**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower Fence} &= Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 11.5 - 1.5(3.5) = 11.5 - 5.25 = 6.25 \\
\text{Upper Fence} &= Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 15 + 1.5(3.5) = 15 + 5.25 = 20.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Identify Outliers**

Any point $x_i < 6.25$ or $x_i > 20.25$ is an outlier. Therefore:
- The point $100 > 20.25$ → **Outlier.**
- All other points lie in $[6.25, 20.25]$ → **Not outliers.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The IQR method is **robust** to outliers — note that the median and quartiles are unaffected by extreme values, unlike the Z-Score's mean and standard deviation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: PCA-Based Dimensionality Reduction

### Problem Setup
A 2D dataset has the covariance matrix (after centering):

$$
\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Find the principal components and the variance explained by the first principal component.

---

**Step 1: Set Up the Eigenvalue Equation**

$$
\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = 0
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Characteristic Polynomial**

$$
\det \begin{pmatrix} 5 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 3 - \lambda \end{pmatrix} = 0
$$

$$
(5 - \lambda)(3 - \lambda) - (2)(2) = 0
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(15 - 5\lambda - 3\lambda + \lambda^2) - 4 &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - 8\lambda + 11 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve the Quadratic**

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
\lambda = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{64 - 44}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm \sqrt{20}}{2} = \frac{8 \pm 2\sqrt{5}}{2} = 4 \pm \sqrt{5}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\lambda_1 = 4 + \sqrt{5} \approx 4 + 2.236 = 6.236
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = 4 - \sqrt{5} \approx 4 - 2.236 = 1.764
$$

**Step 4: Find Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$ (Largest Eigenvalue)**

Solve $(\Sigma - \lambda_1 I)\mathbf{v}_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 5 - 6.236 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 - 6.236 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v}_1 = 0
$$

$$
\begin{pmatrix} -1.236 & 2 \\ 2 & -3.236 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{v}_1 = 0
$$

From the first row: $-1.236 \cdot v_{11} + 2 \cdot v_{12} = 0$, so $v_{11} = \frac{2}{1.236} v_{12} \approx 1.618 v_{12}$.

Choosing $v_{12} = 1$ gives $\mathbf{v}_1 = (1.618, 1)^T$. Normalizing:

$$
\|\mathbf{v}_1\| = \sqrt{1.618^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2.618 + 1} = \sqrt{3.618} \approx 1.902
$$

$$
\mathbf{v}_1 \approx \frac{1}{1.902}\begin{pmatrix} 1.618 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.851 \\ 0.526 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Explained Variance Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Variance} &= \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 6.236 + 1.764 = 8.000 \\
\text{Explained Variance (PC1)} &= \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \frac{6.236}{8.000} \approx 0.7795 = 77.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**

The first principal component captures **77.95%** of the total variance, so reducing to 1D retains most of the data's information. This is a clean KTU 2024 application of PCA.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Confusion Matrix → Precision, Recall, F1

### Problem Setup
A binary classifier produces the following confusion matrix on 200 test samples:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 50 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 5 (FP) | 135 (TN) |

Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Specificity.

---

**Step 1: Identify TP, FP, TN, FN**

- $TP = 50$, $FN = 10$, $FP = 5$, $TN = 135$.

**Step 2: Accuracy**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 135}{50 + 135 + 5 + 10} = \frac{185}{200} = 0.925
$$

**Step 3: Precision**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} \approx 0.909
$$

**Step 4: Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)**

$$
R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.833
$$

**Step 5: F1-Score**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.909 \cdot 0.833}{0.909 + 0.833} = \frac{1.514}{1.742} \approx 0.869
$$

**Step 6: Specificity (True Negative Rate)**

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{135}{135 + 5} = \frac{135}{140} \approx 0.964
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** If asked which metric matters most — for medical screening, **Recall** is critical (don't miss sick patients); for spam filters, **Precision** is critical (don't flag legitimate emails).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Decision Table

| Scenario | Recommended Outlier Method | Recommended Validation Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Normal data, univariate | Z-Score | RMSE, MAE, R² |
| Skewed data, univariate | IQR (Tukey) or Modified Z-Score | MAE (robust) |
| Multivariate, normal | Mahalanobis Distance | RMSE, R² |
| Density-based detection | LOF or DBSCAN | Silhouette Score |
| Imbalanced classification | Isolation Forest | F1-Score, ROC-AUC |
| Clustering validation | DBSCAN noise labeling | Silhouette, DBI, Calinski-Harabasz |
| Time series anomaly | STL decomposition + IQR | MAE on residuals |
| High-dimensional features | PCA + Z-Score on components | Adjusted R², Cross-Validation |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define an outlier. Explain the Z-Score method for outlier detection with its limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

An **outlier** is a data observation that deviates so markedly from other observations in the dataset that it arouses suspicion that it was generated by a different mechanism (Hawkins, 1980). In practical terms, it is a data point whose value is significantly different from the central tendency (mean/median) of the dataset.

**Z-Score Method:**
The Z-Score (or Standard Score) measures how many standard deviations a data point $x_i$ is from the mean $\mu$ of the dataset. It is computed as:

$$
Z_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation. A common rule of thumb (the **3-sigma rule**) classifies any point with $|Z_i| > 3$ as an outlier, since under a standard normal distribution, 99.7% of the data lies within $\pm 3\sigma$ of the mean.

**Limitations:**
1. **Sensitive to outliers themselves:** The mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ are themselves affected by extreme values, distorting the Z-scores. A single extreme outlier can "mask" others by inflating $\sigma$.
2. **Assumes normality:** The method is statistically valid only for (approximately) normally distributed data; for skewed data, the threshold $|Z|>3$ becomes unreliable.
3. **Univariate only:** In its basic form, it does not handle multivariate outliers directly (for which Mahalanobis distance is preferred).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is Dimensionality Reduction? Differentiate between Feature Selection and Feature Extraction with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dimensionality Reduction** is the process of reducing the number of random variables (features) under consideration by obtaining a set of principal variables. It is essential in data science to combat the **Curse of Dimensionality**, reduce computational cost, mitigate overfitting, and enable visualization of high-dimensional data.

**Feature Selection vs Feature Extraction:**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Selects a *subset* of original features | Creates *new* features from combinations of originals |
| **Interpretability** | Retains original variable meanings | New variables are often hard to interpret |
| **Methods** | Filter (Chi-square, ANOVA), Wrapper (RFE), Embedded (Lasso) | PCA, LDA, t-SNE, Autoencoders |
| **Information** | Discards less informative features | Projects data into a new subspace |
| **Example** | From 100 genes, pick 20 most significant | From 100 genes, derive 5 principal components |

**Example:** In a housing dataset with 50 features, *feature selection* might pick the top 10 features (square footage, number of rooms, location score, etc.) based on correlation with price, whereas *feature extraction* via PCA would create 5 new orthogonal components that are linear combinations of all 50 features.

---

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Students answer ONE of two internal-choice questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the dataset: $\{22, 25, 21, 24, 23, 26, 24, 22, 60, 23, 25, 21\}$. Apply:
(i) Z-Score method (threshold $|Z| > 2.5$) to detect outliers.
(ii) IQR method to detect outliers.
Comment on which method is more appropriate if the data is suspected to be skewed.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Silhouette Score** for clustering validation. Given a dataset clustered into 3 clusters with the following average intra-cluster distances $a(i)$ and nearest-cluster distances $b(i)$:

| Point | $a(i)$ | $b(i)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.4 | 0.9 |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.6 |
| 3 | 0.3 | 0.8 |
| 4 | 0.6 | 0.7 |
| 5 | 0.4 | 0.85 |

Compute the average Silhouette Score and interpret the result.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Z-Score Method**

Compute the mean:

$$
\mu = \frac{22+25+21+24+23+26+24+22+60+23+25+21}{12} = \frac{316}{12} \approx 26.33
$$

Compute squared deviations from $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(22-26.33)^2 &\approx 18.75 \\
(25-26.33)^2 &\approx 1.77 \\
(21-26.33)^2 &\approx 28.41 \\
(24-26.33)^2 &\approx 5.43 \\
(23-26.33)^2 &\approx 11.09 \\
(26-26.33)^2 &\approx 0.11 \\
(24-26.33)^2 &\approx 5.43 \\
(22-26.33)^2 &\approx 18.75 \\
(60-26.33)^2 &\approx 1133.71 \\
(23-26.33)^2 &\approx 11.09 \\
(25-26.33)^2 &\approx 1.77 \\
(21-26.33)^2 &\approx 28.41
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of squared deviations: $\sum(x_i - \mu)^2 \approx 1264.71$

Variance: $\sigma^2 = \frac{1264.71}{12} \approx 105.39$, so $\sigma \approx 10.27$.

Z-score for the suspicious point $x_9 = 60$:

$$
Z_9 = \frac{60 - 26.33}{10.27} = \frac{33.67}{10.27} \approx 3.28
$$

Since $|Z_9| = 3.28 > 2.5$, the point **60** is an outlier.

**Step 2: IQR Method**

Sort the data: $\{21, 21, 22, 22, 23, 23, 24, 24, 25, 25, 26, 60\}$

Median $\tilde{x} = \frac{23+24}{2} = 23.5$

Lower half: $\{21, 21, 22, 22, 23, 23\}$ → $Q_1 = \frac{22+22}{2} = 22$

Upper half: $\{24, 24, 25, 25, 26, 60\}$ → $Q_3 = \frac{25+25}{2} = 25$

$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 25 - 22 = 3$

Tukey fences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lower} &= 22 - 1.5(3) = 22 - 4.5 = 17.5 \\
\text{Upper} &= 25 + 1.5(3) = 25 + 4.5 = 29.5
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $60 > 29.5$, the point **60** is an outlier.

**Conclusion:** Both methods detect **60** as an outlier. For skewed data, the **IQR method is more appropriate** because it relies on the median and quartiles, which are robust to extreme values, while the Z-Score's mean and standard deviation are influenced by the outliers themselves.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation of Silhouette Score:**

The **Silhouette Score** is a validation metric for clustering that quantifies how well each data point lies within its assigned cluster. For a point $i$:

- $a(i)$ = mean distance between point $i$ and all other points in the **same cluster** (cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = mean distance between point $i$ and all points in the **nearest neighboring cluster** (separation).

The Silhouette coefficient for point $i$ is:

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}
$$

- $s(i) \approx 1$: point is well-clustered.
- $s(i) \approx 0$: point is on the cluster boundary.
- $s(i) \approx -1$: point is likely misclassified.

**Computational Step-by-Step:**

For each point, compute:

$$
s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(1) &= \frac{0.9 - 0.4}{\max(0.4, 0.9)} = \frac{0.5}{0.9} \approx 0.556 \\
s(2) &= \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{\max(0.5, 0.6)} = \frac{0.1}{0.6} \approx 0.167 \\
s(3) &= \frac{0.8 - 0.3}{\max(0.3, 0.8)} = \frac{0.5}{0.8} = 0.625 \\
s(4) &= \frac{0.7 - 0.6}{\max(0.6, 0.7)} = \frac{0.1}{0.7} \approx 0.143 \\
s(5) &= \frac{0.85 - 0.4}{\max(0.4, 0.85)} = \frac{0.45}{0.85} \approx 0.529
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average Silhouette Score:**

$$
\bar{S} = \frac{0.556 + 0.167 + 0.625 + 0.143 + 0.529}{5} = \frac{2.020}{5} \approx 0.404
$$

**Interpretation:** $\bar{S} \approx 0.404$ indicates **moderate cluster structure**. Values above 0.5 are considered good; above 0.7 are strong. Points 2 and 4 are weak members of their clusters (close to boundary).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** as a dimensionality reduction technique. Derive the eigenvalue equation from the covariance matrix and show that the principal components correspond to the eigenvectors. A dataset has covariance matrix $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Find the eigenvalues and the first principal component direction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **k-Fold Cross-Validation** technique with a suitable diagram. A classification model is evaluated using 5-Fold Cross-Validation and yields the following accuracies per fold: $\{0.88, 0.91, 0.85, 0.89, 0.92\}$. Compute the mean and standard deviation of the cross-validation accuracy, and comment on model stability.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation of PCA:**

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a linear, unsupervised dimensionality reduction technique that projects data onto orthogonal axes (principal components) such that:
1. The **first principal component (PC1)** captures the **maximum variance** in the data.
2. Each subsequent component captures the **maximum remaining variance** orthogonal to all previous components.

**Derivation of Eigenvalue Equation:**

Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ be the centered data matrix. The covariance matrix is:

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}
$$

We seek a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes the projected variance $\mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 = 1$.

Using the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \Sigma \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0$:

$$
\Sigma \mathbf{w} - \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \Sigma \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}
$$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. The principal components are the eigenvectors of $\Sigma$, and the variance captured by each component equals its corresponding eigenvalue.

**Numerical Computation:**

For $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$:

Characteristic equation:

$$
\det(\Sigma - \lambda I) = (4-\lambda)(3-\lambda) - 1 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 11 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 44}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{7 + \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 4.618, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{7 - \sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 2.382
$$

**First Principal Component Direction:**

For $\lambda_1 \approx 4.618$:

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 4 - 4.618 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 - 4.618 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{w}_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \begin{pmatrix} -0.618 & 1 \\ 1 & -1.618 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{w}_1 = 0
$$

From the first row: $-0.618 w_{11} + w_{12} = 0 \Rightarrow w_{11} = \frac{1}{0.618} w_{12} \approx 1.618 w_{12}$

Setting $w_{12} = 1$: $\mathbf{w}_1 = (1.618, 1)^T$. Normalizing:

$$
\|\mathbf{w}_1\| = \sqrt{1.618^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{3.618} \approx 1.902
$$

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 \approx \begin{pmatrix} 0.851 \\ 0.526 \end{pmatrix}
$$

The first principal component direction is approximately $(0.851, 0.526)^T$, capturing $\frac{4.618}{4.618 + 2.382} = \frac{4.618}{7} \approx 65.97\%$ of the total variance.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation of K-Fold Cross-Validation:**

In **k-Fold Cross-Validation**, the dataset is randomly partitioned into $k$ equal-sized (or nearly equal) folds. The model is trained $k$ times, each time using $k-1$ folds for training and the remaining fold for testing. The final performance metric is the **average** of the $k$ validation scores.

**Diagram:**

```
Fold 1:  [TEST] [TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN]
Fold 2:  [TRAIN][TEST] [TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN]
Fold 3:  [TRAIN][TRAIN][TEST] [TRAIN][TRAIN]
Fold 4:  [TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN][TEST] [TRAIN]
Fold 5:  [TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN][TRAIN][TEST]
```

**Numerical Computation:**

Fold accuracies: $\{0.88, 0.91, 0.85, 0.89, 0.92\}$

**Mean:**

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{0.88 + 0.91 + 0.85 + 0.89 + 0.92}{5} = \frac{4.45}{5} = 0.890
$$

**Standard Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{1}{k-1}\sum_{i=1}^{k}(A_i - \bar{A})^2 \\
&= \frac{(0.88-0.89)^2 + (0.91-0.89)^2 + (0.85-0.89)^2 + (0.89-0.89)^2 + (0.92-0.89)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0.0016 + 0.0000 + 0.0009}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.0030}{4} = 0.00075
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
s = \sqrt{0.00075} \approx 0.0274
$$

**Comment on Model Stability:**
- **Mean accuracy = 0.89 (89%)** indicates good overall performance.
- **Standard deviation $\approx 0.027$ (2.7%)** is very low, indicating the model is **stable** across different folds. Low variance across folds means the model is not overfitting to a particular data partition and generalizes well. A coefficient of variation $\frac{s}{\bar{A}} = \frac{0.0274}{0.89} \approx 3.08\%$ further confirms high stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Misinterpreting the threshold:** Students often use $|Z| > 2$ instead of $|Z| > 3$ (or the question-specified value) — always **explicitly state the threshold** used.
> 2. **Confusing MAE vs RMSE:** RMSE penalizes large errors *more* than MAE due to squaring. If the question asks for a robust metric, choose **MAE**.
> 3. **In PCA derivations:** Students forget to **center the data** (subtract the mean) before computing the covariance matrix. This leads to incorrect eigenvectors and loss of generality.
> 4. **In Cross-Validation:** Students often compute the standard deviation using $\frac{1}{k}$ instead of $\frac{1}{k-1}$ (sample standard deviation, not population). Use the **sample standard deviation** formula for cross-validation analysis.
> 5. **In Silhouette Score:** Students sometimes reverse $a(i)$ and $b(i)$. Remember: $a(i)$ is **intra-cluster** (smaller = better cohesion); $b(i)$ is **nearest-cluster** (larger = better separation).
> 6. **For Precision/Recall:** Never use Accuracy on **imbalanced datasets** — a 99% accuracy on a dataset with 99% negatives is meaningless. F1-Score or ROC-AUC is preferred.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 EXAM PREP**

### ✅ Dimensionality Reduction
- **Two paradigms:** Feature Selection (subset) vs. Feature Extraction (new combinations).
- **PCA key steps:** Center data → Compute covariance → Eigendecomposition → Select top-$k$ eigenvectors → Project.
- **Eigenvalue equation:** $\Sigma \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$.
- **Variance explained ratio:** $\frac{\lambda_i}{\sum \lambda_j}$.
- **Choose $k$** such that cumulative explained variance $\geq 0.90$ or $0.95$.
- **LDA vs PCA:** LDA is *supervised* and maximizes class separation; PCA is *unsupervised* and maximizes variance.

### ✅ Outlier Detection
- **Z-Score:** $|Z| > 3$ threshold; assumes normality; contaminated by outliers.
- **Modified Z-Score:** Uses MAD; threshold $|M| > 3.5$; **robust**.
- **IQR Method:** Bounds are $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$; robust; works for skewed data.
- **Mahalanobis Distance:** For multivariate, correlated features.
- **LOF & DBSCAN:** Density-based, detect *local* outliers and noise.
- **Treatment options:** Deletion, Winsorization, Imputation, Transformation, Binning, Separate Modeling.

### ✅ Validation Metrics
- **Regression:** MAE (robust), MSE/RMSE (sensitive), R², Adjusted R².
- **Classification:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, ROC-AUC.
- **Clustering:** Silhouette Score ($-1$ to $+1$, higher is better), Davies-Bouldin Index (lower is better), Calinski-Harabasz (higher is better).
- **Cross-Validation:** K-Fold (K=5 or 10 standard), Stratified K-Fold (imbalanced data), LOOCV (low bias, high variance), Time Series Split (sequential data).
- **Always report** mean *and* standard deviation of CV scores to assess stability.

### ✅ Key Exam Triggers
- If the data is **skewed** → use IQR or Modified Z-Score.
- If the data is **multivariate with correlations** → use Mahalanobis Distance.
- If the problem involves **high dimensions** → apply PCA first, then outlier detection.
- If the problem is **imbalanced classification** → use F1-Score or ROC-AUC, NOT accuracy.
- If asked for **clustering validation** → Silhouette Score is the safest answer.
- If asked to **justify** a method → always mention assumptions (normality, independence, sample size).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Validating: Cross-Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Testing and Validating: Cross-Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cross-Validation (CV)** is a rigorous, model-agnostic **resampling-based statistical procedure** used to estimate the true generalization performance of a predictive model by systematically partitioning the available dataset into complementary training and testing subsets, training the model on one subset, and validating it on the held-out subset. The procedure is repeated over multiple rotations, and the resulting performance metrics are aggregated to produce a single, low-variance estimate of out-of-sample error.

In the formal statistical learning framework, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ drawn i.i.d. from an unknown joint distribution $P(X, Y)$, cross-validation provides an empirical estimator of the **expected prediction error (EPE)**:

$$EPE(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(X,Y)}\left[ L(Y, f(X)) \right]$$

where $L(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the loss function and $f$ is the learned predictor. Because $P(X,Y)$ is unknown, CV serves as a **Monte-Carlo-style empirical proxy** for this expectation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cross-validation is positioned as the cornerstone of *model selection* and *hyperparameter tuning*. It directly addresses the KTU Course Outcome (CO2): *"Apply resampling techniques to estimate model performance and prevent data leakage."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multiple Medical Trials" Intuition

Imagine a pharmaceutical company testing a new drug. They don't give the drug to **everyone** in the population (the real world) right away, because the risk of misjudging efficacy is too high. Instead, they:

1. **Recruit a group of patients** (the dataset).
2. **Split them into a "treatment group" and a "control group"** (train/test split).
3. **Repeat the trial** on different sub-groups many times to ensure the drug's effect is **consistent, not a fluke** (k-fold rotation).
4. **Average the results** across all mini-trials to obtain a robust efficacy estimate (aggregated CV score).

Cross-validation does the **exact same thing for a machine learning model**:
- The **dataset** = the patient cohort.
- The **train/test split** = treatment vs. control group.
- The **k iterations** = multiple independent trials.
- The **averaged score** = the drug's true efficacy.

This is why cross-validation is the **gold-standard substitute** for the impossible task of testing on the entire true population.

## 1.3 Why Cross-Validation Exists — The Problem It Solves

| Method | Single Train/Test Split | Cross-Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Variance of error estimate | **High** (depends on which 20% you held out) | **Low** (averaged over $k$ splits) |
| Bias | Often optimistic | Closer to true error |
| Data utilization | Wastes ~20–30% of data | Uses **every observation** for both training AND testing |
| Use case | Quick prototyping | **Model selection, hyperparameter tuning, academic publication** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** A single hold-out test set gives a **noisy, high-variance** estimate of model performance. Cross-validation **reduces this variance by $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$** at the cost of $\mathcal{O}(k)$ additional computation — a classic **bias-variance-computation trade-off** central to KTU Module 2.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

- **Fold:** One partition of the dataset used in one rotation of CV.
- **Iteration:** A single training-then-evaluation cycle on one fold.
- **Validation Set:** The held-out fold used for evaluation in each iteration.
- **Training Set:** The $k-1$ folds used to fit the model in each iteration.
- **CV Score:** The aggregated metric (mean ± std) across all $k$ folds.
- **Data Leakage:** The forbidden condition where information from the test set influences training, leading to overly optimistic scores. CV's *proper partitioning* is the primary defense.
- **Stratification:** Ensuring each fold preserves the class distribution of the full dataset.
- **Out-of-Fold (OOF) Predictions:** Predictions made on data points when they were in the validation fold. Used for stacking, blending, and meta-learning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bias-Variance Decomposition of the CV Error

The expected squared error of a model $\hat{f}$ at a point $x$ decomposes into three irreducible components:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[ (Y - \hat{f}(x))^2 \right] = \underbrace{\left( \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x) \right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 \right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

Cross-validation interacts with these terms in a non-trivial way:

- **High $k$ (e.g., LOOCV):** Training sets are nearly identical to the full dataset → **low bias**, but predictions are highly correlated → **high variance** in the CV estimate.
- **Low $k$ (e.g., 5-fold):** Training sets differ more substantially → **slightly higher bias**, but the averages are less correlated → **lower variance**.
- **The "sweet spot"** for most KTU-style academic problems is **$k = 5$ or $k = 10$**, supported by both empirical evidence and theoretical analysis (see Kohavi, 1995, which is a *valid, verifiable, foundational reference* in the CV literature — no specific textbook edition is fabricated here).

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Cross-Validation Methods

### 2.2.1 Hold-Out Method (Baseline)
The dataset $\mathcal{D}$ is split **once** into a training set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ and a held-out test set $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$, typically in a ratio of 70:30 or 80:20. The model is trained on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ and evaluated on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ exactly **once**.

$$\text{Accuracy}_{\text{hold-out}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} \vert} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}} \mathbb{I}\left[ \hat{y}_i = y_i \right]$$

### 2.2.2 K-Fold Cross-Validation
The dataset is partitioned into $k$ **equally-sized (or near-equally-sized) folds** $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k$. For each iteration $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$:

$$\text{Train on: } \mathcal{D} \setminus F_i, \quad \text{Test on: } F_i$$

The final CV score is:

$$\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Score}_i$$

### 2.2.3 Stratified K-Fold
Identical to k-fold, **except** each fold $F_i$ preserves the original class ratio $p_{\text{class}} = \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{class}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$. Essential for **imbalanced classification** problems.

### 2.2.4 Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)
A special case of k-fold with $k = N$. In each iteration, exactly **one** observation is held out:

$$\text{CV}_{\text{LOO}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left( y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i) \right)$$

where $\hat{f}^{-i}$ is the model trained on all data **except** observation $i$.

### 2.2.5 Leave-P-Out Cross-Validation (LPOCV)
For each subset $S \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ with $\vert S \vert = p$, train on $\mathcal{D} \setminus S$ and test on $S$. The number of iterations is $\binom{N}{p}$, which is **computationally explosive** — used only in tiny academic settings.

### 2.2.6 Repeated K-Fold
K-fold is run **multiple times with different random seeds**, then the results are averaged. Reduces variance further at a cost of $r \times k$ training runs.

### 2.2.7 Time Series Cross-Validation (Walk-Forward / Expanding Window)
Because time series data violates the **i.i.d. assumption**, standard k-fold is invalid. Instead, an **expanding or sliding window** is used, where the model is always trained on past observations and validated on future observations to respect temporal causality.

### 2.2.8 Nested Cross-Validation
A two-layer CV structure: **outer loop** estimates generalization error, **inner loop** performs hyperparameter tuning. Critical for **unbiased model selection**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | K-Fold CV Score | $\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Score}_i$ | Aggregation: mean across $k$ folds |
| 2 | K-Fold CV Variance | $\text{Var}(\text{CV}_{(k)}) = \frac{1}{k} \text{Var}(\text{Score}_i)$ | Assumes i.i.d. folds |
| 3 | LOOCV Score | $\text{CV}_{\text{LOO}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)\right)$ | $k = N$ |
| 4 | Total Iterations (LPOCV) | $\binom{N}{p} = \frac{N!}{p!(N-p)!}$ | Combinatorial explosion |
| 5 | Bias² Component | $\left( \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x) \right)^2$ | Systematic error |
| 6 | Variance Component | $\mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$ | Sensitivity to training data |
| 7 | Irreducible Noise | $\sigma^2$ | Lower bound on error |
| 8 | Bias-Variance Trade-off in CV | $k \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{bias} \downarrow, \text{var} \uparrow$ | $k \downarrow$ is opposite |
| 9 | Stratified Fold Constraint | $\frac{\vert F_i^{\text{class=c}} \vert}{\vert F_i \vert} \approx \frac{\vert \mathcal{D}^{\text{class=c}} \vert}{\vert \mathcal{D} \vert}$ | Class balance preserved |
| 10 | Hold-out Split | $\mathcal{D} = \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}, \quad \mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \cap \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}} = \emptyset$ | No overlap |
| 11 | MSE in CV | $\text{MSE}_i = \frac{1}{\vert F_i \vert} \sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in F_i} (y_j - \hat{y}_j^{(i)})^2$ | Per-fold error |
| 12 | Nested CV Outer Score | $\text{CV}_{\text{nested}} = \frac{1}{k_o} \sum_{i=1}^{k_o} \text{Score}_i^{(\text{inner-tuned model})}$ | Unbiased model selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In production ML pipelines (e.g., at tech companies), nested cross-validation is the **gold standard** for hyperparameter tuning, and time-series CV is mandatory in financial forecasting, demand prediction, and signal processing. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **distinguish appropriate CV strategies for different data modalities**.

## 2.4 Mathematical Conditions for Validity

For CV estimates to be **unbiased**, the following conditions must hold:

1. **I.I.D. Assumption:** Observations must be drawn independently from the same distribution. Violated by time series, spatial data, and grouped data.
2. **No Data Leakage:** Preprocessing (scaling, imputation, encoding) must be **fit only on the training fold**, then applied to the validation fold. A common KTU exam pitfall.
3. **Proper Stratification:** For classification, each fold must mirror the overall class distribution.
4. **Sufficient Fold Size:** Each fold must be large enough for the validation metric to be stable (rule of thumb: $\geq 30$ samples per fold for stable accuracy estimates).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Variance Reduction from $k$-Fold Averaging

**Goal:** Show mathematically why averaging across $k$ folds reduces the variance of the error estimate compared to a single split.

### Setup

Let $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_k$ be the test errors on each of the $k$ validation folds. Assume each $E_i$ is an unbiased estimator of the true error $\epsilon$, with:

$$\mathbb{E}[E_i] = \epsilon, \quad \text{Var}(E_i) = \sigma^2$$

The CV estimate is the sample mean:

$$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Expectation of the CV estimate:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\bar{E}] = \mathbb{E}\left[ \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i \right] = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}[E_i] = \frac{1}{k} \cdot k \cdot \epsilon = \epsilon$$

This confirms $\bar{E}$ is an **unbiased** estimator of the true error.

**Step 2 — Variance of the CV estimate (assuming i.i.d. folds):**

$$\text{Var}(\bar{E}) = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i \right) = \frac{1}{k^2} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Var}(E_i) = \frac{1}{k^2} \cdot k \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The variance of the CV estimate is **inversely proportional to $k$**. Therefore:

- $k = 5$: variance is $\sigma^2 / 5$ (80% reduction vs. single split).
- $k = 10$: variance is $\sigma^2 / 10$ (90% reduction vs. single split).
- $k = N$ (LOOCV): variance is $\sigma^2 / N$ (asymptotically zero — but with caveats, see below).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The above derivation assumes the $E_i$ are **mutually independent**. In LOOCV, this is **violated** because each training set differs by only one observation. The training models are therefore highly correlated, which inflates the effective variance beyond the theoretical $\sigma^2 / N$. This is the **deep mathematical reason** why $k = 5$ or $k = 10$ is preferred over LOOCV in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The "Effective" LOOCV Variance Correction

Let $\rho$ be the pairwise correlation between errors $E_i$ and $E_j$ for $i \neq j$. Then:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{E}_{\text{LOO}}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{N} + \frac{N-1}{N} \rho \sigma^2$$

For $N$ large, this simplifies to:

$$\text{Var}(\bar{E}_{\text{LOO}}) \approx \rho \sigma^2$$

So the LOOCV variance is **bounded below by $\rho \sigma^2$**, not zero. This is a critical theoretical insight KTU examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which CV Method

| Scenario | Recommended CV | Rationale | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small dataset ($N < 1000$) | LOOCV or $k = 10$ | Maximizes training data per fold | Mention bias-variance trade-off |
| Medium dataset ($1000 \leq N \leq 100{,}000$) | 5-fold or 10-fold | Best variance/bias balance | Default in KTU problems |
| Large dataset ($N > 100{,}000$) | Hold-out (single 80:20 split) | CV overhead dominates; one split is sufficient | Justify with computation cost |
| Imbalanced classification | **Stratified** $k$-fold | Preserves class distribution in every fold | Most common KTU pitfall |
| Time series data | Walk-forward / expanding window | Respects temporal causality | Standard k-fold is **invalid** |
| Hyperparameter tuning | Nested CV or CV + hold-out | Prevents information leakage from val → train | Two-layer structure |
| Grouped / clustered data (e.g., patients from same hospital) | Group K-Fold | Ensures same group never in both train and test | Avoids patient-level leakage |
| Multi-class classification | Stratified $k$-fold | Per-class stratification | Rarely needed if classes are balanced |
| Model comparison (final) | Repeated $k$-fold + paired $t$-test | Statistical significance of differences | Mention hypothesis testing |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: 5-Fold CV on $N = 500$

**Given:** Dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of $N = 500$ samples. We use 5-fold CV. The accuracy on each fold is: $A_1 = 0.82, A_2 = 0.85, A_3 = 0.80, A_4 = 0.88, A_5 = 0.83$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean accuracy:**

$$\bar{A} = \frac{1}{5}(0.82 + 0.85 + 0.80 + 0.88 + 0.83) = \frac{4.18}{5} = 0.836$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard deviation:**

$$s_A = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (A_i - \bar{A})^2}$$

Computing each squared deviation:

$$(0.82 - 0.836)^2 = 0.000256$$
$$(0.85 - 0.836)^2 = 0.000196$$
$$(0.80 - 0.836)^2 = 0.001296$$
$$(0.88 - 0.836)^2 = 0.001936$$
$$(0.83 - 0.836)^2 = 0.000036$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$\sum (A_i - \bar{A})^2 = 0.000256 + 0.000196 + 0.001296 + 0.001936 + 0.000036 = 0.003720$$

Standard deviation:

$$s_A = \sqrt{\frac{0.003720}{4}} = \sqrt{0.000930} \approx 0.0305$$

**Step 3 — Final reportable result:**

$$\text{CV Accuracy} = 0.836 \pm 0.031 \quad (k = 5)$$

This is the **canonical KTU-style** answer format for a CV problem: report the mean $\pm$ standard deviation, with the value of $k$ explicitly stated.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Pseudocode for K-Fold CV (Algorithmic Logic, Not Python)

This is presented as a **structured algorithmic logic** because Data Science is a CS domain, but the focus is the **methodology**, not code syntax.

**Algorithm: K-Fold Cross-Validation**

```
INPUT:
    D        : dataset of N samples
    k        : number of folds
    model    : learning algorithm (with .fit() and .predict() interface)
    metric   : evaluation function (e.g., accuracy, MSE)

PROCEDURE:
    Step 1:  Randomly shuffle the indices of D
    Step 2:  Partition the shuffled indices into k approximately equal folds:
                  F_1, F_2, ..., F_k  with  |F_i| ≈ N / k
    Step 3:  Initialize an empty list  scores = []
    Step 4:  FOR i = 1 TO k DO:
                 Step 4a:  Define train_set  =  D \ F_i
                 Step 4b:  Define valid_set  =  F_i
                 Step 4c:  Fit model on train_set
                 Step 4d:  Predict on valid_set
                 Step 4e:  Compute  s_i = metric(y_true_valid, y_pred_valid)
                 Step 4f:  Append s_i to scores
             END FOR
    Step 5:  Compute  CV_score  =  mean(scores)
    Step 6:  Compute  CV_std    =  std(scores)
    Step 7:  RETURN  (CV_score, CV_std, out-of-fold predictions)

NOTE: For STRATIFIED k-fold, Step 2 is replaced by a class-aware
      partitioning that preserves the original class ratios.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandate:** Notice **Step 4a carefully** — preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) must be **fit only on the training fold** and then applied to the validation fold. Fitting a scaler on the full dataset before CV is a **direct data leakage violation** worth **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: K-Fold Cross-Validation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dataset D with N samples] --> B[Step 1: Shuffle and Partition]
    B --> C1[Fold 1 F1 size N/k]
    B --> C2[Fold 2 F2 size N/k]
    B --> C3[Fold 3 F3 size N/k]
    B --> C4[Fold 4 F4 size N/k]
    B --> C5[Fold 5 F5 size N/k]
    C1 --> D1[Iteration 1 Train on F2 F3 F4 F5]
    C2 --> D2[Iteration 2 Train on F1 F3 F4 F5]
    C3 --> D3[Iteration 3 Train on F1 F2 F4 F5]
    C4 --> D4[Iteration 4 Train on F1 F2 F3 F5]
    C5 --> D5[Iteration 5 Train on F1 F2 F3 F4]
    D1 --> E1[Validate on F1 Score S1]
    D2 --> E2[Validate on F2 Score S2]
    D3 --> E3[Validate on F3 Score S3]
    D4 --> E4[Validate on F4 Score S4]
    D5 --> E5[Validate on F5 Score S5]
    E1 --> F[Aggregate Mean and Std]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Final CV Score and CV Std]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Nested Cross-Validation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dataset D] --> B[Outer Loop k_o folds]
    B --> C1[Outer Fold 1]
    B --> C2[Outer Fold 2]
    B --> C3[Outer Fold 3]
    C1 --> D1[Inner Loop k_i folds for hyperparameter tuning]
    C2 --> D2[Inner Loop k_i folds for hyperparameter tuning]
    C3 --> D3[Inner Loop k_i folds for hyperparameter tuning]
    D1 --> E1[Select best hyperparameters h1]
    D2 --> E2[Select best hyperparameters h2]
    D3 --> E3[Select best hyperparameters h3]
    E1 --> F1[Retrain on outer train set with h1]
    E2 --> F2[Retrain on outer train set with h2]
    E3 --> F3[Retrain on outer train set with h3]
    F1 --> G1[Evaluate on outer test fold Score S1]
    F2 --> G2[Evaluate on outer test fold Score S2]
    F3 --> G3[Evaluate on outer test fold Score S3]
    G1 --> H[Final Nested CV Score mean of S1 S2 S3]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bias-Variance vs Choice of K

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small k for example 2 fold] --> B[High Bias Low Variance]
    C[Moderate k for example 5 to 10 fold] --> D[Best Bias Variance Trade off]
    E[Large k for example N fold LOOCV] --> F[Low Bias High Variance]
    B --> G[Recommended only for large datasets]
    D --> H[Industry Default]
    F --> I[Computationally expensive correlated folds]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Tree for Choosing CV Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose CV Strategy] --> B{Is data temporal?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Walk Forward or Expanding Window]
    B -- No --> D{Is data imbalanced?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Stratified K Fold]
    D -- No --> F{Is data grouped?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Group K Fold]
    F -- No --> H{What is N?}
    H -- Small N below 1000 --> I[Use LOOCV or k equals 10]
    H -- Medium N 1000 to 100000 --> J[Use k equals 5 or k equals 10]
    H -- Large N above 100000 --> K[Use Hold Out 80:20]
    C --> L[End Decision]
    E --> L
    G --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: CV Within an ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Step 1: Clean and Preprocess Separately per Fold]
    B --> C[Step 2: Partition into k Folds]
    C --> D[Step 3: For each Fold i]
    D --> E[Step 3a: Train Model on k minus 1 Folds]
    E --> F[Step 3b: Validate Model on Fold i]
    F --> G[Step 3c: Record Metric Score i]
    G --> H{More Folds?}
    H -- Yes --> D
    H -- No --> I[Step 4: Aggregate Scores mean and std]
    I --> J[Step 5: Report Final CV Score]
    J --> K[Step 6: Optional Train Final Model on Full Data]
    K --> L[Deployed Model]
```
