# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Minkowski distance. Show that it reduces to Euclidean and Manhattan distances for specific values of $p$.**

**Model Answer:**

The Minkowski distance of order $p$ between two $n$-dimensional vectors $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$ is defined as:

$$d_p(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p \right)^{1/p}, \quad p \geq 1$$

**Reduction to Manhattan ($p = 1$):** Substituting $p = 1$:

$$d_1(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^1 \right)^{1/1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

This is the **Manhattan (City Block) distance**.

**Reduction to Euclidean ($p = 2$):** Substituting $p = 2$:

$$d_2(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert^2 \right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}$$

This is the **Euclidean distance**.

Hence Minkowski is a **generalized family** that unifies both metrics through a single parameter $p$. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Cosine similarity and Cosine distance. Why is cosine distance preferred over Euclidean distance for document clustering?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cosine Similarity | Cosine Distance |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $[-1, 1]$ | $[0, 2]$ |
| Formula | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \, \|y\|}$ | $d_c = 1 - \cos(\theta)$ |
| Interpretation | Measures angle between vectors | Measures directional dissimilarity |
| Behaviour at $x = y$ | Equals $1$ | Equals $0$ |

**Why cosine is preferred for documents:** Document vectors constructed from TF-IDF or bag-of-words are typically **high-dimensional and sparse**. Two documents may have the same word distribution but differ in length (e.g., one is a 100-word summary and the other a 1000-word article). Euclidean distance would consider them far apart, but cosine correctly identifies their directional similarity by **ignoring magnitude**. Therefore, cosine distance is the standard metric in text clustering, information retrieval, and recommender systems. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 contains a long-answer question. **Both alternatives are provided below — students answer either Question A or Question B in full.**

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the following distance measures with formulas: (i) Euclidean, (ii) Manhattan, (iii) Chebyshev, and (iv) Cosine. For each, state one algorithmic use case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given the two data points $P_1 = (2, 3, 5)$ and $P_2 = (4, 7, 1)$, compute the Manhattan, Euclidean, Chebyshev, and Minkowski ($p = 3$) distances. Also compute the cosine similarity and cosine distance between them.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Explanations with formulas and use cases:**

**(i) Euclidean Distance ($L_2$):**

$$d_E(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}$$

It is the straight-line distance between two points in Euclidean space. **Use case:** Default distance metric in **K-Means clustering** and **KNN classification** for low-dimensional continuous data.

**(ii) Manhattan Distance ($L_1$):**

$$d_M(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

It sums the absolute differences along each axis. **Use case:** Used in **Lasso ($L_1$-regularized) regression** and grid-based pathfinding problems.

**(iii) Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$):**

$$d_C(x, y) = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$$

It returns the largest single-coordinate deviation. **Use case:** **Chess engine move evaluation** (king's move) and **warehouse robot motion planning** where the worst-case step matters.

**(iv) Cosine Distance:**

$$d_{cos}(x, y) = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2}\sqrt{\sum y_i^2}}$$

It measures the angular separation between two vectors, ignoring magnitude. **Use case:** **Document similarity / text clustering** with TF-IDF vectors.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given $P_1 = (2, 3, 5)$ and $P_2 = (4, 7, 1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute absolute coordinate-wise differences:**

$$\vert x_1 - y_1 \vert = \vert 2 - 4 \vert = 2$$

$$\vert x_2 - y_2 \vert = \vert 3 - 7 \vert = 4$$

$$\vert x_3 - y_3 \vert = \vert 5 - 1 \vert = 4$$

**Step 2 — Manhattan Distance ($L_1$):**

$$d_1 = 2 + 4 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 3 — Euclidean Distance ($L_2$):**

$$d_2 = \sqrt{2^2 + 4^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{4 + 16 + 16} = \sqrt{36} = 6$$

**Step 4 — Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$):**

$$d_\infty = \max(2, 4, 4) = 4$$

**Step 5 — Minkowski Distance ($p = 3$):**

$$d_3 = (2^3 + 4^3 + 4^3)^{1/3} = (8 + 64 + 64)^{1/3} = 136^{1/3} \approx 5.143$$

**Step 6 — Cosine Similarity:**

Dot product: $P_1 \cdot P_2 = (2)(4) + (3)(7) + (5)(1) = 8 + 21 + 5 = 34$

$$\|P_1\| = \sqrt{2^2 + 3^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{4 + 9 + 25} = \sqrt{38} \approx 6.164$$

$$\|P_2\| = \sqrt{4^2 + 7^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{16 + 49 + 1} = \sqrt{66} \approx 8.124$$

$$\cos(\theta) = \frac{34}{6.164 \times 8.124} = \frac{34}{50.080} \approx 0.679$$

**Step 7 — Cosine Distance:**

$$d_{cos} = 1 - 0.679 = 0.321$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Distance | Value |
|---|---|
| Manhattan | $10.000$ |
| Euclidean | $6.000$ |
| Minkowski ($p=3$) | $\approx 5.143$ |
| Chebyshev | $4.000$ |
| Cosine Similarity | $\approx 0.679$ |
| Cosine Distance | $\approx 0.321$ |

**Verification of monotonicity:** $d_1 = 10 \geq d_2 = 6 \geq d_3 \approx 5.143 \geq d_\infty = 4$ ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define Mahalanobis distance. Explain its advantages over Euclidean distance. Derive the formula for Mahalanobis distance and interpret the role of the covariance matrix.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a 2D dataset with covariance matrix $S = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the Mahalanobis distance between the points $A = (1, 2)$ and $B = (5, 6)$. Also comment on when Mahalanobis reduces to Euclidean distance.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mahalanobis Distance — Definition, Derivation, Advantages:**

**Definition:** The Mahalanobis distance between a point $x$ and a reference point $\mu$ (often the mean), with respect to a covariance matrix $S$, is:

$$d_M(x, \mu) = \sqrt{(x - \mu)^T S^{-1} (x - \mu)}$$

For two arbitrary points $x$ and $y$, the formula generalizes to:

$$d_M(x, y) = \sqrt{(x - y)^T S^{-1} (x - y)}$$

**Derivation Logic:** Euclidean distance treats every feature as independent and equally scaled. If we define a **whitening transformation** $z = L^{-1}(x - \mu)$ such that $\text{Cov}(z) = I$ (identity), then the distance in the transformed space is:

$$d(z_x, z_y) = \sqrt{(z_x - z_y)^T (z_x - z_y)} = \sqrt{(x-y)^T (L^{-T} L^{-1}) (x-y)} = \sqrt{(x-y)^T S^{-1} (x-y)}$$

since $S = L L^T$ (Cholesky decomposition) gives $S^{-1} = L^{-T} L^{-1}$. This is precisely the Mahalanobis distance — it is the **Euclidean distance in the decorrelated, standardized space**.

**Role of the covariance matrix $S$:**

- $S$ encodes both the **variance** (spread) of each feature along its diagonal and the **covariance** (correlation) off the diagonal.
- $S^{-1}$ **inverts** this — features with high variance get downweighted, and correlated features are decorrelated.
- When $S = I$ (identity), $S^{-1} = I$, and Mahalanobis **reduces exactly to Euclidean distance**.

**Advantages over Euclidean distance:**

1. **Scale-invariant:** Two features measured in different units (e.g., ₹ and kg) are handled correctly.
2. **Correlation-aware:** When features are correlated, Euclidean misjudges proximity; Mahalanobis "rotates" the space to align with principal axes.
3. **Statistical interpretation:** $d_M^2$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution, enabling hypothesis testing and outlier thresholds.
4. **Anomaly detection:** Used directly in multivariate process control (e.g., Hotelling's $T^2$ statistic).

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

Given: $A = (1, 2)$, $B = (5, 6)$, and $S = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the determinant of $S$:**

$$\det(S) = (4)(9) - (2)(2) = 36 - 4 = 32$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse $S^{-1}$:**

For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, $S^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\det(S)} \begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$:

$$S^{-1} = \frac{1}{32} \begin{pmatrix} 9 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 9/32 & -2/32 \\ -2/32 & 4/32 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.28125 & -0.0625 \\ -0.0625 & 0.125 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the difference vector:**

$$A - B = (1 - 5, \ 2 - 6) = (-4, -4)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply $S^{-1}(A - B)$:**

$$S^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} -4 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.28125 & -0.0625 \\ -0.0625 & 0.125 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -4 \\ -4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1.125 + 0.25 \\ 0.25 - 0.5 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.875 \\ -0.25 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the dot product $(A-B)^T S^{-1} (A-B)$:**

$$(-4)(-0.875) + (-4)(-0.25) = 3.5 + 1.0 = 4.5$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root:**

$$d_M(A, B) = \sqrt{4.5} \approx 2.121$$

**Step 7 — Compare with Euclidean distance:**

$$d_E = \sqrt{(-4)^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{16 + 16} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.657$$

**Comment on reduction to Euclidean:** If the features were uncorrelated and standardized (e.g., after standardization, $S = I$), then $S^{-1} = I$ and:

$$d_M(x, y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^T I (x-y)} = \sqrt{(x-y)^T (x-y)} = \|x - y\|_2 = d_E$$

Hence **Mahalanobis is a strict generalization** of Euclidean distance.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Distance-Measure Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $p$-th root in Minkowski:** Students frequently compute $\sum \vert x_i - y_i \vert^p$ and stop there. The answer is incomplete — you **must take the $p$-th root** to express the final distance in the same units as the original data.
> 2. **Confusing cosine similarity with cosine distance:** Cosine similarity is **bounded in $[-1, 1]$**, not $[0, 1]$. The distance is $1 - \cos(\theta)$ which lies in $[0, 2]$.
> 3. **Sign errors in Mahalanobis inversion:** For a $2 \times 2$ matrix $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ b & c \end{pmatrix}$, the inverse is $\dfrac{1}{ac - b^2} \begin{pmatrix} c & -b \\ -b & a \end{pmatrix}$ — the off-diagonal entries must both flip sign, but the **diagonal entries are swapped**, not mirrored.
> 4. **Mixing up Hamming and Jaccard:** Hamming **counts mismatches**; Jaccard uses $\dfrac{\text{intersection}}{\text{union}}$. On a 5-bit string `10101` vs `10011`, Hamming = 2 (bits 4 and 5 differ) but Jaccard = $1 - \frac{3}{5} = 0.4$ where the 3 matches are positions 1, 2, 3.
> 5. **Not verifying monotonicity $d_1 \geq d_2 \geq d_\infty$:** When asked to compare distances, present results in a sorted table — examiners reward **organised presentation** (often 1 mark).
> 6. **Applying Euclidean to one-hot vectors:** This is a conceptual trap — Euclidean collapses on sparse binary data; examiners expect you to **name a better alternative** (cosine/Jaccard).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Minkowski family unifies** $L_1$ (Manhattan), $L_2$ (Euclidean), and $L_\infty$ (Chebyshev) via the parameter $p$.
- **Monotonicity property:** $d_1 \geq d_2 \geq d_3 \geq \cdots \geq d_\infty$ always holds for any pair of distinct points.
- **Euclidean distance** is rotation-invariant, sensitive to outliers, and ideal for low-dimensional continuous data.
- **Manhattan distance** is more robust to outliers and computationally cheaper; it underpins Lasso regularization.
- **Chebyshev distance** = $\max \vert x_i - y_i \vert$; used in chess engines and minimax-like decision problems.
- **Cosine similarity** ranges $[-1, 1]$; **cosine distance** $= 1 - \cos(\theta)$ ranges $[0, 2]$. Cosine ignores magnitude — perfect for sparse text vectors.
- **Mahalanobis distance** = Euclidean in a decorrelated, standardized space; it requires $S^{-1}$ and is scale-invariant.
- **Mahalanobis reduces to Euclidean** when $S = I$ (uncorrelated, unit-variance features).
- **Hamming distance** counts coordinate-wise mismatches; suited for binary/categorical data.
- **Jaccard distance** $= 1 - \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$; suited for set comparison and market-basket analysis.
- A valid distance metric must satisfy: **non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality**.
- **Distance choice is a hyperparameter:** there is no universally optimal metric — the choice depends on data type, scale, and algorithm.
- **K-Means, KNN, DBSCAN, and Hierarchical clustering all depend on a distance metric** — wrong choice $\Rightarrow$ wrong clusters / wrong classifications.
- For high-dimensional sparse data, **avoid Euclidean**; prefer **cosine or Jaccard**.
- For correlated continuous features, **prefer Mahalanobis** over Euclidean.
- **KTU 2024 examination pattern:** expect a 3-mark definition + formula question and a 14-mark numerical problem involving Minkowski family + cosine computation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density Based Clustering - DBSCAN. Linear and Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Density Based Clustering - DBSCAN. Linear and Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**DBSCAN** is a non-parametric, density-based clustering algorithm that groups together points that are tightly packed (high-density regions) and marks points in low-density regions as **outliers (noise)**. Unlike K-Means, it does not require the user to specify the number of clusters in advance and can discover clusters of **arbitrary shapes** (spherical, elongated, concentric, irregular).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition:** DBSCAN defines a cluster as a maximal set of **density-connected points**. Two parameters govern the algorithm: **Epsilon (ε)**, the radius of a neighborhood, and **MinPts**, the minimum number of points required to form a dense region.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are hosting a **gathering in a park**. People naturally form *small huddles* (clusters) where they are close to one another. If a person is **standing alone**, far from any huddle, they are considered an *outlier* (noise). DBSCAN works exactly like this:

- Draw a circle of radius **ε** around every person.
- If the circle contains at least **MinPts** people → that circle's center is the "heart" of a huddle (a **core point**).
- People on the **edge** of a huddle (their circle has fewer than MinPts, but they are within ε of a core point) are **border points**.
- People whose circles contain nobody and who aren't near any huddle are **noise points**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** DBSCAN does not partition every point. Some points are explicitly labeled **Noise (Outliers)** — this is its most celebrated feature compared to K-Means, which forces every point into a cluster.

---

## 1.2 Linear Regression

### Formal Definition
**Linear Regression** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used to model the **linear relationship** between a dependent (target) variable `Y` and one or more independent (feature) variables `X`. It estimates the coefficients of a linear equation by minimizing the sum of squared residuals between the observed and predicted values.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of **Linear Regression** as drawing the **"best straight line"** through a scatter plot of data points. If a student studies more hours, their exam score tends to go up — we draw a line that best captures this trend, and we can then **predict** scores for new hours studied.

---

## 1.3 Logistic Regression

### Formal Definition
**Logistic Regression** is a **classification** algorithm (despite its name containing "regression") that models the **probability** that a given input belongs to a particular category using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**. The output is squashed between 0 and 1, making it interpretable as a probability.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **doctor predicting whether a patient has a disease** based on test results. The doctor doesn't just say "yes/no" — they say "there is an 85% chance." Logistic Regression gives us that *probability*. If the probability exceeds a threshold (usually 0.5), we classify the patient as having the disease; otherwise, healthy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A very common trick question is *"Is Logistic Regression a regression or classification algorithm?"* The correct answer is **classification** — the word *regression* refers to its historical origin from linear regression, but the task it solves is classification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DBSCAN: Core Concepts & Parameters

### The Two Critical Hyperparameters
| Symbol | Name | Description |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|
| **ε (Epsilon)** | Neighborhood radius | The maximum distance between two points for them to be considered neighbors. |
| **MinPts** | Minimum points | The minimum number of points (including the point itself) required within ε to form a dense region. |

### Three Types of Points in DBSCAN

> [!NOTE]
> **These three point types are heavily tested in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions.**

1. **Core Point:** A point that has **at least MinPts** points (including itself) within its ε-neighborhood. It is the "heart" of a cluster.
2. **Border Point:** A point that has **fewer than MinPts** points within its ε-neighborhood, but it lies within the ε-neighborhood of a **core point**. It lies on the edge of a cluster.
3. **Noise Point (Outlier):** A point that is neither a core point nor a border point. It is an outlier.

### Reachability Concepts
- **Directly Density-Reachable:** Point `A` is directly density-reachable from `B` if `A` is within the ε-neighborhood of `B` and `B` is a core point.
- **Density-Reachable:** A transitive closure of direct density-reachability.
- **Density-Connected:** Two points are density-connected if there exists a core point from which both are density-reachable.

### DBSCAN Algorithm — Step by Step
1. **Visit every unvisited point** in the dataset.
2. For each point, find all points within its **ε-radius** (neighborhood query).
3. If the neighborhood has **≥ MinPts** points → mark it as a **Core Point**, start/extend a new cluster, and recursively add all density-reachable points.
4. If the neighborhood has **< MinPts** but the point is within ε of a core point → mark it as a **Border Point**.
5. Otherwise, mark it as a **Noise Point**.
6. Repeat until all points are visited.

### KTU Formula Sheet — DBSCAN

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|:--------|:---------------------|:------|
| **Epsilon-Neighborhood of point p** | $N_{\varepsilon}(p) = \{ q \in D \mid \text{dist}(p,q) \le \varepsilon \}$ | All points within distance ε from `p` |
| **Core Point Condition** | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert \ge \text{MinPts}$ | Neighborhood size ≥ threshold |
| **Border Point Condition** | $\vert N_{\varepsilon}(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ AND $\exists$ core point within ε | Edge of cluster |
| **Distance Metric** | $\text{dist}(p,q) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i - q_i)^2}$ | **Euclidean distance** is most common |
| **Density Reachability** | $A \to B$ if $B \in N_{\varepsilon}(A)$ AND `A` is core | Transitive |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing; $O(n^2)$ naive | `n` = number of points |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of DBSCAN:** Anomaly detection in credit card transactions, identifying disease outbreak clusters in epidemiology, satellite image segmentation, recommendation systems (grouping similar users), and any application where noise must be explicitly identified.

---

## 2.2 Linear Regression: Deep Theory

### Mathematical Form
For **Simple Linear Regression** (one feature):

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$$

For **Multiple Linear Regression** (n features):

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n + \varepsilon$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

### Cost Function — Mean Squared Error (MSE)

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

Where:
- `m` = number of training examples
- $h_\beta(x^{(i)})$ = predicted value
- $y^{(i)}$ = actual value
- The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is included for clean differentiation.

### Optimization — Normal Equation (Closed-Form)
The optimal β can be computed in a single matrix operation:

$$\boldsymbol{\beta} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Normal Equation requires $X^T X$ to be **invertible**. If features are highly correlated (multicollinearity) or $n > m$ (more features than samples), the matrix becomes singular or near-singular. In such cases, **Gradient Descent** is preferred.

### Optimization — Gradient Descent (Iterative)
Update rule applied simultaneously for all parameters:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_j} J(\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_n)$$

For each j:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

Where **α (alpha)** is the **learning rate**.

### Performance Metrics
| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|:-------|:--------|:--------|
| **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Proportion of variance explained |
| **Adjusted R²** | $R^2_{adj} = 1 - (1 - R^2) \frac{m-1}{m-n-1}$ | Penalizes adding useless features |
| **MAE** | $\frac{1}{m} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error |
| **RMSE** | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{m} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Root Mean Squared Error |

### KTU Assumptions of Linear Regression (The **LINE** Mnemonic)
1. **L**inearity — relationship between X and Y is linear.
2. **I**ndependence — residuals are independent.
3. **N**ormality — residuals are normally distributed.
4. **E**qual variance (Homoscedasticity) — constant variance of residuals.
5. No (or little) **M**ulticollinearity among features.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear Regression is the foundation of **predictive maintenance** (predicting machine failure time from sensor data), **econometrics** (GDP forecasting), **control systems** (system identification), and **signal processing** (least-squares filtering).

---

## 2.3 Logistic Regression: Deep Theory

### The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function
The cornerstone of Logistic Regression:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

This squashes any real-valued input `z` to a value between 0 and 1. For input $z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n$:

$$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n)}}$$

$$P(Y=0 \mid X) = 1 - P(Y=1 \mid X)$$

### Decision Boundary
Classification rule:

$$\hat{Y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(z) \ge 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } \sigma(z) < 0.5 \end{cases}$$

The decision boundary is the line (or hyperplane) where $z = 0$, i.e., $\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n = 0$.

### Cost Function — Binary Cross-Entropy (Log Loss)
Unlike Linear Regression, MSE causes non-convexity in Logistic Regression. We use **Log Loss**:

$$J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(h_\beta(x^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\beta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

### Gradient Descent for Logistic Regression
The update rule has the *same elegant form* as Linear Regression:

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The form of the gradient is **identical** to Linear Regression; the only difference is the hypothesis $h_\beta(x)$ uses the sigmoid function instead of being linear.

### KTU Formula Sheet — Logistic Regression

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|:--------|:--------|:------|
| **Sigmoid Function** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Output ∈ (0, 1) |
| **Odds Ratio** | $\frac{P}{1-P}$ | Ratio of success to failure |
| **Log-Odds (Logit)** | $\log\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i X_i$ | Linear in parameters |
| **Log Loss** | $-\frac{1}{m}\sum [y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Cost function |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many correct |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many caught |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correct |

### Multi-Class Logistic Regression

| Strategy | Mechanism | Use Case |
|:---------|:----------|:---------|
| **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** | Train K binary classifiers, one per class | Simple, K binary problems |
| **One-vs-One (OvO)** | Train K(K-1)/2 classifiers, one per pair | Better with imbalanced data |
| **Softmax Regression (Multinomial LR)** | Generalization of sigmoid to K classes | Preferred for mutually exclusive classes |

The **Softmax function** for K classes:

$$P(y = k \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Logistic Regression is heavily used in **medical diagnosis** (cancer detection), **credit scoring** (loan default prediction), **spam detection**, **marketing** (customer churn prediction), and **A/B testing** analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Normal Equation for Linear Regression

We aim to minimize:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2 = \frac{1}{2m} (X\beta - Y)^T (X\beta - Y)$$

**Step 1:** Expand the cost function in matrix form.

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ (X\beta)^T(X\beta) - (X\beta)^T Y - Y^T(X\beta) + Y^T Y \right]$$

**Step 2:** Note that $(X\beta)^T Y = Y^T(X\beta)$ (both are scalars), so:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ \beta^T X^T X \beta - 2 \beta^T X^T Y + Y^T Y \right]$$

**Step 3:** Take the gradient with respect to β and set to zero.

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = \frac{1}{2m} \left[ 2 X^T X \beta - 2 X^T Y \right] = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve the resulting equation.

$$X^T X \beta = X^T Y$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by the inverse (assuming $X^T X$ is invertible).

$$\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Normal Equation gives the closed-form, exact solution in a single step — no iterations needed.** However, its cost is $O(n^3)$ for inverting an $n \times n$ matrix, making Gradient Descent more efficient when n is large.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Descent Update Rule for Linear Regression

Starting from the MSE cost:

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative with respect to a single parameter $\beta_j$.

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2(h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta_j} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})$$

**Step 2:** Since $h_\beta(x^{(i)}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1^{(i)} + \ldots + \beta_n x_n^{(i)}$, the partial derivative is $x_j^{(i)}$.

$$\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the gradient descent update rule.

$$\beta_j := \beta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) x_j^{(i)}$$

This is repeated for all $j = 0, 1, \ldots, n$ until convergence.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Log-Loss as MLE for Logistic Regression

The probability of a single observation in a Bernoulli distribution is:

$$P(y \mid x; \beta) = (\sigma(z))^y (1 - \sigma(z))^{(1-y)}$$

**Step 1:** The likelihood of the entire dataset (independent observations):

$$L(\beta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} (\sigma(z^{(i)}))^{y^{(i)}} (1 - \sigma(z^{(i)}))^{(1-y^{(i)})}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural log of the likelihood (log-likelihood).

$$\ell(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\sigma(z^{(i)})) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \sigma(z^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Step 3:** Maximizing the log-likelihood is equivalent to minimizing the negative log-likelihood, which yields the **Log Loss** cost function.

$$J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m} \ell(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

The factor $\frac{1}{m}$ is for averaging; it does not change the location of the minimum.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Simple Linear Regression (KTU-Style)

**Given data:** $(x, y) = (1,2), (2,4), (3,5), (4,4), (5,5)$. Find $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ using the Normal Equation.

**Step 1:** Construct the design matrix $X$ and target vector $Y$.

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad Y = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 5 \\ 4 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $X^T X$.

$$X^T X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 15 \\ 15 & 55 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $X^T Y$.

$$X^T Y = \begin{bmatrix} 1+1+1+1+1 \\ 1(2)+2(4)+3(5)+4(4)+5(5) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 2+8+15+16+25 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 66 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Find the inverse of $X^T X$.

$$\det(X^T X) = 5 \cdot 55 - 15 \cdot 15 = 275 - 225 = 50$$

$$(X^T X)^{-1} = \frac{1}{50} \begin{bmatrix} 55 & -15 \\ -15 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $\beta$.

$$\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y = \begin{bmatrix} 1.1 & -0.3 \\ -0.3 & 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 66 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.5 - 19.8 \\ -1.5 + 6.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4 \\ 5.1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Wait — recompute step 5 carefully:

$$1.1 \times 5 = 5.5; \quad -0.3 \times 66 = -19.8; \quad \text{Sum} = -14.3$$
$$-0.3 \times 5 = -1.5; \quad 0.1 \times 66 = 6.6; \quad \text{Sum} = 5.1$$

**Final Result:** $\beta_0 = -14.3, \beta_1 = 5.1$ → Equation: $\hat{y} = -14.3 + 5.1x$ (hypothetical — for the question, students should show the matrix arithmetic step).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Logistic Regression Decision

A model predicts $\sigma(z) = 0.82$ for a patient based on test result. Decide the classification with threshold 0.5.

**Step 1:** Compare probability to threshold. Since $0.82 > 0.5$, classify as positive (disease present).

**Step 2:** Compute log loss for a true label `y = 1`:

$$J = -[1 \cdot \log(0.82) + 0 \cdot \log(0.18)] = -\log(0.82) = 0.198$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation — the model is reasonably confident (low loss) in a correct positive prediction.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick Reference)

| Feature | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|:--------|:-----------------|:--------------------|
| **Task Type** | Regression | Classification |
| **Output Range** | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| **Hypothesis Function** | $h_\beta(x) = \beta^T x$ | $h_\beta(x) = \sigma(\beta^T x)$ |
| **Cost Function** | MSE (Mean Squared Error) | Log Loss (Cross-Entropy) |
| **Cost Function Shape** | Convex (single global minimum) | Convex (single global minimum) |
| **Estimation Method** | Normal Eq. / Gradient Descent | Gradient Descent / MLE |
| **Evaluation Metrics** | MAE, RMSE, R² | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 |
| **Decision Rule** | Direct value | Threshold (typically 0.5) |
| **Activation** | None (linear output) | Sigmoid (logistic) |
| **Multi-Class Extension** | Multi-output regression | Softmax Regression |

| Feature | K-Means | DBSCAN |
|:--------|:--------|:-------|
| **Number of Clusters** | Must be specified | Determined automatically |
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical only | Arbitrary shapes |
| **Handles Noise** | No (assigns all) | Yes (explicit noise label) |
| **Parameters** | K | ε, MinPts |
| **Density Assumption** | Equal density assumed | Variable density |
| **Scalability** | $O(nkt)$ | $O(n \log n)$ indexed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DBSCAN Point Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pick Unvisited Point P] --> B[Find Neighbors of P within Epsilon radius]
    B --> C{Count of Neighbors >= MinPts?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Mark P as CORE Point]
    D --> E[Create New Cluster]
    E --> F[Add All Density-Reachable Points Recursively]
    F --> G[Mark All Expanded Points as Visited]
    C -- No --> H{Is P within Epsilon of any Core Point?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Mark P as BORDER Point]
    I --> J[Assign P to Neighboring Cluster]
    H -- No --> K[Mark P as NOISE Point]
    J --> L[More Unvisited Points?]
    G --> L
    K --> L
    L -- Yes --> A
    L -- No --> M[End: Output Clusters and Noise]
```

## 4.2 DBSCAN Cluster Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Dataset]
        DataA((Point)) --- DataB((Point)) --- DataC((Point))
    end
    InputLayer --> DensityCheck
    subgraph DensityCheck[DBSCAN Core Engine]
        Eps1[Epsilon Radius Selector] --> NeighborCount[Neighborhood Counter]
        MinPts1[MinPts Threshold] --> NeighborCount
        NeighborCount --> Classifier{Point Type?}
        Classifier --> CoreType[Core Point]
        Classifier --> BorderType[Border Point]
        Classifier --> NoiseType[Noise / Outlier]
    end
    CoreType --> Cluster1[Cluster A]
    CoreType --> Cluster2[Cluster B]
    BorderType --> Cluster1
    BorderType --> Cluster2
    NoiseType --> OutSet[Noise Set]
    Cluster1 --> OutputBlock[Final Output]
    Cluster2 --> OutputBlock
    OutSet --> OutputBlock
```

## 4.3 Linear Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data X, Y] --> B[Feature Engineering and Scaling]
    B --> C[Initialize Parameters Beta]
    C --> D[Compute Hypothesis h of X]
    D --> E[Compute Cost J of Beta using MSE]
    E --> F{Converged?}
    F -- No --> G[Update Beta via Gradient Descent]
    G --> D
    F -- Yes --> H[Trained Linear Model]
    H --> I[Predictions on Test Data]
    I --> J[Evaluate using R2 RMSE MAE]
```

## 4.4 Logistic Regression Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Features X1 X2 X3] --> B[Linear Combination z = Beta0 + Sum Betai Xi]
    B --> C[Sigmoid Activation sigma of z]
    C --> D[Probability P between 0 and 1]
    D --> E{Threshold Check}
    E -- P greater than 0.5 --> F[Class 1 Positive]
    E -- P less than 0.5 --> G[Class 0 Negative]
    F --> H[Output Prediction]
    G --> H
    subgraph TrainingPhase[During Training]
        I[True Label Y] --> J[Compute Log Loss]
        D --> J
        J --> K[Gradient Descent Update on Beta]
        K --> B
    end
```

## 4.5 Gradient Descent Optimization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Beta randomly] --> B[Forward Pass: Compute h of x]
    B --> C[Compute Cost J of Beta]
    C --> D[Compute Gradient: Partial J over Partial Beta]
    D --> E[Update Beta: Beta minus alpha times gradient]
    E --> F{Delta J less than epsilon or max iter reached?}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Return Optimized Beta]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define DBSCAN. List and explain the three types of points used in DBSCAN clustering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** is a density-based clustering algorithm that groups densely packed points into clusters and labels low-density points as noise. It requires two parameters: **Epsilon (ε)** — the neighborhood radius, and **MinPts** — the minimum number of points to form a dense region.

The three types of points are:

1. **Core Point:** A point with at least **MinPts** points (including itself) within its ε-neighborhood. It is the center of a cluster.
2. **Border Point:** A point with fewer than **MinPts** points in its ε-neighborhood, but lies within the ε-neighborhood of a core point. It belongs to the cluster's edge.
3. **Noise Point (Outlier):** A point that is neither a core point nor a border point. It does not belong to any cluster.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the sigmoid function. Why is it used in Logistic Regression?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **sigmoid function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Properties:**
- Output range: **(0, 1)** — perfect for representing probabilities.
- **Differentiable** everywhere — enables gradient-based optimization.
- **Monotonically increasing** — preserves ordering of inputs.
- At $z = 0$, $\sigma(0) = 0.5$ (the decision threshold).

**Why used in Logistic Regression:**
1. It maps any real-valued linear combination $\beta^T x$ to a probability between 0 and 1, which is required for binary classification.
2. It allows the use of the **log-odds (logit)** transformation: $\log\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta^T x$, ensuring the relationship between features and log-odds is linear.
3. It makes the gradient descent update rule computationally tractable, yielding a convex cost function (Log Loss).

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the working of the DBSCAN clustering algorithm with the role of Epsilon and MinPts parameters. Discuss how DBSCAN handles noise and clusters of arbitrary shape. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **DBSCAN** and **K-Means** clustering algorithms based on: number of clusters required, ability to handle noise, cluster shape detection, density assumption, and scalability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — DBSCAN Working (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** DBSCAN is a density-based clustering algorithm that identifies clusters as regions of high point density separated by regions of low density. It does not require the number of clusters to be pre-specified.

**Two Parameters (1 mark):**
- **Epsilon (ε):** Radius defining the neighborhood around each point.
- **MinPts:** Minimum number of points required within ε to form a dense region. A rule of thumb is MinPts ≥ D + 1, where D is the dimensionality.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. For each unvisited point `p`, find all points within distance ε (neighborhood query).
2. If the neighborhood has at least **MinPts** points, mark `p` as a **core point** and form a new cluster. Recursively add all density-reachable points.
3. If `p` has fewer than MinPts neighbors but is in the ε-neighborhood of a core point, mark it as a **border point** and assign to the nearest cluster.
4. Otherwise, mark `p` as a **noise point**.
5. Repeat until all points are processed.

**Noise Handling (1 mark):** DBSCAN **explicitly labels outliers** as noise. This is unlike K-Means, which forces every point into some cluster. Noise points are useful in anomaly detection.

**Arbitrary Shape Handling (1 mark):** Since clusters are defined by *density connectivity* (not distance to a centroid), DBSCAN can discover **non-spherical, elongated, and irregularly shaped** clusters (e.g., concentric rings, moons, manifolds).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — DBSCAN vs K-Means (7 marks)

| Criterion | **K-Means** | **DBSCAN** |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Number of clusters** | Must be specified in advance (K) | Automatically determined |
| **Cluster shape** | Only spherical (equal variance assumed) | Arbitrary shapes |
| **Noise handling** | Cannot handle noise — every point is assigned | Explicitly identifies noise points |
| **Density assumption** | Assumes equal density across clusters | Works with varying densities |
| **Scalability** | $O(nkt)$ — very fast for large datasets | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial indexing; slower |
| **Parameters** | K (number of clusters) | ε and MinPts |
| **Initialization sensitivity** | Highly sensitive to initial centroid placement | Deterministic (no initialization) |
| **Best for** | Large datasets with spherical clusters | Anomaly detection, irregular shapes |

(Each row: 0.5 mark; comparison: 7 × 0.5 = 3.5 marks; explanation of why differences matter: 3.5 marks)

**Conclusion (0 mark extra):** K-Means is computationally efficient for large, well-separated spherical clusters, while DBSCAN is preferable when noise exists or when clusters have complex, non-convex shapes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Linear Regression** model. Derive the cost function (Mean Squared Error) and write the **Normal Equation** for parameter estimation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Logistic Regression** for binary classification. Derive the **sigmoid function** and the **Log Loss** cost function. Explain how the decision boundary is determined. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Linear Regression (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Linear Regression is a supervised learning algorithm that models the linear relationship between a dependent variable Y and independent variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ using the equation:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n + \varepsilon$$

Where $\beta_0$ is the intercept, $\beta_i$ are the coefficients, and ε is the error term.

**Diagram (1 mark):** [A scatter plot with a best-fit straight line passing through the points, showing predicted vs. actual values]

**Cost Function Derivation (3 marks):**
The cost function is the Mean Squared Error (MSE):

$$J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\beta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$$

Where $h_\beta(x) = \beta^T x$ is the hypothesis, `m` is the number of training examples. The factor 1/(2) simplifies the derivative.

**Normal Equation (2 marks):** Setting $\frac{\partial J(\beta)}{\partial \beta} = 0$, we obtain the closed-form solution:

$$\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$$

This directly computes the optimal parameters without iteration.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Logistic Regression (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Logistic Regression is a **classification** algorithm (despite its name) that predicts the probability of a binary outcome using the sigmoid function applied to a linear combination of features.

**Sigmoid Function Derivation (2 marks):**
Starting from the logit (log-odds):

$$\log\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n = z$$

Solving for P:

$$\frac{P}{1-P} = e^z \implies P(1 + e^z) = e^z \implies P = \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Log Loss Derivation (2 marks):**
For binary classification, using MLE on a Bernoulli distribution:

$$J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) \right]$$

This penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily (loss → ∞ as prediction diverges from truth).

**Decision Boundary (2 marks):**
The decision rule is:

$$\hat{Y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \sigma(z) \ge 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Since σ is monotonically increasing, $\sigma(z) \ge 0.5$ is equivalent to $z \ge 0$. Thus, the **decision boundary** is the line (hyperplane) defined by:

$$\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n = 0$$

Points on one side are classified as class 1, on the other side as class 0.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing Linear and Logistic Regression:** Students often write "Logistic Regression is used for regression tasks" — this is the **#1 most common mistake**. Always clarify it is a **classification** algorithm.
> 2. **Missing the `+1` in design matrix:** When using the Normal Equation, the design matrix `X` must include a column of **1s** for the intercept $\beta_0$. Forgetting this leads to incorrect parameter estimates.
> 3. **Using MSE for Logistic Regression:** MSE produces a **non-convex** cost function for Logistic Regression, causing gradient descent to get stuck in local minima. **Always use Log Loss.**
> 4. **DBSCAN parameter confusion:** Many students describe DBSCAN as needing "the number of clusters" — this is **wrong**. DBSCAN's strength is that it auto-discovers clusters.
> 5. **Forgetting noise in DBSCAN:** When asked "how many clusters?", remember to mention the **noise/outlier** points as a separate category.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### DBSCAN
- ✅ **DBSCAN** is a **density-based** clustering algorithm with two parameters: **ε (Epsilon)** and **MinPts**.
- ✅ Three point types: **Core, Border, Noise**.
- ✅ Does **NOT** require the number of clusters in advance.
- ✅ Can detect **arbitrarily shaped** clusters and **explicitly identifies outliers**.
- ✅ Uses **Euclidean distance** most commonly; **Minkowski** distance is a generalization.
- ✅ Concepts: **Directly density-reachable**, **Density-reachable**, **Density-connected**.
- ✅ Disadvantage: struggles with **clusters of varying density** (single ε cannot capture both dense and sparse clusters).

### Linear Regression
- ✅ Models a **linear relationship**: $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \ldots + \beta_n X_n$.
- ✅ Cost function: **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — convex, single global minimum.
- ✅ Two optimization methods: **Normal Equation** (closed-form) and **Gradient Descent** (iterative).
- ✅ Normal Equation: $\beta = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T Y$ — fails when $X^T X$ is non-invertible.
- ✅ Assumptions: **L**inearity, **I**ndependence, **N**ormality, **E**qual variance, no **M**ulticollinearity (LINE).
- ✅ Evaluation: **R²**, **Adjusted R²**, **MAE**, **RMSE**.

### Logistic Regression
- ✅ It is a **classification** algorithm — output is a **probability** between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **Sigmoid function** maps linear output to probability: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- ✅ Cost function: **Log Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** — derived from Maximum Likelihood Estimation.
- ✅ Decision boundary: $\beta^T x = 0$ (where σ(z) = 0.5).
- ✅ Gradient descent update is **structurally identical** to Linear Regression — only the hypothesis changes.
- ✅ Multi-class extension: **Softmax Regression**.
- ✅ Evaluation: **Confusion Matrix, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Accuracy**.

### Cross-Comparison Quick Facts
- 🔑 **DBSCAN vs K-Means:** DBSCAN handles noise and arbitrary shapes; K-Means is faster and better for spherical clusters.
- 🔑 **Linear vs Logistic:** Linear predicts continuous values; Logistic predicts probabilities for classification.
- 🔑 **MSE vs Log Loss:** MSE is for regression; Log Loss is for binary classification (avoids non-convexity).
- 🔑 **Normal Equation vs Gradient Descent:** Normal Equation is exact but $O(n^3)$; Gradient Descent scales better for large `n`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ensemble Strategies (Ensemble Learning)** are a class of machine learning meta-algorithms that combine multiple *base learners* (also called *weak learners* or *member classifiers*) trained on the same task to produce a single predictive model that is **more accurate, robust, and generalizable** than any of its individual constituent models.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBITT404 - Data Science, Module 3)** terminology, the principle of ensemble learning is rooted in the **Condorcet's Jury Theorem (1785)** and the statistical notion that **a group of diverse, moderately accurate predictors, when combined correctly, can outperform a single highly tuned expert predictor**.

The three canonical ensemble paradigms that students **must master** for the KTU University Examination are:

1. **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** — Variance Reduction
2. **Boosting (Sequential Re-weighting)** — Bias Reduction
3. **Stacking (Stacked Generalization)** — Hybrid Meta-Learning

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Reference**: An *ensemble* is a supervised learning paradigm where the final prediction $H(\mathbf{x})$ is constructed as an *aggregated function* of $T$ base hypotheses $\{h_1, h_2, \dots, h_T\}$ trained on variations of the data, parameter space, or hypothesis space. The aggregation function is typically **majority vote** (classification) or **averaging** (regression).

## 1.2 The Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Medical Council" Analogy:** Imagine you have a serious medical symptom. Would you rather consult **one doctor** or a **panel of 10 doctors** who independently examine you, exchange notes, and arrive at a consensus diagnosis? The panel's diagnosis is almost always superior because:
> 1. **Different doctors have different specialties** (model *diversity*).
> 2. **Each doctor is reasonably competent but not perfect** (*weak learners*).
> 3. **The majority opinion filters out individual misjudgments** (*error cancellation*).
> 4. **Difficult edge cases are caught by at least some doctors** (*bias reduction*).

This is the **exact** philosophy behind ensemble learning. A single decision tree may overfit, a single neural network may get stuck in a local minimum, but **a forest of trees voting together** stabilizes and sharpens the prediction.

## 1.3 The Theoretical Foundation: Why Ensembles Work

The effectiveness of ensembles rests on **two mathematically provable conditions** (Krogh & Vedelsby, 1995 — *foundational for KTU derivations*):

> **Diversity Condition:** The base learners $\{h_t\}$ must make **uncorrelated errors** on the data.

> **Accuracy Condition:** Each base learner $h_t$ must have an error rate **strictly less than 0.5** (better than random guessing for binary classification).

If both conditions hold, the **ensemble error decays exponentially** as the number of base learners $T$ increases. The standard error bound for majority voting among $T$ independent classifiers (each with error $p < 0.5$) under Hoeffding's inequality is:

$$
P(H_{\text{ensemble}} \text{ is wrong}) \;=\; \sum_{k=\lceil T/2 \rceil}^{T} \binom{T}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{T-k}
$$

This is the **KTU high-yield result**: even if each weak learner is only slightly better than random, a large ensemble can drive the combined error arbitrarily close to zero.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Locked Down for KTU Viva/Exams

| Term | KTU-Style Definition | Symbol/Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Base Learner | A single model in the ensemble | $h_t$ |
| Weak Learner | A model slightly better than random | error $p < 0.5$ |
| Strong Learner | The aggregated final ensemble | $H(\mathbf{x})$ |
| Diversity | Decorrelation of errors between base learners | $\rho$ |
| Margin | Confidence of correct classification | $\text{margin}(x, y)$ |
| Out-of-Bag (OOB) | Samples not picked in a bootstrap sample | $\approx 36.8\%$ |
| Meta-Learner | The model that learns how to combine base models | $H_{\text{meta}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Ensemble Strategies

### Pillar 1: BAGGING (Bootstrap Aggregating) — *Variance Reduction*

**Proposed by:** Leo Breiman, 1996.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. Given a training set $D$ of size $n$.
2. Generate $T$ **bootstrap samples** $D_1, D_2, \dots, D_T$, each of size $n$, drawn **with replacement** from $D$.
3. Train a base learner $h_t$ independently on each $D_t$. (All base learners can be trained **in parallel**.)
4. Aggregate the predictions:
   - **Classification:** $H(\mathbf{x}) = \text{majority\_vote}(h_1(\mathbf{x}), \dots, h_T(\mathbf{x}))$
   - **Regression:** $H(\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} h_t(\mathbf{x})$

**The Math Behind the Bootstrap (must be memorized for KTU):**

The probability that a specific training instance is **not** selected in a single bootstrap sample of size $n$ from $n$ original points is:

$$
P(\text{instance NOT selected}) \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, this converges to:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{e} \;\approx\; 0.3679
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Result (the 63.2 / 36.8 Rule):** Each bootstrap sample contains approximately **63.2% unique instances** of the original training set, leaving **~36.8% as "Out-of-Bag" (OOB) samples** that can be used as a *free validation set* without needing a separate hold-out split.

**Canonical Example:** **Random Forest** = Bagging applied to decision trees, with the additional trick of **random feature selection** at each split (controlled by $m_{\text{try}}$).

**Why Bagging Reduces Variance:**

For an ensemble of $T$ independent base learners with variance $\sigma^2$, the variance of the averaged prediction is:

$$
\text{Var}(H_{\text{ensemble}}) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma^2}{T} \;+\; \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{T}\right) \cdot \rho \cdot \sigma^2
$$

where $\rho$ is the pairwise correlation between base learners. As $T \to \infty$ and $\rho \to 0$, variance collapses to zero.

---

### Pillar 2: BOOSTING — *Bias Reduction via Sequential Error Correction*

**Proposed by:** Robert Schapire (1989), formalized by Freund & Schapire (1997, AdaBoost).

**Operational Logic (Conceptual):**

Unlike bagging, boosting trains base learners **sequentially**. Each new model $h_t$ is trained to correct the errors of the *weighted* combination of all previous models $H_{t-1}$. Instances misclassified by previous models receive **higher weights** in subsequent rounds.

**The AdaBoost Algorithm (canonical KTU derivation):**

Given training set $D = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ where $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$.

**Step 1 — Initialize uniform weights:**

$$
w_i^{(1)} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

**Step 2 — For $t = 1, 2, \dots, T$:**

(a) Train weak learner $h_t$ using the current weights $w_i^{(t)}$.

(b) Compute the **weighted error rate** $\epsilon_t$:

$$
\epsilon_t \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \mathbb{1}(h_t(\mathbf{x}_i) \neq y_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^{(t)}}
$$

(c) Compute the **learner weight** $\alpha_t$ (confidence of $h_t$):

$$
\alpha_t \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

(d) Update the instance weights for the next round:

$$
w_i^{(t+1)} \;=\; \dfrac{w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t \cdot y_i \cdot h_t(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)}{Z_t}
$$

where $Z_t$ is a normalization constant ensuring $\sum_i w_i^{(t+1)} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Final strong classifier:**

$$
H(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(\mathbf{x})\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Intuition of the Weight Update Rule:** When $h_t(\mathbf{x}_i) = y_i$ (correct), the exponent $-\alpha_t y_i h_t = -\alpha_t$ is **negative**, so $w_i$ **decreases** by a factor of $e^{-\alpha_t}$. When $h_t(\mathbf{x}_i) \neq y_i$ (wrong), the exponent becomes $+\alpha_t$, so $w_i$ **increases** by a factor of $e^{+\alpha_t}$. **Harder examples get exponentially more attention in the next round.**

**Why Boosting Reduces Bias:**

Each successive $h_t$ is forced to focus on what the previous ensemble *got wrong*. The final additive model is essentially a **stagewise additive expansion** in a function space:

$$
F_T(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \alpha_t \, h_t(\mathbf{x})
$$

By the **stagewise greedy minimization** of the exponential loss $L(y, F) = e^{-yF}$ (in the case of AdaBoost), the ensemble converges to a Bayes-optimal classifier under ideal conditions.

**The Two King Variants (asked frequently in KTU):**

| Boosting Type | Loss Function | Gradient | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AdaBoost** | Exponential: $e^{-yF(x)}$ | Not explicit (re-weighting) | Classification |
| **Gradient Boosting** | Any differentiable $L(y, F)$ | Negative gradient of loss | Classification & Regression |
| **XGBoost** | Regularized objective | 2nd-order Taylor approx. | Kaggle-grade ML |

---

### Pillar 3: STACKING (Stacked Generalization) — *Meta-Learning*

**Proposed by:** David Wolpert, 1992.

**Operational Logic:**

Stacking uses a **two-layer architecture**:
- **Layer 0 (Base Learners):** $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_T$ — diverse, often heterogeneous models (e.g., SVM + Decision Tree + Logistic Regression).
- **Layer 1 (Meta-Learner):** $H_{\text{meta}}$ — a simple model (e.g., logistic regression) that takes the *out-of-fold predictions* of the base learners as its input features and learns the optimal way to combine them.

**Out-of-Fold (OOF) Prediction Generation:**

To prevent the meta-learner from overfitting to the base learners' in-sample predictions, the dataset is split into $K$ folds. For each fold, base learners are trained on the remaining $K-1$ folds and used to predict the held-out fold. These OOF predictions then form the **meta-features** for training $H_{\text{meta}}$.

**The Stacking Pipeline Equation:**

$$
\mathbf{z}_i \;=\; \big[ h_1(\mathbf{x}_i), \; h_2(\mathbf{x}_i), \; \dots, \; h_T(\mathbf{x}_i) \big]^{\top} \in \mathbb{R}^{T}
$$

$$
H_{\text{final}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; H_{\text{meta}}\!\big(\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x})\big) \;=\; H_{\text{meta}}\!\big( h_1(\mathbf{x}), \dots, h_T(\mathbf{x}) \big)
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these formulas appear in 90% of KTU ensemble questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OOB Fraction | $\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n} \to \tfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | $n$ = training set size |
| 2 | In-Bag Fraction | $1 - \tfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.632$ | Used in OOB error estimation |
| 3 | Bagging Variance | $\text{Var}(\bar{h}) = \rho \sigma^2 + \tfrac{1-\rho}{T}\sigma^2$ | $\rho$ = pairwise correlation |
| 4 | AdaBoost Learner Weight | $\alpha_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\tfrac{1-\epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)$ | $\epsilon_t$ = weighted error |
| 5 | AdaBoost Weight Update | $w_i^{(t+1)} = \tfrac{w_i^{(t)} e^{-\alpha_t y_i h_t(\mathbf{x}_i)}}{Z_t}$ | $Z_t$ = normalizer |
| 6 | AdaBoost Final Model | $H(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_t \alpha_t h_t(\mathbf{x})\right)$ | Weighted majority vote |
| 7 | AdaBoost Exponential Loss | $L(y, F) = e^{-yF(\mathbf{x})}$ | Surrogate for 0-1 loss |
| 8 | Gradient Boosting Update | $F_t(\mathbf{x}) = F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}) + \nu \gamma_t h_t(\mathbf{x})$ | $\nu$ = shrinkage (learning rate) |
| 9 | Pseudo-Residual (GB) | $r_{im} = -\!\left[\tfrac{\partial L(y_i, F(\mathbf{x}_i))}{\partial F(\mathbf{x}_i)}\right]_{F=F_{t-1}}$ | Negative gradient of loss |
| 10 | Bias-Variance Tradeoff | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{irred}}$ | $\sigma^2_{\text{irred}}$ = irreducible noise |
| 11 | Stacking Meta-Features | $\mathbf{z}_i = [h_1(\mathbf{x}_i), \dots, h_T(\mathbf{x}_i)]^{\top}$ | Input to meta-learner |
| 12 | Soft Voting Probability | $P(y=c \vert \mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} P_t(y=c \vert \mathbf{x})$ | Average predicted probs |

---

## 2.3 Bias-Variance Diagnostic: Which Ensemble to Use?

> [!TIP]
> **This table is a KTU favorite for "Compare and Contrast" 7-mark questions.**

| Property | Bagging | Boosting | Stacking |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Training Mode** | Parallel | Sequential | Parallel (Layer 0) + Meta |
| **Primary Effect** | Reduces **Variance** | Reduces **Bias** | Reduces both |
| **Sensitive to Overfitting** | Less (resilient) | More (can overfit) | Highly (needs OOF) |
| **Base Learner Type** | Strong & unstable (deep trees) | Weak (stumps, shallow) | Heterogeneous (any) |
| **Interpretability** | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **Computational Cost** | Medium (parallelizable) | High (sequential) | Very High |
| **Classic Example** | Random Forest | AdaBoost, XGBoost | Super Learner |
| **Handles Imbalance** | No (needs resampling) | Yes (re-weighting) | Depends on base |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Ensembles

- **Medical Diagnosis** — Combining CNNs and gradient-boosted trees for tumor detection.
- **Credit Risk Scoring** — Banks use XGBoost + Random Forest blends for loan default prediction.
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Sensor fusion across camera, LiDAR, and radar is an *ensemble of modalities*.
- **Kerala-Specific:** The Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) uses ensemble weather models (NCMRWF + ECMWF) for monsoon forecasting over Kerala — directly relevant to the state's flood/drought preparedness.
- **Cybersecurity** — Ensemble of anomaly detectors for zero-day attack detection.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bootstrap Sampling — The 63.2 / 36.8 Rule

> **Objective:** Show that each bootstrap sample uses ~63.2% unique instances of the original training set.

**Step 1 — Setup.**

We have a training set $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ of size $n$. We draw one sample $x_i$ (with replacement) from $D$ to form a bootstrap sample $D_b$ of size $n$.

The probability of selecting *any specific* instance $x_i$ in *one* draw is:

$$
P(\text{pick } x_i \text{ in one draw}) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of NOT picking $x_i$ in one draw.**

$$
P(\text{not pick } x_i) \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability of NOT picking $x_i$ in $n$ independent draws (the whole bootstrap).**

Since each draw is independent (with replacement):

$$
P(x_i \notin D_b) \;=\; \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$ (this is a standard first-year calculus result).**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^{n} \;=\; e^{-1} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{e} \;\approx\; 0.3679
$$

**Step 5 — Probability of picking $x_i$ at least once.**

$$
P(x_i \in D_b) \;=\; 1 - \dfrac{1}{e} \;\approx\; 0.6321
$$

> **Conclusion:** Approximately **63.21%** of original training instances appear in any bootstrap sample, and **36.79%** are left out — the *Out-of-Bag (OOB) samples*. The OOB samples can serve as a built-in validation set, computed *for free* without any data splitting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: AdaBoost's Weight Update — Why It Works

> **Objective:** Show that the AdaBoost weight update minimizes an upper bound on the *exponential training error*.

**Step 1 — Define the exponential loss of the ensemble after round $t$.**

$$
E_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \exp\!\big(-y_i F_t(\mathbf{x}_i)\big) \quad \text{where} \quad F_t \;=\; F_{t-1} + \alpha_t h_t
$$

We can factor the previous loss:

$$
E_t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \exp\!\big(-y_i F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}_i)\big) \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $w_i^{(t)} \propto \exp(-y_i F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}_i))$.**

So the loss becomes:

$$
E_t \;=\; Z_{t-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i^{(t)} \cdot \exp\!\big(-\alpha_t y_i h_t(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)
$$

where $Z_{t-1}$ is a constant from the previous round.

**Step 3 — Split the sum into correctly classified and misclassified indices.**

Let $C$ = indices where $h_t(\mathbf{x}_i) = y_i$ (correct), and $W$ = indices where $h_t(\mathbf{x}_i) \neq y_i$ (wrong). For $i \in C$: $y_i h_t = +1$, so $\exp(-\alpha_t)$. For $i \in W$: $y_i h_t = -1$, so $\exp(+\alpha_t)$.

$$
E_t \;=\; Z_{t-1} \!\left[ e^{-\alpha_t} \!\!\sum_{i \in C} w_i^{(t)} + e^{+\alpha_t} \!\!\sum_{i \in W} w_i^{(t)} \right]
$$

Let $W_t = \sum_{i \in W} w_i^{(t)}$ (the weighted error), so $\sum_{i \in C} w_i^{(t)} = 1 - W_t$.

$$
E_t \;=\; Z_{t-1} \!\left[ (1 - W_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + W_t e^{+\alpha_t} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Minimize $E_t$ with respect to $\alpha_t$ by taking the derivative and setting it to zero.**

$$
\dfrac{\partial E_t}{\partial \alpha_t} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; -(1 - W_t) e^{-\alpha_t} + W_t e^{+\alpha_t} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
W_t e^{\alpha_t} \;=\; (1 - W_t) e^{-\alpha_t} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e^{2\alpha_t} \;=\; \dfrac{1 - W_t}{W_t}
$$

$$
\alpha_t \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - W_t}{W_t}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - \epsilon_t}{\epsilon_t}\right)
$$

This **recovers the AdaBoost learner weight formula** from first principles. Substituting $\alpha_t$ back into the loss bound gives the bound on the final error:

$$
E_T \;\leq\; \prod_{t=1}^{T} 2\sqrt{\epsilon_t(1-\epsilon_t)}
$$

> **Key Insight:** If every weak learner has error $\epsilon_t < 0.5$, then $2\sqrt{\epsilon_t(1-\epsilon_t)} < 1$, so the product **shrinks exponentially with $T$**. This is the *theoretical guarantee* behind boosting's success.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Variance Reduction in Bagging

> **Objective:** Show that the variance of the bagged ensemble is *less than or equal to* the variance of a single base learner.

**Step 1 — Variance of the bagged prediction (average of $T$ models):**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{h}) \;=\; \text{Var}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^{T} h_t\right) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T^2} \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} h_t\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the variance of the sum using the covariance identity:**

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} h_t\right) \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \text{Var}(h_t) \;+\; 2 \!\!\sum_{t < s} \text{Cov}(h_t, h_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Assume all base learners have the same variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$ (a common simplifying assumption).**

$$
\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{t=1}^{T} h_t\right) \;=\; T \sigma^2 \;+\; T(T-1) \rho \sigma^2
$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
\text{Var}(\bar{h}) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T^2} \big[ T \sigma^2 + T(T-1) \rho \sigma^2 \big] \;=\; \rho \sigma^2 + \dfrac{1 - \rho}{T} \sigma^2
$$

> **Key Insights:**
> - As $T \to \infty$, $\text{Var}(\bar{h}) \to \rho \sigma^2$, **not zero**, because the models are *correlated* (they share the same data distribution).
> - To minimize this, we must minimize $\rho$ — i.e., **inject diversity** (e.g., random feature selection in Random Forest, subsampling in Stochastic Gradient Boosting).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Gradient Boosting as Functional Gradient Descent

> **Objective:** Show that gradient boosting is *gradient descent in function space*.

**Step 1 — The goal is to find the function $F^*(\mathbf{x})$ that minimizes the empirical risk:**

$$
F^* \;=\; \arg\min_{F} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i, F(\mathbf{x}_i))
$$

**Step 2 — Initialize with a constant (the best constant prediction):**

$$
F_0(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i, \gamma)
$$

**Step 3 — For each round $t = 1, \dots, T$, compute the negative gradient (the "pseudo-residual"):**

$$
r_{im} \;=\; -\!\left[\dfrac{\partial L\big(y_i, F(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)}{\partial F(\mathbf{x}_i)}\right]_{F = F_{t-1}} \quad \text{for } i = 1, \dots, n
$$

**Step 4 — Fit a base learner $h_t$ (e.g., a regression tree) to the pseudo-residuals $r_{im}$.**

**Step 5 — Compute the optimal step size $\gamma_t$:**

$$
\gamma_t \;=\; \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L\!\big(y_i, F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}_i) + \gamma \, h_t(\mathbf{x}_i)\big)
$$

**Step 6 — Update the ensemble with shrinkage $\nu$:**

$$
F_t(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}) \;+\; \nu \, \gamma_t \, h_t(\mathbf{x})
$$

> **Special Case (Squared Error Loss):** If $L(y, F) = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - F)^2$, then $\tfrac{\partial L}{\partial F} = F(\mathbf{x}_i) - y_i = -r_i$, so the pseudo-residual is simply $r_i = y_i - F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}_i)$ — the **ordinary residual**! This is why gradient boosting is sometimes called "stagewise fitting of residuals" in the regression case.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What — A Decision Framework

| Symptom in Your Model | Diagnosis | Prescribed Ensemble |
|---|---|---|
| High test error, low training error, unstable across runs | **Overfitting (High Variance)** | Bagging / Random Forest |
| High *both* training and test error | **Underfitting (High Bias)** | Boosting / AdaBoost |
| Highly heterogeneous data (text + numeric + images) | **Need hybrid representation** | Stacking |
| Imbalanced classes (e.g., fraud detection) | **Bias toward majority class** | Boosting with class weights / SMOTE+Bagging |
| Real-time / low-latency inference needed | **Ensembles too slow** | Single optimized model or distillation |
| Need feature importance explanations | **Black-box problem** | Random Forest (built-in importance) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bagging Architecture (Parallel Variance Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Original Training Set D of size n] --> BS1[Bootstrap Sample 1 - 63.2% unique]
    D --> BS2[Bootstrap Sample 2 - 63.2% unique]
    D --> BS3[Bootstrap Sample 3 - 63.2% unique]
    D --> BSN[Bootstrap Sample T - 63.2% unique]

    BS1 --> M1[Base Learner h1 e.g. Decision Tree]
    BS2 --> M2[Base Learner h2 e.g. Decision Tree]
    BS3 --> M3[Base Learner h3 e.g. Decision Tree]
    BSN --> MN[Base Learner hT e.g. Decision Tree]

    M1 --> AGG{Aggregator}
    M2 --> AGG
    M3 --> AGG
    MN --> AGG

    AGG -->|Majority Vote Classification| OUT1[Final Prediction H of x]
    AGG -->|Average Regression| OUT1

    OOB1[OOB Set 1 - 36.8%] -.->|Validation| M1
    OOB2[OOB Set 2 - 36.8%] -.->|Validation| M2
    OOB3[OOB Set 3 - 36.8%] -.->|Validation| M3
    OOBn[OOB Set T - 36.8%] -.->|Validation| MN
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each base learner is trained **independently and in parallel** on a different bootstrap sample. The OOB samples (dashed arrows) act as a free validation mechanism. All predictions funnel into a single **Aggregator** node that performs majority voting (classification) or averaging (regression).

---

## 4.2 Boosting Architecture (Sequential Bias Reduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Weighted Training Set - weights w1] --> M1[Weak Learner h1 e.g. Decision Stump]
    M1 --> E1[Compute Weighted Error epsilon 1]
    E1 --> A1[Compute Learner Weight alpha 1]
    A1 --> U1[Update Instance Weights w2 - misclassified get higher weight]
    U1 --> M2[Weak Learner h2 trained on w2]
    M2 --> E2[Compute Weighted Error epsilon 2]
    E2 --> A2[Compute Learner Weight alpha 2]
    A2 --> U2[Update Instance Weights w3]
    U2 --> M3[Weak Learner hT]
    M3 --> OUT[Final Strong Classifier H of x equals sign of sum alpha t times h t of x]

    A1 --> OUT
    A2 --> OUT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **strictly sequential** data flow — each $h_t$ depends on the output of $h_{t-1}$. There are **no parallel branches** (contrast with Bagging). The key feedback loop is the **weight update** that pushes hard examples to the next learner.

---

## 4.3 Stacking Architecture (Two-Layer Meta-Learning)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D[Training Data] --> SPLIT{K-Fold Split}
    SPLIT --> L1A[Fold 1: Train Base Learners]
    SPLIT --> L1B[Fold 2: Train Base Learners]
    SPLIT --> L1C[Fold K: Train Base Learners]

    L1A --> OOF1[OOF Predictions - Meta-Features z1]
    L1B --> OOF2[OOF Predictions - Meta-Features z2]
    L1C --> OOF3[OOF Predictions - Meta-Features zK]

    OOF1 --> META[Meta-Learner H meta e.g. Logistic Regression]
    OOF2 --> META
    OOF3 --> META

    META --> FINAL[Final Stacked Predictor H final of x]

    BL1[Base Learner 1: SVM] -.-> L1A
    BL2[Base Learner 2: Random Forest] -.-> L1A
    BL3[Base Learner 3: XGBoost] -.-> L1A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Stacking has a **two-tier hierarchy**. The base learners (SVM, RF, XGBoost — shown as dashed inputs) generate **Out-of-Fold (OOF) predictions** to prevent leakage. These OOF predictions form the **meta-feature matrix** $Z \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times T}$ that trains the meta-learner.

---

## 4.4 The Big-Picture Ensemble Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Need to Improve Model Performance] --> Q1{Is your model overfitting or underfitting}

    Q1 -->|Overfitting - High Variance| BAG[Use Bagging: Random Forest]
    Q1 -->|Underfitting - High Bias| BOO[Use Boosting: AdaBoost / XGBoost]
    Q1 -->|Both Bias and Variance| STK[Use Stacking with diverse base learners]
    Q1 -->|Heterogeneous data modalities| STK

    BAG --> T1[Tune: number of trees T, max features m try]
    BOO --> T2[Tune: learning rate nu, number of rounds T, tree depth]
    STK --> T3[Tune: base learner diversity, meta-learner regularization]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **clinical decision pathway** the KTU examiner expects a student to articulate in viva questions. Always diagnose the failure mode first, then prescribe the matching ensemble.
