# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define "Customer Churn" in the context of a Data Science project. Why is **Recall** preferred over **Accuracy** for evaluating a churn prediction model?

**Model Answer:**

> **Customer Churn** refers to the phenomenon where an existing customer of a company (telecom, banking, SaaS, etc.) discontinues their business relationship within a defined time window. In a Data Science project, churn prediction is formulated as a **binary classification** problem where the target variable $y_i = 1$ indicates that customer $i$ will churn and $y_i = 0$ indicates retention.
>
> **Recall is preferred over Accuracy** because churn datasets are inherently **imbalanced** (typically only 20–30% of customers churn). A trivial model that always predicts "no churn" achieves 80% accuracy but provides **zero business value**. The cost of a False Negative (missing a churner) is the **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**, often $500–$2,000 per lost customer, while the cost of a False Positive (offering a discount to a stayer) is only ~$20. Recall directly measures the model's ability to capture the *at-risk* customers, making it the operationally critical metric.
>
> $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
Explain the **KS Statistic** and its significance in **Credit Analysis** projects. What is the minimum acceptable KS value for an industry-grade credit scoring model?

**Model Answer:**

> The **Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) Statistic** measures the maximum vertical separation between the cumulative distribution functions (CDFs) of predicted scores for the *good* (non-default) and *bad* (default) classes in a credit model:
>
> $$\text{KS} = \max_{s} \left\vert F_{\text{good}}(s) - F_{\text{bad}}(s) \right\vert$$
>
> It quantifies the **discriminatory power** of the model — its ability to rank good customers above bad customers. A higher KS indicates the model can clearly separate the two classes at some threshold.
>
> The **minimum acceptable KS value for an industry-grade credit scoring model is 0.4** (the "KS > 0.4" rule is used by banks and fintech lenders globally). The classification is:
> - KS < 0.2 → Poor model
> - 0.2 ≤ KS < 0.4 → Acceptable
> - **0.4 ≤ KS < 0.5 → Good (industry standard)**
> - 0.5 ≤ KS < 0.6 → Excellent
> - KS ≥ 0.6 → Suspicious (possible overfitting or data leakage)
>
> In KTU 2024 NEP-aligned projects, the KS statistic also satisfies the **Basel II/III regulatory requirement** for model validation, making it both an academic and industry-relevant metric.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common reasons students lose marks on Module 4 project-related questions:
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — they are *not* interchangeable. Precision is about "what % of positive predictions are correct"; Recall is about "what % of actual positives did I capture."
> 2. **Forgetting class imbalance** — quoting accuracy on imbalanced data without mentioning SMOTE or class-weighting loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the Business Understanding phase** — KTU awards marks for the *why* of the project, not just the *how*.
> 4. **Writing code in theory exams** — KTU Board theory papers require algorithms, formulas, and explanations, *not* Python code blocks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the data split** — any model discussion without a 70:15:15 or 80:20 stratified split is incomplete.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Modified from Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Discuss the **CRISP-DM** framework and explain each of its six phases in the context of a **Churn Prediction** project for a telecom company. Briefly describe the dataset characteristics and key challenges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **F1-Score** and **ROC-AUC** metrics. Derive the F1-score formula from Precision and Recall, and show that a perfect classifier has an AUC of 1.0. Why is the AUC a more reliable metric than accuracy for imbalanced datasets? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CRISP-DM for Churn Prediction:**

The **Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining (CRISP-DM)** is the industry-standard methodology for executing a data science project. Applied to Churn Prediction:

**Phase 1 — Business Understanding:**
The telecom company aims to reduce customer attrition. The business objective is to identify high-risk customers *before* they churn, so retention teams can intervene with targeted offers. Success is measured by **reduction in churn rate** and **ROI of retention campaigns**.

**Phase 2 — Data Understanding:**
The **Telco Customer Churn dataset** (IBM, Kaggle) is used. It has 7,043 customer records with 21 features. Target distribution: 26.5% churn, 73.5% retained. Features include demographics, subscribed services, contract type, tenure, and charges. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) reveals that **month-to-month contracts**, **low tenure**, and **high monthly charges** correlate strongly with churn.

**Phase 3 — Data Preparation:**
- Handle missing values in `TotalCharges` (cast to float, drop rows with NaN).
- Encode categorical variables via **one-hot encoding**.
- Bin `tenure` into ordinal groups (0–12, 13–24, 25–48, 49+ months).
- Scale numerical features with `StandardScaler`.
- Split data 80:20 with stratification.

**Phase 4 — Modeling:**
- Baseline: Logistic Regression.
- Mid-tier: Random Forest.
- Final: XGBoost with hyperparameter tuning via `GridSearchCV`.

**Phase 5 — Evaluation:**
Use **Stratified 5-Fold Cross-Validation**. Report Recall, F1-Score, and AUC. XGBoost achieves the best performance (Recall ~0.52, AUC ~0.86).

**Phase 6 — Deployment:**
Deploy via a **Streamlit dashboard** where retention managers input customer attributes and receive churn risk scores.

**Key Challenges:**
1. **Class imbalance** (only 26.5% churners).
2. **Mixed data types** (numerical + categorical).
3. **Cost-sensitive errors** (FN cost >> FP cost).
4. **Concept drift** as customer behavior evolves over time.

**(b) F1-Score and ROC-AUC:**

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

The harmonic mean penalizes extreme values — a model with high Precision but low Recall (or vice versa) gets a low F1, encouraging a *balanced* classifier.

**The ROC-AUC** is the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, plotted as TPR vs FPR for varying thresholds:

$$
\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}) \, d(\text{FPR})
$$

**Proof that a perfect classifier has AUC = 1.0:**
A perfect classifier ranks all positive examples above all negative examples. For any threshold $t$ between the lowest positive score and the highest negative score, TPR = 1.0 and FPR = 0.0. The ROC curve traces from (0,0) up to (0,1) and then right to (1,1), enclosing the entire unit square. Hence:

$$
\text{AUC}_{\text{perfect}} = \int_0^1 1 \, d(\text{FPR}) = 1
$$

**AUC is more reliable than accuracy for imbalanced data** because:
1. AUC is **threshold-independent** — it measures ranking quality, not a single hard prediction.
2. AUC is invariant to class prior — a model evaluated on 27% churners gives the same AUC as one evaluated on 50% churners (provided ranking is unchanged).
3. Accuracy can be gamed by the majority class (predicting "no churn" always gives 73.5% accuracy but 0.5 AUC).
4. AUC answers the question: *"What is the probability that a randomly chosen churner receives a higher risk score than a randomly chosen non-churner?"* — directly useful for retention prioritization.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **Amazon Employee Access Challenge** as a classification problem. Explain the **Truncated SVD** technique used to handle the high-dimensional sparse data, and derive how the reduced feature matrix is constructed from the original sparse binary matrix $\mathbf{X}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Classical NLP** approach (TF-IDF + Logistic Regression) with the **Deep Learning** approach (BERT) for **Fake News Detection**. Include in your answer: a derivation of the TF-IDF formula, a discussion of the [CLS] token's role in BERT, and a justification for the choice of evaluation metrics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Employee Access Challenge & Truncated SVD:**

The **Amazon Employee Access Challenge** (Kaggle, 2013) is a binary classification problem: given a (employee, resource) pair, predict whether access should be granted. The dataset contains 32,769 training pairs and 91,221 test pairs, with only ~8.6% positive labels.

**High-Dimensional Sparsity Problem:**
After one-hot encoding the 7,516 unique roles and 7,528 unique resources, the feature matrix becomes:

$$
\mathbf{X} \in \{0, 1\}^{32769 \times 15044}
$$

with ~99.99% sparsity. With features nearly half the number of samples, classical tree-based models overfit and linear models need regularization.

**Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD):**
The full SVD of $\mathbf{X}$ is:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{V}^\top
$$

where $\mathbf{U} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (diagonal with singular values $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \ldots \geq 0$), and $\mathbf{V} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.

**Truncation** keeps the top $k$ singular values:

$$
\mathbf{X} \approx \mathbf{X}_k = \mathbf{U}_k \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k \mathbf{V}_k^\top
$$

**Reduced feature matrix** (passed to the classifier):

$$
\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{U}_k \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}
$$

The choice of $k$ is determined by the **explained variance ratio**:

$$
\text{EVR}(k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{\min(m,n)} \sigma_i^2} \geq 0.90
$$

For the Employee Access dataset, $k = 200$ typically achieves $\text{EVR} \geq 0.90$. The reduced features $\mathbf{Z}$ are then fed to Logistic Regression (the original Kaggle winner) or XGBoost, achieving AUC $\approx$ 0.88.

**(b) Fake News Detection — Classical vs Deep:**

**Classical Approach: TF-IDF + Logistic Regression**

After preprocessing (lowercasing, tokenization, stopword removal, stemming), each document is converted to a TF-IDF vector.

**Term Frequency** (normalized):

$$
\text{TF}(t, d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}
$$

**Inverse Document Frequency** (with smoothing):

$$
\text{IDF}(t) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \text{df}(t)} + 1
$$

**TF-IDF score:**

$$
\text{TF-IDF}(t, d) = \text{TF}(t, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(t)
$$

The document becomes a sparse vector $\mathbf{x}_d \in \mathbb{R}^V$ where $V$ is the vocabulary size. Logistic regression then learns:

$$
P(y = 1 \mid \mathbf{x}_d) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_d + b)
$$

**Strengths:** Fast, interpretable, low compute, works on small datasets.

**Deep Learning Approach: BERT**

BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) processes the entire article as a sequence of WordPiece tokens. The first token is always the special `[CLS]` token, whose **final hidden state** $\mathbf{h}_{[\text{CLS}]} \in \mathbb{R}^{768}$ serves as a **pooled, contextual representation of the entire document**:

$$
\mathbf{x}_d \xrightarrow{\text{BERT}} \mathbf{h}_{[\text{CLS}]} \xrightarrow{\text{Softmax}} \hat{y}_d
$$

**Why `[CLS]`?** During pre-training, BERT is trained on a Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) task that uses the `[CLS]` token's representation for sentence-level classification. Fine-tuning leverages this pre-learned aggregator for fake news detection.

**Strengths:** Captures context, semantics, sarcasm, and long-range dependencies. State-of-the-art accuracy (~98%).

**Weakness:** Requires substantial compute (GPU) and large training data for fine-tuning.

**Evaluation Metrics:**

Since fake news datasets are roughly balanced, **Accuracy** is acceptable. However, **F1-Score** is the academically preferred primary metric because:
- It balances Precision (avoiding false accusations of real news being fake) and Recall (catching all fake news).
- High Precision is critical for **media platforms** (false accusations damage reputation).
- High Recall is critical for **public safety** (missing fake news allows misinformation to spread).

For the KTU 2024 project report, **always report F1, Precision, Recall, Accuracy, and AUC** for completeness.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Project Topics**

### Universal Concepts
- [ ] All four projects are **binary classification** problems (Employee Access can be extended to multi-class).
- [ ] The **CRISP-DM** lifecycle is mandatory: Business → Data → Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment.
- [ ] **Confusion Matrix** → Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1.
- [ ] **AUC-ROC** is the threshold-independent, imbalance-robust metric.
- [ ] **Stratified K-Fold Cross-Validation** is required for imbalanced datasets.
- [ ] **Data leakage prevention**: fit preprocessing on training set only.
- [ ] **Binary Cross-Entropy** is the standard loss for all sigmoid-output classifiers.

### Churn Prediction
- [ ] Dataset: Telco (7,043 rows, 21 features, 26.5% churn rate).
- [ ] Critical preprocessing: cast `TotalCharges` to float, drop NaN.
- [ ] Tenure binning (0–12, 13–24, 25–48, 49+) improves tree models.
- [ ] **Recall is the primary metric** (FN cost = CLV).
- [ ] Best model: XGBoost with GridSearchCV.

### Credit Analysis
- [ ] Datasets: German Credit (1,000), Lending Club (2M+), HMEQ.
- [ ] **KS Statistic > 0.4** = industry-grade discrimination.
- [ ] **Gini = 2·AUC − 1** is the Basel II/III regulatory metric.
- [ ] Apply **SMOTE only on training set** (after split).
- [ ] The "Five C's": Character, Capacity, Capital, Collateral, Conditions.
- [ ] Acknowledge **fairness** and the 80% disparate impact rule (NEP 2020).

### Employee Access Challenge
- [ ] Dataset: Amazon, 32,769 train / 91,221 test, ~8.6% positive.
- [ ] High-dimensional sparse data: 15,044 features after one-hot.
- [ ] **Truncated SVD** reduces 15,044 → ~200 dimensions.
- [ ] **Logistic Regression with L2** is the surprise winner of the original Kaggle competition.
- [ ] Interaction features ($\mathbf{x}_{\text{role}} \otimes \mathbf{x}_{\text{resource}}$) boost performance.

### Fake News Detection
- [ ] Datasets: LIAR, FakeNewsNet, Kaggle Fake News, ISOT.
- [ ] **Classical:** TF-IDF (max 50,000 features) + LR/SVM/Naive Bayes.
- [ ] **Deep:** BERT fine-tuning with `[CLS]` token classification.
- [ ] Preprocessing: lowercase, tokenize, stopwords, stem/lemmatize.
- [ ] Stylometric features (caps ratio, punctuation, sentiment) boost classical models.
- [ ] **F1-Score** is the primary metric (Precision–Recall balance).

### Final KTU Viva Mantra
> *"Define the business problem, preprocess carefully, choose the right metric for the class imbalance, validate with stratified K-fold, deploy a working demo."*

This single sentence, expanded with the technical details above, will earn full marks in any Module 4 project-related question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: House Price Prediction Using Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 5
> - **Topic:** House Price Prediction Using Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **House Price Prediction (HPP)** is a canonical **supervised machine learning regression problem** in which a model is trained on historical real-estate data (features such as area, number of bedrooms, location, age of property) to learn a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that estimates the **continuous target variable** — the monetary price $y \in \mathbb{R}^+$ of a house.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary:

$$
\hat{y} = f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = f_\theta(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ is the **feature vector** of a house.
- $\theta$ represents the **learnable parameters** of the model.
- $\hat{y}$ is the **predicted (estimated) house price**.
- The objective is to **minimise** a loss function $\mathcal{L}(y, \hat{y})$ over the training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Data Science syllabus, House Price Prediction is treated as the **flagship illustrative case study** for the regression family of algorithms and the **end-to-end ML pipeline** (data ingestion → preprocessing → feature engineering → model training → evaluation → deployment).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Experienced Estate Agent"

Imagine you are an **old, experienced real-estate agent** who has seen **thousands** of houses being sold over **30 years**. When a new house arrives, you don't blindly guess — you **instinctively** look at:

- How **big** is it? (square footage)
- Where is it? (city, neighbourhood, proximity to schools)
- How many **bedrooms and bathrooms** does it have?
- How **old** is the building?
- Is there a **garage, garden, swimming pool**?

From memory of similar past sales, you quote a price. This is *exactly* what a regression model does — it is a **mathematical crystallisation of an expert's intuition**, learned statistically from data.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** The ML model is *not magic*. It is a function that **fits a curve (or a hyperplane, or a set of decision rules)** through historical price data so that future, unseen houses can be priced automatically.

---

### 1.3 Why House Price Prediction Matters (Engineering Utility)

| Domain | Application of HPP Models |
|---|---|
| **Banking & Finance** | Mortgage risk assessment, collateral valuation |
| **Real Estate Tech (PropTech)** | Automated listing platforms (Zillow Zestimate, NoBroker) |
| **Urban Planning** | Land-use optimisation, tax assessment |
| **Insurance** | Property insurance premium calculation |
| **Government** | Property tax brackets, affordable-housing policy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard benchmark dataset used globally is the **Boston Housing Dataset** (now deprecated for ethical reasons) and its modern replacement, the **Ames Housing Dataset** (De Cock, 2011) and the **Kaggle "House Prices: Advanced Regression Techniques"** competition dataset.

---

### 1.4 The Regression Family — Where HPP Lives

House price prediction belongs to the broader family of **regression algorithms**, which KTU 2024 classifies as:

$$
\text{Regression} = \begin{cases} \text{Linear Regression (Simple \& Multiple)} \\ \text{Polynomial Regression} \\ \text{Decision Tree Regression} \\ \text{Random Forest Regression (Ensemble)} \\ \text{Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM)} \\ \text{Support Vector Regression (SVR)} \\ \text{Regularised Models (Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet)} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 5 specifically emphasises the **comparative study** of at least **two** regression algorithms on the housing problem to highlight **bias-variance trade-off** and **model selection**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The End-to-End ML Pipeline for HPP

A robust House Price Prediction system follows the **CRISP-DM** (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining) pipeline. The operational flow is:

1. **Problem Definition** — Frame as supervised regression.
2. **Data Collection** — Historical transaction records, public datasets.
3. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** — Distribution plots, correlation heatmaps, outlier detection.
4. **Data Preprocessing** — Handling missing values, encoding categoricals, scaling.
5. **Feature Engineering** — Creating new informative features (e.g., price per sq. ft., age of house).
6. **Train / Validation / Test Split** — Typically **70 / 15 / 15** or **80 / 20**.
7. **Model Selection** — Try multiple regressors.
8. **Hyperparameter Tuning** — GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV.
9. **Model Evaluation** — MAE, MSE, RMSE, $R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$.
10. **Deployment** — Serialise model (pickle/joblib), expose via REST API (Flask/FastAPI).

---

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Core Models

#### 2.2.1 Simple Linear Regression (SLR)

Models a **straight-line** relationship between one feature and the price:

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i
$$

The **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** objective is to find $\beta_0, \beta_1$ that minimise the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i) \right)^2
$$

The closed-form solution (via the **normal equation**) is:

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

#### 2.2.2 Multiple Linear Regression (MLR)

Extension to $n$ features:

$$
y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 x_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_n x_{in} + \epsilon_i
$$

In **vectorised matrix form**:

$$
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}
$$

The OLS estimator in matrix form is:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ must be **invertible** (non-singular). This requires (a) no perfect multicollinearity and (b) $N > n$. Regularisation (Ridge/Lasso) handles ill-conditioned $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$.

#### 2.2.3 Decision Tree Regression

Partitions the feature space into **rectangular regions** and assigns a constant value (mean of training $y$ in that region) to each region. Predictions:

$$
\hat{y} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} c_m \cdot \mathbb{I}(\mathbf{x} \in R_m)
$$

where $R_m$ is the $m$-th leaf region and $c_m$ is the mean target value in that region. Splitting criterion uses **MSE reduction**:

$$
\Delta \text{MSE} = \text{MSE}_{\text{parent}} - \left( \frac{N_L}{N}\text{MSE}_L + \frac{N_R}{N}\text{MSE}_R \right)
$$

#### 2.2.4 Random Forest Regression (Ensemble)

Averages predictions from $B$ decorrelated decision trees (bagging):

$$
\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} f_b(\mathbf{x})
$$

Each tree $f_b$ is trained on a **bootstrap sample** using a **random subset of features** at each split. This reduces **variance** while keeping **bias** low.

#### 2.2.5 Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)

Sequentially adds weak learners to correct residual errors:

$$
F_{t}(\mathbf{x}) = F_{t-1}(\mathbf{x}) + \eta \cdot h_t(\mathbf{x})
$$

where $h_t$ is the new tree fit to the negative gradient (residuals) and $\eta$ is the **learning rate**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD** — it consolidates every formula you must memorise for KTU University Exam questions on House Price Prediction / Regression.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SLR Prediction | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Estimated price from single feature |
| 2 | MLR Prediction | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{x}^\top \boldsymbol{\beta}$ | Estimated price from multiple features |
| 3 | OLS Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ | Closed-form coefficient solution |
| 4 | Slope (SLR) | $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Best-fit slope |
| 5 | Intercept | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | y-axis intercept |
| 6 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Mean Absolute Error (robust to outliers) |
| 7 | MSE | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Mean Squared Error (penalises large errors) |
| 8 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Root MSE (same unit as $y$) |
| 9 | $R^2$ Score | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Coefficient of determination $\in [-\infty, 1]$ |
| 10 | Adjusted $R^2$ | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(N-1)}{N-n-1}$ | Penalises extra features |
| 11 | Ridge Penalty | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{Ridge}} = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum \beta_j^2$ | $L_2$ regularisation |
| 12 | Lasso Penalty | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{Lasso}} = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 + \lambda \sum \vert \beta_j \vert$ | $L_1$ regularisation (sparse) |
| 13 | Gradient Descent | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \alpha \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Iterative optimiser |
| 14 | Train Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{train}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Empirical risk |
| 15 | Bias-Variance | $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2_\varepsilon$ | Irreducible + reducible error |
| 16 | Sigmoid (for completeness) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Not used in HPP, but in logistic regression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units:** MAE, MSE, RMSE are all in the **same units as the target price** (squared for MSE, original for MAE/RMSE). $R^2$ is **dimensionless**. $\lambda$ in Ridge/Lasso is in **(error units)$^2$ per (coefficient units)$^2$**.

---

### 2.4 The Bias-Variance Trade-Off (Critical for KTU)

$$
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(\mathbf{x}))^2\right]}_{\text{Prediction Error}} = \underbrace{\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})] - f(\mathbf{x})\right)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(\mathbf{x})])^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_\varepsilon}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}
$$

| Model | Bias | Variance | HPP Performance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression | **High** | **Low** | Underfits if data is non-linear |
| Decision Tree (deep) | **Low** | **High** | Overfits noisy data |
| Random Forest | **Low** | **Low** (bagged) | **Best baseline** for HPP |
| XGBoost | **Low** | **Low** (boosted) | **SOTA** for HPP competitions |
| Ridge/Lasso | Slightly higher | **Much lower** | Best when multicollinearity exists |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"Why does Random Forest outperform a single Decision Tree on the housing dataset?"* — Answer: **Bagging reduces variance by averaging decorrelated trees**, while a single deep tree has high variance (sensitive to training data perturbations).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: OLS Slope and Intercept for Simple Linear Regression

We want to minimise the SSE:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)^2
$$

**Step 1 — Take partial derivatives:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Set derivatives to zero (first-order optimality conditions):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left( y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i \right) = 0
$$

**Step 3 — From the first equation, solve for $\beta_0$:**

$$
\sum y_i = N\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum x_i \;\Longrightarrow\; \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\beta_0$ into the second equation:**

$$
\sum x_i y_i - (\bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}) \sum x_i - \beta_1 \sum x_i^2 = 0
$$

Expanding and using $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum x_i$:

$$
\sum x_i y_i - N \bar{x}\bar{y} - \beta_1 \left( \sum x_i^2 - N\bar{x}^2 \right) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form solution:**

$$
\boxed{\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - \bar{x})^2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A housing dataset contains the following 5 observations of *area (in 100 sq. ft.)* and *price (in lakhs ₹)*:

| House | $x_i$ (Area) | $y_i$ (Price) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5 | 25 |
| 2 | 7 | 35 |
| 3 | 8 | 40 |
| 4 | 10 | 50 |
| 5 | 12 | 60 |

Fit a Simple Linear Regression model $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ using OLS. Predict the price of a house with area $= 9$ (i.e., **900 sq. ft.**).

#### Step 1 — Compute the means

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{5+7+8+10+12}{5} = \frac{42}{5} = 8.4
$$

$$
\bar{y} = \frac{25+35+40+50+60}{5} = \frac{210}{5} = 42.0
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute deviations and cross-products

| $i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-3.4$ | $-17$ | $57.8$ | $11.56$ |
| 2 | $-1.4$ | $-7$ | $9.8$ | $1.96$ |
| 3 | $-0.4$ | $-2$ | $0.8$ | $0.16$ |
| 4 | $1.6$ | $8$ | $12.8$ | $2.56$ |
| 5 | $3.6$ | $18$ | $64.8$ | $12.96$ |
| **Sum** | $0$ | $0$ | **$146.0$** | **$29.20$** |

#### Step 3 — Compute slope $\beta_1$

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum (x_i-\bar{x})^2} = \frac{146.0}{29.20} = 5.0
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute intercept $\beta_0$

$$
\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x} = 42.0 - 5.0 \times 8.4 = 42.0 - 42.0 = 0.0
$$

#### Step 5 — Final regression equation and prediction

$$
\hat{y} = 0.0 + 5.0 \cdot x
$$

For $x = 9$:

$$
\hat{y} = 5.0 \times 9 = 45 \text{ lakhs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **predicted price for a 900 sq. ft. house is ₹45 lakhs** under this simple linear model.

#### Step 6 — Verify with R²

Total Sum of Squares (TSS) and Residual Sum of Squares (RSS):

$$
\text{TSS} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 17^2 + 7^2 + 2^2 + 8^2 + 18^2 = 289 + 49 + 4 + 64 + 324 = 730
$$

$$
\text{RSS} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = (25-25)^2 + (35-35)^2 + (40-40)^2 + (50-50)^2 + (60-60)^2 = 0
$$

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{RSS}}{\text{TSS}} = 1 - \frac{0}{730} = 1.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $R^2 = 1.0$ is a **perfect fit** because the data was generated from a perfect linear rule: $\text{Price} = 5 \times \text{Area}$. In real-world HPP, $R^2 \in [0.7, 0.95]$ is considered excellent.

---

### 3.3 Multi-Feature Analytical Matrix (Feature Engineering Blueprint)

For the Ames Housing / Kaggle HPP dataset, the **canonical feature engineering** matrix is:

| Raw Feature | Transformation | Engineered Feature | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| `YearBuilt`, `YrSold` | Subtraction | `HouseAge = YrSold - YearBuilt` | Captures depreciation |
| `1stFlrSF`, `2ndFlrSF` | Addition | `TotalSF = 1stFlrSF + 2ndFlrSF` | Total living area |
| `FullBath`, `HalfBath` | Weighted sum | `TotalBath = FullBath + 0.5 \cdot HalfBath` | Industry standard |
| `OverallQual`, `OverallCond` | Product | `QualCond = OverallQual \times OverallCond` | Interaction term |
| `Neighborhood` | Target encoding | `NeighborhoodMeanPrice` | Replaces high-cardinality categorical |
| `LotArea`, `TotalSF` | Ratio | `LotAreaPerSF` | Land efficiency |
| All numeric | Standardisation | $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Required for regularised models |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison of Models (Bias-Variance Lens)

| Aspect | Linear Regression | Decision Tree | Random Forest | XGBoost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Functional Form** | Hyperplane | Step function | Averaged steps | Boosted steps |
| **Captures Non-Linearity** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| **Handles Outliers** | Poor (squares them) | Good | Good | Moderate |
| **Interpretability** | Very High | High | Medium | Low |
| **Training Time** | Fastest | Fast | Slow | Slowest |
| **Hyperparameters to Tune** | $\sim 2$ | $\sim 5$ | $\sim 8$ | $\sim 12$ |
| **Typical HPP $R^2$** | $0.75$–$0.85$ | $0.70$–$0.85$ | $0.88$–$0.93$ | $0.90$–$0.95$ |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | **Very High** | Low | Medium (if not regularised) |
| **Extrapolation** | **Good** | Poor | Poor | Poor |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark questions, always **compare at least two models** using a table like the above, justify your choice with **bias-variance** language, and back it up with the **$R^2$/RMSE values** you computed.
