# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Define a Recommendation System and list its three main types.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Recommendation System is an information filtering system that predicts a user's preference for an item and suggests relevant items. **(1 mark)**
> The three main types are:
> 1. **Content-Based Filtering (CBF):** Recommends items similar in attribute space to those the user has previously liked. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Collaborative Filtering (CF):** Recommends items based on the preferences of similar users (user-based) or similar items (item-based). **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Hybrid Systems:** Combine multiple techniques to overcome individual limitations such as cold-start. *(Bonus point often expected.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. Distinguish between Explicit and Implicit feedback in retail recommendation systems with one example each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Explicit Feedback:** Direct input from the user, such as star ratings (1–5), likes/dislikes, written reviews. Example: A customer giving 4 stars to a shirt on Flipkart. **(1.5 marks)**
> - **Implicit Feedback:** Indirectly inferred from user behaviour — clicks, purchase, add-to-cart, dwell time, search history. Example: A customer browsing a product for 3 minutes without rating it. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> > - Students *very often* confuse **Jaccard** with **Cosine** similarity. Remember: Jaccard is for *binary* sets, Cosine is for *rated* vectors.
> > - In Market Basket Analysis, students forget that **Lift > 1** is the *only* indicator of a *useful* rule. Confidence alone is not enough.
> > - For Matrix Factorization, students write the prediction formula but *skip the SGD derivation*. KTU examiners allocate **3–4 marks** specifically to the derivation.
> > - In numericals, **forgetting to mean-center** in Pearson similarity is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> > - Always state the **range** (e.g., $[-1, 1]$) and **business interpretation** of every metric you compute.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **User-Based Collaborative Filtering** algorithm in detail. Derive the rating prediction formula. Discuss its advantages and limitations for an e-commerce retail platform like Flipkart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following user–item rating matrix for a small retail store (1–5 scale, 0 means not rated). Predict the rating of **User A for Item 4** using **User-Based CF with Pearson similarity and $k=2$ neighbours**.

| | Item 1 | Item 2 | Item 3 | Item 4 | Item 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 5 | 3 | 4 | 0 | 2 |
| **B** | 4 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 1 |
| **C** | 1 | 5 | 2 | 4 | 5 |
| **D** | 3 | 4 | 0 | 5 | 4 |

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Algorithm Steps:** **(3 marks)**
1. Build the User–Item matrix $R$ from explicit or implicit feedback.
2. For the target user $u$, compute similarity $\text{sim}(u, v)$ with every other user $v$ using Cosine or Pearson.
3. Select the top-$k$ most similar users as the neighbourhood $N_k(u)$.
4. Filter $N_k(u)$ to retain only those who have rated the target item $i$.
5. Predict the rating using the weighted-average formula (derived below).
6. Rank all unrated items by their predicted ratings and return the top-$K$.

**Derivation of Prediction Formula:** **(2 marks)**

For a neighbourhood $N_k(u)$, the prediction must account for *user bias* (some users rate generously, some harshly). The baseline-corrected formula is:

$$
\hat{r}_{ui} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \text{sim}(u,v) \cdot (r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_{v \in N_k(u)} \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}
$$

**Advantages (for retail e-commerce):** **(1 mark)**
- No need for item content/description — works purely on transaction logs.
- Discovers *non-obvious* cross-sell patterns (e.g., "diapers → beer" discovery).
- Improves as more users interact (network effect).

**Limitations:** **(1 mark)**
- **Cold-start problem** for new users and new products.
- **Sparsity** — retail matrices are >99% sparse; similarity is unstable.
- **Scalability** — user-user similarity is $O(m^2 n)$; becomes infeasible for millions of customers.
- **Popularity bias** — popular items get recommended disproportionately.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute mean rating per user:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\bar{r}_A = \frac{5+3+4+2}{4} = 3.5, \quad \bar{r}_B = \frac{4+2+5+3+1}{5} = 3.0
$$

$$
\bar{r}_C = \frac{1+5+2+4+5}{5} = 3.4, \quad \bar{r}_D = \frac{3+4+5+4}{4} = 4.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Pearson similarity (A vs B, A vs C, A vs D):**

**A vs B** (co-rated items: 1, 2, 3, 5 — Item 4 is missing for A): **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{num} = (5-3.5)(4-3) + (3-3.5)(2-3) + (4-3.5)(5-3) + (2-3.5)(1-3)
$$
$$
= (1.5)(1) + (-0.5)(-1) + (0.5)(2) + (-1.5)(-2) = 1.5 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 3.0 = 6.0
$$

$$
\text{den} = \sqrt{1.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 1.5^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2 + 2^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{10} \approx 7.071
$$

$$
\text{sim}(A, B) = \frac{6.0}{7.071} \approx 0.848
$$

**A vs C** (co-rated items: 1, 2, 3, 5): **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{num} = (1.5)(-2.4) + (-0.5)(1.6) + (0.5)(-1.4) + (-1.5)(1.6) = -3.6 - 0.8 - 0.7 - 2.4 = -7.5
$$

$$
\text{den} = \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{2.4^2 + 1.6^2 + 1.4^2 + 1.6^2} = \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{12.0} = \sqrt{60} \approx 7.746
$$

$$
\text{sim}(A, C) = \frac{-7.5}{7.746} \approx -0.968
$$

**A vs D** (co-rated items: 1, 2, 5 — Items 3 and 4 are missing for D): **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{num} = (1.5)(-1) + (-0.5)(0) + (-1.5)(0) = -1.5
$$

$$
\text{den} = \sqrt{1.5^2 + 0.5^2 + 1.5^2} \cdot \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{5} \cdot 1 \approx 2.236
$$

$$
\text{sim}(A, D) = \frac{-1.5}{2.236} \approx -0.671
$$

**Step 3 — Select top-2 neighbours by similarity (only those who have rated Item 4):** **(1 mark)**

Who has rated Item 4? **B (rating 3), C (rating 4), D (rating 5)**.

Their similarities with A: $\text{sim}(A, B) = 0.848$, $\text{sim}(A, C) = -0.968$, $\text{sim}(A, D) = -0.671$.

Top-2 (by absolute or signed similarity) = **B and C** (or B and D, depending on convention; we take B and C as the two most informative — B is strongly positive, C is strongly negative).

**Step 4 — Apply UBCF formula:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\hat{r}_{A,4} = \bar{r}_A + \frac{\text{sim}(A,B)(r_{B,4}-\bar{r}_B) + \text{sim}(A,C)(r_{C,4}-\bar{r}_C)}{\vert \text{sim}(A,B) \vert + \vert \text{sim}(A,C) \vert}
$$

$$
= 3.5 + \frac{0.848 \times (3-3.0) + (-0.968) \times (4-3.4)}{0.848 + 0.968}
$$

$$
= 3.5 + \frac{0.848 \times 0 + (-0.968) \times 0.6}{1.816} = 3.5 + \frac{-0.581}{1.816} = 3.5 - 0.32
$$

$$
\boxed{\hat{r}_{A,4} \approx 3.18}
$$

**Step 5 — Business Interpretation:** *(Bonus 0.5 mark in 7-mark questions, but always attempt it)*

Since the predicted rating is moderate (~3.18/5), the system will rank Item 4 *moderately* in User A's recommendation list. The retail store may *not* place Item 4 prominently in A's homepage, but may include it in a "You might also like" carousel.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question Paper]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Matrix Factorization-based recommendation system** for a retail store. Derive the SGD update rule for $p_{uf}$ and $q_{if}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retail store has 5,000 transactions. The rule *Mobile Cover ⇒ Earphones* has Support = 0.04, Confidence = 0.65. If Support(Earphones) = 0.15, compute the **Lift** of the rule and explain whether the rule should be kept. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram Description:** **(2 marks)**

The MF system has the following components:
1. **Input layer:** Sparse User–Item matrix $R$ ($m \times n$) with missing entries.
2. **Embedding layer:** Two low-rank matrices — User factors $P$ ($m \times f$) and Item factors $Q$ ($n \times f$).
3. **Multiplication layer:** $\hat{R} = P \cdot Q^T$, a dense predicted matrix.
4. **Loss layer:** Regularized squared error $\mathcal{L} = \sum (r_{ui} - \hat{r}_{ui})^2 + \lambda(\|p_u\|^2 + \|q_i\|^2)$.
5. **Optimizer:** SGD/Adam updates $P$ and $Q$ to minimize $\mathcal{L}$.
6. **Output layer:** Top-K items by $\hat{r}_{ui}$.

**Derivation of SGD Update Rule:** **(5 marks)**

**Step 1 — Loss function:**

$$
\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(u,i) \in \mathcal{K}} (r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u)^2 + \lambda (\|p_u\|^2 + \|q_i\|^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Error term:**

$$
e_{ui} = r_{ui} - q_i^T p_u
$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $p_{uf}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_{uf}} = -2 e_{ui} q_{if} + 2 \lambda p_{uf}
$$

**Step 4 — Update rule with learning rate $\eta$:**

$$
p_{uf}^{\text{new}} = p_{uf}^{\text{old}} - \eta \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_{uf}} = p_{uf}^{\text{old}} + \eta(e_{ui} q_{if} - \lambda p_{uf})
$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for $q_{if}$:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_{if}} = -2 e_{ui} p_{uf} + 2 \lambda q_{if} \implies q_{if}^{\text{new}} = q_{if}^{\text{old}} + \eta(e_{ui} p_{uf} - \lambda q_{if})
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Recall the Lift formula:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:** **(1 mark)**

$$
\text{Lift}(\text{Mobile Cover} \Rightarrow \text{Earphones}) = \frac{0.65}{0.15} \approx 4.33
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:** **(2 marks)**

Since $\text{Lift} = 4.33 \gg 1$, customers who buy a Mobile Cover are **4.33 times more likely** to buy Earphones compared to a random customer. The rule is **strongly positive** and should definitely be retained.

**Step 4 — Business action:** **(1 mark)**

- Place Mobile Covers and Earphones on **adjacent shelves** in the retail store.
- Create a **combo offer** (e.g., "Buy Mobile Cover + Earphones at 10% off").
- Use the rule in the **online "Frequently Bought Together"** widget.
- Train store staff to **cross-sell** Earphones whenever a customer buys a Mobile Cover.

**Step 5 — Compare with random baseline:** *(Bonus)*

If there were no association (independent purchases), Lift would be exactly 1.0. A lift of 4.33 represents **333% more cross-purchase than random chance** — a *high-impact* business rule.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read the Night Before the Exam)**

- ☐ A **Recommendation System** predicts user preferences and suggests items from a large catalog.
- ☐ The **User–Item Matrix $R$** is *sparse* (>99% empty in retail); this sparsity is the core challenge.
- ☐ **Three paradigms:** Content-Based, Collaborative Filtering (user-based + item-based), and Hybrid.
- ☐ **Cosine similarity** uses raw ratings; **Pearson** uses mean-centered ratings; **Jaccard** is for binary sets only.
- ☐ **UBCF formula:** $\hat{r}_{ui} = \bar{r}_u + \frac{\sum_v \text{sim}(u,v)(r_{vi} - \bar{r}_v)}{\sum_v \vert \text{sim}(u,v) \vert}$
- ☐ **IBCF formula:** $\hat{r}_{ui} = \frac{\sum_j \text{sim}(i,j) r_{uj}}{\sum_j \vert \text{sim}(i,j) \vert}$
- ☐ **Matrix Factorization** decomposes $R \approx P Q^T$ where $P$ and $Q$ are low-rank latent factor matrices.
- ☐ **SGD update:** $p_{uf} \leftarrow p_{uf} + \eta(e_{ui} q_{if} - \lambda p_{uf})$ and similarly for $q_{if}$.
- ☐ **Market Basket Analysis** uses Support, Confidence, and **Lift > 1** as the rule-acceptance criterion.
- ☐ **RMSE** penalizes large errors quadratically; **MAE** penalizes them linearly. RMSE is sensitive to outliers.
- ☐ **Precision@K** measures recommendation accuracy; **Recall@K** measures coverage of relevant items; **F1@K** is the harmonic mean.
- ☐ The **Cold-Start Problem** affects new users, new items, and new systems — addressed by CBF or hybrid models.
- ☐ In KTU derivations, *always* show the **partial derivative step** and *always* state the **business interpretation**.
- ☐ For numericals, *always* mention the **range** of similarity (e.g., $\text{sim} \in [-1, 1]$).
- ☐ A "good" recommendation system improves **AOV (Average Order Value)**, **CTR (Click-Through Rate)**, and **conversion rate** — tie your answer to these business KPIs whenever possible.
- ☐ Avoid the pitfalls: don't apply Jaccard to 1–5 ratings, don't skip the SGD derivation, don't forget to mean-center in Pearson, don't interpret Confidence without Lift.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBITT404 - DATA SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 12
> - **Topic:** Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sentiment Analysis (SA)**, also referred to as **Opinion Mining (OM)**, is a sub-field of **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** and **Computational Linguistics** that systematically identifies, extracts, quantifies, and studies affective states and subjective information expressed in textual data.

In the context of **e-commerce and product reviews**, Sentiment Analysis is formally defined as a **supervised/semi-supervised text classification task** that maps a free-form product review document $d \in \mathcal{D}$ to a sentiment label $y \in \mathcal{Y}$, where the label space typically includes:
- $\mathcal{Y} = \{\text{Positive}, \text{Neutral}, \text{Negative}\}$ (3-class polarity)
- $\mathcal{Y} = \{\text{Very Negative}, \text{Negative}, \text{Neutral}, \text{Positive}, \text{Very Positive}\}$ (5-class fine-grained)

Mathematically, the classifier learns a function $f: \mathcal{D} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that the empirical risk is minimized:

$$\hat{f} = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f(d_i), y_i)$$

where $\mathcal{L}$ is a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy), and $N$ is the number of training samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Sentiment Analysis of Product Reviews is officially classified as a **Text Classification problem** under **Supervised Learning** in the KTU PBITT404 syllabus. Students must explicitly mention "feature extraction" and "classification model" in their 14-mark answers to score full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine you own a small restaurant that receives **10,000 customer reviews** per month on Google, Zomato, and Swiggy. Reading each review manually is humanly impossible. Now, imagine a **digital assistant that acts as an emotional translator** — it reads every review, understands whether the customer is *happy*, *angry*, or *indifferent*, and tags it accordingly. This digital assistant is, in essence, a **Sentiment Analysis system**.

Think of it like a **3-stage filter funnel**:

1. **Top Funnel — Text Cleaning**: The assistant strips away noise (HTML tags, emojis, slang) so it can focus only on meaningful words.
2. **Middle Funnel — Understanding Words**: It looks up each word in a "mood dictionary" (e.g., *awesome = +1*, *terrible = -1*) or learns patterns from thousands of past labeled reviews.
3. **Bottom Funnel — Decision**: It tallies the mood scores and outputs a final verdict: *Positive / Neutral / Negative*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Polarity** | Orientation of sentiment (positive/negative/neutral) | **Core concept** |
| **Subjectivity** | Degree to which text expresses opinion vs. fact | Often asked in Part A |
| **Aspect-Based SA (ABSA)** | Sentiment tied to a specific product feature (e.g., "battery life") | **High-yield 14-mark topic** |
| **F1-Score** | Harmonic mean of precision and recall | **Must know formula** |
| **Lexicon** | Pre-built dictionary mapping words to sentiment scores | **VADER, TextBlob** are KTU-favorite examples |
| **Corpus** | Structured collection of text used for training/testing | Term used in pipeline diagrams |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant / Threshold Convention:** Standard polarity thresholds in lexicon-based systems are **+0.05 (positive)** and **-0.05 (negative)**, with the **range [-1.0, +1.0]** for VADER compound scores. The neutral band is **[-0.05, +0.05]**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The end-to-end architecture of a product review sentiment analysis system consists of **six sequential stages**:

#### **Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Corpus Construction**
- Reviews are scraped from e-commerce platforms (Amazon, Flipkart) using APIs or web crawlers.
- The output is an unlabeled corpus $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_N\}$ that must be **manually annotated** (or weakly labeled using star ratings as proxy labels: 1-2 stars → Negative, 3 → Neutral, 4-5 → Positive).

#### **Stage 2 — Text Pre-processing**
Raw reviews contain substantial noise. The pre-processing sub-steps are:
- **Tokenization**: Splitting sentences into words. $d_i \rightarrow [w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n]$
- **Lowercasing**: Normalize case (e.g., "GREAT" → "great").
- **Stopword Removal**: Eliminate common words (the, is, a) that carry little sentiment.
- **Stemming & Lemmatization**: Reduce to root form ("running" → "run").
- **Punctuation & Emoji Handling**: Convert emojis to sentiment tokens (e.g., 😊 → POS_EMOJI).
- **Negation Tagging**: Critical step — "not good" must flip polarity.

#### **Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (Vectorization)**
Convert text into numerical feature vectors. The three dominant techniques are:

**(a) Bag-of-Words (BoW) / Count Vectorizer**
Each document is represented as a vector of word counts over a vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = [\text{count}(w_1, d_i), \text{count}(w_2, d_i), \dots, \text{count}(w_{\vert V \vert}, d_i)]$$

**(b) TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)**
Weighs words by their importance across the corpus:

$$\text{TF}(w, d) = \frac{f_{w,d}}{\sum_{w' \in d} f_{w',d}}$$

$$\text{IDF}(w, D) = \log \frac{N}{1 + \vert \{d \in D : w \in d\} \vert}$$

$$\text{TF-IDF}(w, d, D) = \text{TF}(w, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(w, D)$$

**(c) Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT)**
Dense vector representations capturing semantic similarity:

$$\mathbf{e}_w \in \mathbb{R}^{d}, \quad d \in \{50, 100, 300, 768\}$$

#### **Stage 4 — Model Training (Classification)**
Three families of classifiers dominate:

- **Lexicon-Based**: Rule-driven (VADER, TextBlob). No training required.
- **Classical Machine Learning**: Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest.
- **Deep Learning**: LSTM, GRU, BERT, RoBERTa (Transformer-based).

#### **Stage 5 — Prediction & Inference**
The trained model assigns polarity to unseen reviews:

$$\hat{y}_i = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} P(y \mid \mathbf{x}_i; \theta)$$

#### **Stage 6 — Evaluation**
Performance quantified using confusion matrix-derived metrics.

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the "How"

| Step | Reason for Inclusion | KTU Exam Tip |
|------|----------------------|---------------|
| Pre-processing | Reduces dimensionality by **40-60%**; improves accuracy | Mention "noise reduction" in 7-mark answers |
| Negation Handling | Flips polarity of ~15-20% of reviews | Often a 3-mark direct question |
| TF-IDF vs BoW | TF-IDF down-weights common words, boosts rare discriminative terms | Frequently asked as comparison question |
| Word Embeddings | Capture semantic similarity (king − man + woman ≈ queen) | High-yield for 14-mark answers |
| F1-Score Choice | Balances precision and recall — essential for imbalanced review datasets | Always quote the formula |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Units / Range** |
|------|------------------------|--------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Polarity Labeling (Threshold) | $\hat{y} = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{if } s \geq \tau^+ \\ \;0 & \text{if } \tau^- < s < \tau^+ \\ -1 & \text{if } s \leq \tau^- \end{cases}$ | $\tau^+ = +0.05, \tau^- = -0.05$ |
| 2 | Term Frequency | $\text{TF}(w, d) = \dfrac{f_{w,d}}{\sum_{w' \in d} f_{w',d}}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Inverse Document Frequency | $\text{IDF}(w, D) = \log \dfrac{N}{1 + n_w}$ | $[0, \log N]$ |
| 4 | TF-IDF | $\text{TF-IDF}(w, d, D) = \text{TF}(w, d) \cdot \text{IDF}(w, D)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 5 | Cosine Similarity (between docs) | $\cos(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x}_i \cdot \mathbf{x}_j}{\Vert \mathbf{x}_i \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert \mathbf{x}_j \Vert_2}$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| 6 | Naive Bayes Posterior | $P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(y) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid y)$ | Probability |
| 7 | Sigmoid (Logistic Regression) | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | $(0, 1)$ |
| 8 | Softmax (Multi-class) | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_j = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$ | $\sum_j \sigma_j = 1$ |
| 9 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \log(\hat{y}_i)$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 10 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 11 | Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 12 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 13 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 14 | Macro-Averaged F1 | $F_1^{\text{macro}} = \dfrac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} F_1^{(k)}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| 15 | VADER Compound Score | $s_c = \dfrac{\sum_i s_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i s_i^2 + \alpha}}, \quad \alpha = 15$ | $[-1, 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are awarded for **correct formula notation** in 14-mark derivations. Always state the **range/units** of each metric — students who omit ranges typically lose 1 mark per formula.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **E-commerce Recommendation Engines**: Amazon uses ABSA to identify "battery complaints" vs. "screen praise" and recommends relevant products.
- **Brand Monitoring Dashboards**: Companies like Brandwatch and Hootsuite aggregate sentiment scores from millions of reviews for executive decision-making.
- **Voice-of-Customer (VoC) Analytics**: Automotive and electronics firms mine reviews to drive product design improvements.
- **Financial Market Prediction**: Sentiment scores from product launches influence stock-price movement models (Hedge funds).
- **Healthcare Review Mining**: Patient reviews analyzed to detect hospital/service quality issues.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Raw Review Text to Predicted Sentiment (Worked Numerical Example)

Consider a **mini-corpus** of three product reviews, where the task is to classify sentiment using **Naive Bayes** with **Bag-of-Words** features.

**Step 1 — Construct the labeled dataset**

Let the corpus be:

| Doc ID | Review Text | Label (y) |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| $d_1$ | "great product love it" | Positive (1) |
| $d_2$ | "terrible product hate it" | Negative (0) |
| $d_3$ | "great service but hate packaging" | Negative (0) |

**Step 2 — Build the vocabulary**

After tokenization and lowercasing, the vocabulary is:
$$V = \{\text{great}, \text{product}, \text{love}, \text{terrible}, \text{hate}, \text{it}, \text{service}, \text{but}, \text{packaging}\}$$
Therefore $\vert V \vert = 9$.

**Step 3 — Compute class prior probabilities**

$$P(y = 1) = \frac{\text{count}(\text{Positive})}{N} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$P(y = 0) = \frac{\text{count}(\text{Negative})}{N} = \frac{2}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute likelihoods with Laplace smoothing**

Laplace smoothing parameter $\alpha = 1$. For each word $w$ and class $y$:

$$P(w \mid y) = \frac{\text{count}(w, y) + \alpha}{\text{count}(y) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

For the **Positive class** (only $d_1$, total words = 4):

$$P(\text{great} \mid y=1) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 9} = \frac{2}{13}$$

$$P(\text{love} \mid y=1) = \frac{1 + 1}{4 + 9} = \frac{2}{13}$$

For the **Negative class** ($d_2, d_3$, total words = 8):

$$P(\text{great} \mid y=0) = \frac{0 + 1}{8 + 9} = \frac{1}{17}$$

$$P(\text{hate} \mid y=0) = \frac{2 + 1}{8 + 9} = \frac{3}{17}$$

**Step 5 — Classify a new review**

Test review: $d_{\text{test}} =$ *"great but terrible packaging"*

Using Naive Bayes (ignoring stopwords for clarity), we compare the two posteriors:

$$P(y=1 \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto P(y=1) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid y=1) \cdot P(\text{terrible} \mid y=1) \cdot P(\text{packaging} \mid y=1)$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{13} \cdot \frac{1}{13} \cdot \frac{1}{13} = \frac{2}{3 \cdot 13^3} = \frac{2}{6591}$$

$$P(y=0 \mid d_{\text{test}}) \propto P(y=0) \cdot P(\text{great} \mid y=0) \cdot P(\text{terrible} \mid y=0) \cdot P(\text{packaging} \mid y=0)$$

$$= \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{17} \cdot \frac{2}{17} \cdot \frac{1}{17} = \frac{4}{3 \cdot 17^3} = \frac{4}{14739}$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule**

Since $P(y=0 \mid d_{\text{test}}) > P(y=1 \mid d_{\text{test}})$, the model predicts **Negative** — which intuitively makes sense given the presence of "terrible packaging".

### 3.2 Derivation: TF-IDF Numerical Walkthrough

**Given:**
- Corpus $D$ of $N = 100$ reviews.
- Word $w =$ "excellent".
- Document $d$ contains $f_{w,d} = 3$ occurrences of "excellent".
- Total words in $d$: $\sum_{w'} f_{w',d} = 50$.
- Number of documents containing "excellent": $n_w = 8$.

**Step 1 — Compute Term Frequency**

$$\text{TF}(\text{excellent}, d) = \frac{3}{50} = 0.06$$

**Step 2 — Compute Inverse Document Frequency**

$$\text{IDF}(\text{excellent}, D) = \log\left(\frac{100}{1 + 8}\right) = \log\left(\frac{100}{9}\right) \approx 2.4079$$

**Step 3 — Compute TF-IDF**

$$\text{TF-IDF}(\text{excellent}, d, D) = 0.06 \times 2.4079 \approx 0.1445$$

This relatively high TF-IDF score indicates "excellent" is a **discriminative sentiment-bearing word** in document $d$ — a strong positive signal for the classifier.

### 3.3 Derivation: Confusion Matrix → F1-Score (Worked Example)

Suppose a sentiment classifier is evaluated on **200 test reviews** and produces the following confusion matrix:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|--|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Actual Positive** | 90 (TP) | 10 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 20 (FP) | 80 (TN) |

**Step 1 — Compute Precision**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{90}{90 + 20} = \frac{90}{110} = 0.8182$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{90}{90 + 10} = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9000$$

**Step 3 — Compute F1-Score**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8182 \cdot 0.9000}{0.8182 + 0.9000} = \frac{1.4728}{1.7182} \approx 0.8572$$

**Step 4 — Compute Accuracy**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{90 + 80}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.8500$$

**Step 5 — Interpret**

The model has **85% accuracy** and **85.72% F1-Score**, indicating balanced performance across both classes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Lexicon vs. ML vs. Deep Learning

| **Dimension** | **Lexicon-Based (VADER)** | **Classical ML (NB/SVM)** | **Deep Learning (BERT)** |
|---------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------|
| Training Required | No | Yes (labeled data) | Yes (large labeled data) |
| Feature Engineering | Hand-crafted scores | TF-IDF / BoW | Learned embeddings |
| Handles Sarcasm | Poor | Moderate | Good |
| Handles Context | No | Limited | Excellent |
| Computational Cost | Very Low | Low | High (GPU needed) |
| Accuracy (typical) | 60-75% | 75-85% | 88-95% |
| Interpretability | High | Medium | Low (black-box) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High (Part A) | High (Part B) | Medium (theoretical) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Sentiment Analysis. List any two real-world applications of sentiment analysis of product reviews. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Sentiment Analysis, also known as Opinion Mining, is a Natural Language Processing (NLP) technique used to computationally identify, extract, and classify subjective opinions expressed in text as positive, negative, or neutral polarity.

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **E-commerce Product Recommendation** — Amazon and Flipkart analyze customer reviews to identify product strengths/weaknesses and personalize recommendations.
2. **Brand Reputation Management** — Companies monitor sentiment trends in product reviews to detect PR crises and measure customer satisfaction in real time.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for applications = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of negation handling in sentiment analysis with an example. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Negation handling refers to the process of detecting negation words (e.g., "not", "no", "never", "n't") and reversing the polarity of sentiment-bearing words that follow them.

**Example:**
- Sentence: *"The product is not good."*
- Without negation handling: "good" → Positive (incorrectly).
- With negation handling: "not" flips "good" to Negative (correctly).

This step is critical because approximately **15-20% of product reviews** contain negation that can completely invert sentiment. Failure to handle negation results in systematic misclassification and a low F1-Score.

*(1 mark concept, 1 mark example, 1 mark importance = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **end-to-end pipeline of sentiment analysis for product reviews**, listing all major stages with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of pre-processing in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Bag-of-Words and TF-IDF** feature extraction techniques. Derive the TF-IDF formula and show a numerical example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (7 marks):**

The pipeline consists of **6 sequential stages**:

1. **Data Collection**: Product reviews are scraped from e-commerce sites via APIs (Amazon Product Advertising API) or web crawlers. Datasets like Amazon Reviews, Yelp, or IMDb are commonly used benchmarks.

2. **Text Pre-processing** (most critical stage): Raw text contains HTML tags, emojis, abbreviations, and noise. Sub-steps include:
   - Tokenization (sentence/word level)
   - Lowercasing
   - Stopword removal
   - Stemming (Porter Stemmer) and Lemmatization (WordNet Lemmatizer)
   - Punctuation removal
   - **Negation tagging**

3. **Feature Extraction**: Convert text to numerical vectors using BoW, TF-IDF, or embeddings.

4. **Model Training**: Apply classifiers — Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression, or BERT.

5. **Prediction**: Output sentiment label for new reviews.

6. **Evaluation**: Measure performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix.

*Role of Pre-processing (2 marks extra):* Pre-processing reduces vocabulary size by **40-60%**, removes noise, handles class imbalance, and improves classification accuracy by 5-15%. Without it, classifiers overfit to irrelevant features.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 to be drawn = 1 mark)*

---

**(b) Bag-of-Words & TF-IDF (7 marks):**

**Bag-of-Words (BoW):**
Represents each document as a vector of word counts over a fixed vocabulary $V$ of size $\vert V \vert$:

$$\mathbf{x}_i = [x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \dots, x_{i \vert V \vert}]^T, \quad x_{ij} = \text{count}(w_j, d_i)$$

**Limitation:** Treats all words equally — common words like "the", "is" get high counts but carry no sentiment.

**TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency):**
Addresses BoW's limitation by **down-weighting common words** and **up-weighting rare, discriminative words**.

The TF-IDF weight is derived as follows. Term frequency is normalized by document length:

$$\text{TF}(w, d) = \frac{f_{w,d}}{\sum_{w' \in d} f_{w',d}}$$

The inverse document frequency measures how rare a word is across the corpus:

$$\text{IDF}(w, D) = \log \frac{N}{1 + n_w}$$

The two are multiplied to give the final TF-IDF weight:

$$\text{TF-IDF}(w, d, D) = \text{TF}(w, d) \cdot \log \frac{N}{1 + n_w}$$

**Numerical Example (2 marks):**
For $N = 100$ reviews, $f_{w,d} = 3$, total words in $d = 50$, $n_w = 8$:

$$\text{TF-IDF}(w, d, D) = \frac{3}{50} \times \log \frac{100}{9} = 0.06 \times 2.4079 \approx 0.1445$$

This indicates word $w$ is discriminative in $d$.

*(1 mark BoW definition, 2 marks TF-IDF derivation, 2 marks IDF derivation, 2 marks numerical example = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain **Naive Bayes classification** for sentiment analysis. Show the step-by-step computation of Naive Bayes posterior for a small review dataset containing 3 reviews. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy**. Given a confusion matrix, compute all four metrics. State why F1-score is preferred over accuracy for imbalanced review datasets. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Naive Bayes for Sentiment Analysis (7 marks):**

Naive Bayes is a **probabilistic classifier** based on **Bayes' Theorem** with a "naive" assumption of conditional independence between features (words).

The posterior probability is:

$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(y) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant, the classifier predicts:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y} P(y) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid y)$$

To avoid zero probabilities for unseen words, **Laplace smoothing** is applied:

$$P(x_i \mid y) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, y) + \alpha}{\text{count}(y) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

**Worked Example** *(from Section 3.1 above)*:
- Three reviews, vocabulary of 9 words.
- $P(\text{Positive}) = 1/3$, $P(\text{Negative}) = 2/3$.
- For test review *"great but terrible packaging"*: $P(\text{Negative} \mid d_{\text{test}}) \approx 4/14739$ vs. $P(\text{Positive} \mid d_{\text{test}}) \approx 2/6591$.
- After normalization, model predicts **Negative**.

*(2 marks theory, 3 marks worked example, 2 marks smoothing = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Evaluation Metrics (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Precision** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — Of all predicted positives, how many are actually positive.
- **Recall** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — Of all actual positives, how many were correctly identified.
- **F1-Score** $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ — Harmonic mean balancing both.
- **Accuracy** $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ — Overall proportion of correct predictions.

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
Using the confusion matrix from Section 3.3 ($TP=90, FN=10, FP=20, TN=80, N=200$):

$$P = \frac{90}{110} = 0.8182, \quad R = \frac{90}{100} = 0.9000$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.8182 \times 0.9000}{0.8182 + 0.9000} = 0.8572$$

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{90 + 80}{200} = 0.8500$$

**Why F1 > Accuracy for Imbalanced Data (2 marks):**
Product review datasets are typically imbalanced (e.g., 70% Positive, 10% Negative, 20% Neutral). A naive classifier predicting "Positive" always would achieve 70% accuracy but **0% F1-Score on the Negative class**. F1-Score penalizes such bias and provides a more honest evaluation, especially for minority sentiment classes that are often the most actionable (e.g., negative reviews indicating churn risk).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall** — Precision is about *predicted* positives; recall is about *actual* positives. Mixing them up costs 2-3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Omitting Laplace Smoothing in Naive Bayes** — If a word never appears in training, $P(w \mid y) = 0$, nullifying the entire product. Always mention $\alpha = 1$.
> 3. **Forgetting to Normalize in Naive Bayes** — Students compare raw products $P(y) \prod P(x_i \mid y)$ without normalization. KTU expects the argmax form, not absolute values.
> 4. **Writing "F-score" instead of "F1-Score"** — The "1" denotes equal weight to precision and recall. KTU deducts marks for sloppy notation.
> 5. **Confusing TF and IDF** — TF is local (per document), IDF is global (per corpus). Writing them in reverse order is a common error.
> 6. **Not drawing the block diagram** — In 14-mark questions on pipeline, the **diagram carries 2-3 marks**. Skipping it guarantees a deduction even if the text is perfect.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Sentiment Analysis = Text Classification = NLP + ML**. Always frame answers using these three keywords.
- **Three polarity classes**: Positive, Negative, Neutral. (Fine-grained = 5 classes with very-positive / very-negative.)
- **Pipeline is 6 stages**: Collection → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Training → Prediction → Evaluation.
- **Pre-processing steps** (memorize order): Tokenization → Lowercasing → Stopword Removal → Stemming/Lemmatization → Negation Tagging.
- **TF-IDF formula**: $\text{TF}(w,d) \times \log(N / (1 + n_w))$ — note the **+1** for Laplace-style smoothing in denominator.
- **VADER compound score range**: $[-1, +1]$; threshold $\pm 0.05$ for classification.
- **Naive Bayes formula**: $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y) \prod_i P(x_i \mid y)$ — always apply Laplace smoothing.
- **Evaluation metric priorities**: For balanced data → Accuracy; for imbalanced data → **F1-Score (macro-averaged)**.
- **Lexicon vs ML vs DL** — Lexicon (VADER) is fast but context-blind; ML (SVM/NB) is balanced; DL (BERT) is most accurate but data-hungry.
- **Aspect-Based SA (ABSA)** is the **highest-weight 14-mark topic** — link sentiment to specific product features (battery, screen, delivery).
- **Confusion matrix structure** (2×2): Top-left = TP, Top-right = FN, Bottom-left = FP, Bottom-right = TN.
- **Real-world giants using SA**: Amazon, Flipkart, Google Shopping, Zomato, Swiggy — quoting these adds **contextual credibility** to answers.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in pipeline questions — earns 2-3 marks and saves the answer from being marked as "incomplete".
- **Mention precision AND recall** whenever you cite F1-Score — examiners verify you understand it's the harmonic mean of both.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark SA question, structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Step-by-Step → Formula → Numerical Example → Limitations**. This six-part structure maps directly to KTU's marking rubric and typically scores **12-14 out of 14**.
